title: Gosford Hospital leading Australia with new lifesaving stroke prevention surgery from US
content:
Linda Penny is among the first in Australia to undergo the new procedure at Gosford Hospital. ( ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince )
A hospital on the Central Coast of NSW is leading the rollout of a new stroke-prevention procedure in Australia.
The technique was developed in the United States a decade ago, where 150,000 cases have since been performed.
Doctors believe the procedure will help expand what they can do for at-risk patients.
A new surgical procedure to reduce a patient's risk of having a stroke has become available in Australia, 10 years after it caught the attention of one of the nation's pioneering vascular surgeons.
Bernie Bourke from the NSW Central Coast has been a leading vascular specialist for more than 40 years.
He first expressed an interest in the technique when it was developed in the United States a decade ago.
Since then, 150,000 cases have been performed in the US.
But it was not until recently that he was approached by his American counterparts about introducing it in Australia.
Gosford-based vascular surgeons Victor and Bernie Bourke are father and son. ( ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince )
Dr Bourke and his son, Victor, were the first Australian surgeons to be accredited for the Transcarotid Artery Revascularisation (TCAR) technique, which directly targets the removal of plaque in the main artery in the neck, known as the carotid artery.
Lindy Penny from the Central Coast is one of 30 Australian patients who have recently undergone the surgery, after being told she was at extreme risk of stroke late last year.
Linda Penny says she is "really grateful" her blockage was found and that something could be done about it. ( ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince )
Ms Penny, a 71-year-old grandmother of six, was receiving medical treatment for an unrelated condition when follow-up tests showed she had a near-total blockage of her carotid artery.
The diagnosis came "as a shock" even though she was an ex-smoker and taking cholesterol medication.
summary: Linda Penny is among the first in Australia to undergo the new procedure at Gosford Hospital. ( ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince )
author: Mary Louise Vince

title: Fresh warning about using superannuation on health treatments after 100 complaints to regulator
content:
AHPRA has received almost 100 complaints about health practitioners helping patients access their superannuation early. ( Unsplash: Jafar Ahmed )
Almost 100 complaints have been made about health practitioners involved in patients accessing their superannuation early to fund treatments.
Early withdrawal of super is only permitted to treat pain, mental illness or a life-threatening illness or injury.
The Australian Tax Office says patients should be wary of practitioners using social media to advertise early access to super.
A GP found to have engaged in professional misconduct while helping a patient access almost $20,000 of her superannuation for cosmetic liposuction is one of two health practitioners referred to tribunals by regulators amid dozens of related complaints.
Since 2019, the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA) received 95 complaints about health practitioners involved in patients accessing their superannuation early.
The WA State Administrative Tribunal recently found a Perth doctor had engaged in professional misconduct by inappropriately helping a patient access $18,500 of her superannuation to fund liposuction.
The Medical Board of Australia accused the doctor of providing "false and misleading information" by claiming the patient was accessing superannuation early for life-threatening obesity, despite not documenting her weight or body mass index.
Tribunal documents state the doctor had completed the early release of superannuation declaration form in 2018, after the patient told him she wanted to have a tummy tuck or liposuction, to deal with "fatty tissue around the midriff."
His notes did not document any clinical assessment of the patient, according to the tribunal documents, including any measurements, weight, or her body mass index, or make any reference to the patient being obese or having a life-threatening condition.
ATO Deputy Commissioner Ben Kelly said accessing super early had long-term financial risks. ( Supplied: Australian Tax Office )
The State Administrative Tribunal ruled the doctor's registration be suspended for three months, and that he pay $5,000 as a fixed amount of the Medical Board of Australia's costs.
New figures released by the Australian Tax Office (ATO) show that between January 2019 and December 2025, AHPRA received 95 complaints related to compassionate release of superannuation, with most notifications related to treatment outcomes or payment disputes.
After investigations by AHPRA, two doctors were referred to tribunals for alleged professional misconduct, one dentist had conditions imposed on their registration and one doctor received a formal caution.
ATO Deputy Commissioner Ben Kelly said some practitioners were using "predatory practices" to inappropriately access super early.
summary: AHPRA has received almost 100 complaints about health practitioners helping patients access their superannuation early. ( Unsplash: Jafar Ahmed )
author: Stephanie Dalzell

title: Therapeutic Goods Administration issues safety alert amid explosion of illegal peptide use
content:
The TGA is concerned about the rise of unapproved peptide products. ( ABC News: Keana Naughton )
The medicines regulator has issued a health alert about the risks of using unregulated peptides amid a surge in the use of potentially unsafe injectables.
The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) says it has received reports of "severe allergic reactions" to unapproved peptide products.
Doctors say the TGA needs to go further in cracking down on the unregulated products.
Australia's drug regulator has issued a safety alert about unregulated peptides, warning the latest wellness craze poses a "significant" public health risk.
The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) said it was "concerned" about an increase in the importation and supply of unapproved peptides, which were a "safety risk".
"These products have not been evaluated for safety, quality or effectiveness by the TGA," the safety alert said.
There has been an explosion in popularity of unregulated injectable peptides in Australia, with social media users creating content about their supposed benefits and how to buy and use them.
Have you come across injectable peptides yet? They're everywhere on social media right now and we want to know why people are using them, and what their experience has been like.
The TGA said it had been alerted to "severe allergic reactions" that had led to hospitalisation, severe inflammation, full-body intense itching, palpitations, pain, insomnia, blurred vision and musculoskeletal injuries.
"The TGA is actively monitoring this issue from both safety and law enforcement perspectives," it said.
"This information includes how they are manufactured, whether they are sterile, how they work in the body, what side effects they may cause and what substances these products actually contain."
The regulator said examples of unapproved peptide products included those containing BPC157, GHKCu, TB500, retatrutide and CJC1295, often supplied in injectable form.
The TGA previously said it was illegal to intentionally import, export, manufacture or supply therapeutic goods that were not regulated, including those peptides.
Peptides are short chains of amino acids which, when joined together, make up the proteins in our bodies.
summary: The TGA is concerned about the rise of unapproved peptide products. ( ABC News: Keana Naughton )
author: Caitlyn Gribbin

title: Do you taste words or hear colours? Here's the neuroscience behind synaesthesia
content:
All of us naturally combine information from different senses. ( Unsplash: Prateek Gautam )
If you have, you may be among the 1 to 4 per cent of people who have a fascinating trait known as synaesthesia.
Synaesthesia is a neurological phenomenon where the activation of one sense, such as hearing, triggers the activation of another usually unrelated sense, such as sight. This means people with synaesthesia often experience additional sensations compared to the rest of us.
We've devoted a lot of time to understanding this rare phenomenon . While there's much more to unpack, what we do know shows we don't all perceive the world in the same way.
People with synaesthesia are known as synaesthetes. Research suggests synaesthesia may be more common among women, although this could reflect sampling biases , and may be influenced by genetics.
There are many different types of synaesthesia. Some people have auditory-visual synaesthesia , meaning they see colours when they hear sounds. Others see colours when they read, hear or think about letters or numbers. This is known as grapheme-colour synaesthesia . Another example is mirror-touch synaesthesia , where a person feels sensations on their own body when they see another person being touched.
Helen and her daughter argue about which colour goes with each day of the week. Here's how the genetic condition synaesthesia has them seeing the world differently.
All of us naturally combine information from different senses. For instance, when you watch someone speak, your brain blends what you see and hear to understand them better. In synaesthesia these links are a bit different — a sound might, for example, trigger a visual experience — but may still depend on the same mechanisms.
People with synaesthesia don't have any control over how their senses collide. Instead, these are spontaneous, vivid experiences that usually stay the same over time. For example, today a person with grapheme-colour synaesthesia may perceive the letter "A" as being red. And they'll most likely see it as being the same shade even years later.
It's worth noting synaesthesia is not an illness or disorder . And it doesn't cause harm or impairment, although some people may find their synaesthesia overwhelming at times. For example, if they feel pain every time they see someone else in pain, going to the movies can be quite disturbing. However, on the whole it does not seem to interfere with daily life. In fact, many people don't realise they have synaesthesia because it's simply how they perceive the world.
We don't yet know exactly what causes synaesthesia. But scientists have come up with two main theories.
1. Synaesthetes have more connections in their brain
According to this view, known as the cross-activation theory , people with synaesthesia have more connections between different parts of their brain. This could happen because their brain hasn't gotten rid of unused connections between brain cells. This process, known as synaptic pruning , helps the brain work more efficiently and is part of normal development.
Under this theory, a person with grapheme-colour synaesthesia, for example, would have the region that recognises letters directly linked to the part that processes colour. So when they see a letter, they perceive it with a colour.
2. Synaesthetes have slightly different activity in their brain
summary: All of us naturally combine information from different senses. ( Unsplash: Prateek Gautam )
author: Sophie Smit and Anina Rich

title: Sunshine Coast ukulele club's 600 members may be strumming their way to better health
content:
It's mid-week and mid-morning at this Queensland RSL club — but it might as well be a Saturday night.
About 100 people, many with ukuleles in hand, are singing as one.
"I love the connection and I love that people come and watch us and they get a little piece of joy for the week — we all do," Vanessa Schmidt said.
Vanessa Schmidt says the club brings joy to her week. ( ABC Sunshine Coast: Jessica Ross )
Ms Schmidt is part of the Parkbench Ukulele Players.
As the name suggests, the group formed at a park 12 years ago, in Tewantin on the Sunshine Coast.
"Just a few of my friends wanted to learn the ukulele and we were all struggling a bit so we decided to go to the local park and meet once a week," founding member Lin Nolan said.
Group members now meet weekly at their local RSL club after outgrowing previous venues. ( ABC Sunshine Coast: Jessica Ross )
Ms Nolan's husband Terry said since then, the group had changed venues and had technology upgrades.
"We started off on paper with the songs and it got to the stage where I'd need a ream of paper every week," Mr Nolan said.
"I said, 'No, that's not going to happen, go and buy yourselves an iPad or a tablet and I'll give you the music in a format you can put onto it.'
Many of the members now use tablets to follow along with the songs. ( ABC Sunshine Coast: Jessica Ross )
The club plays a wide range of genres, including rockabilly, country, bluegrass and modern rock.
Most of the songs have only three or four chords, making them easier to play.
"It's a great sense of community and fun — we're very relaxed about the playing," Ms Nolan said.
summary: It's mid-week and mid-morning at this Queensland RSL club — but it might as well be a Saturday night.
author: Jessica Ross

title: Is sitting with your legs crossed actually bad for you?
content:
If crossing your legs feels comfortable, there is little reason to treat it as dangerous. ( Pexels: Kaboompics.com )
Most of us were told off at some point for how we sat.
"Don't cross your legs, you'll ruin your knees."
It belongs to that familiar pile of health warnings many of us heard as kids, alongside cracking your knuckles or sitting too close to the television. But is crossing your legs actually bad for you?
For most people, the answer is probably no.
There is little evidence sitting with your legs crossed damages your back, wears out your hips or knees, or causes varicose veins.
If anything, the bigger issue for many of us is staying in one position for too long, getting stiff or sore, and then assuming discomfort must mean something is wrong with the body.
Part of it probably grew out of older ideas about posture.
For a long time, sitting "properly" was treated as a sign of discipline, self-control and good character. Once that kind of thinking takes hold, it is easy for social rules to start sounding like medical facts.
It is also easy (and common) to confuse discomfort with damage. Sitting cross-legged for a while can make you feel stiff, compressed, or ready to move.
The healthiest sitting position is often the one you do not hold for the next hour. ( Pexels: Resume Genius )
But it is usually a cue to change position, not a sign that you are quietly harming your body.
That fits with modern thinking on posture and pain, which has moved away from the idea there is one "perfect" posture.
Crossing your legs is often lumped into the category of "bad posture", as if it twists the spine into trouble.
But research on posture and back pain has not found one ideal sitting position that protects everyone, or one everyday sitting posture that reliably causes harm.
summary: If crossing your legs feels comfortable, there is little reason to treat it as dangerous. ( Pexels: Kaboompics.com )
author: Joshua Pate, Bruno Tirotti Saragiotto and Mark Overton

title: What to do if you get a fishhook injury and how to avoid one
content:
Fishhook injuries can range from minor to severe. So what are the best ways to manage them? ( ABC Far North: Sophie Johnson )
Many people living in north Queensland practise the weekend ritual of getting out on the water and dropping a line.
But what happens when things don't quite go to plan and a hook ends up catching the angler instead of the fish?
Since 2020, 438 people have presented to the Townsville University Hospital with fishhook injuries.
Most who frequent the seas have tales to tell, including Townsville fishing charter company director and skipper Adam Finlay.
"We've had a couple of clients do some stupid things with hooks here and there and had one with a lure piercing in the ear. That was quite interesting," he said.
"We've had a deckhand have a dolphin fish on with a hook go in his leg, so that was a different one."
Adam Finlay runs a fishing charter business in Townsville. ( ABC Far North: Sophie Johnson )
One of Mr Finlay's deckhands, Deacon Martin, has also seen a few fishhook injuries.
"I've seen a couple of mates get lures in the top of their heads … purely just not looking where they're casting," he said.
Also on the charter is Bryce Chaney. He said the most memorable fishing injury he had seen was when his brother got his lure stuck in some mangroves.
"With a bit of frustration, he flicked a bit too hard [to release the lure] and it came back and got him straight between the toes and the web of his feet," Mr Chaney said.
Bryce Chaney, Adam Finlay, and Deacon Martin all have fishhook injury stories to share. ( ABC Far North: Sophie Johnson )
Mr Finlay said he always had a fully equipped first-aid kit on board.
"You just deal with it as you go — pressurise it, make sure the bleeding stops and do what you can do, first-aid wise," he said.
summary: Fishhook injuries can range from minor to severe. So what are the best ways to manage them? ( ABC Far North: Sophie Johnson )
author: Sophie Johnson

title: Safety review links herb in popular cold and flu supplements with life-threatening anaphylaxis
content:
Carmen Wells suffered liver damage after taking a cold and flu supplement containing Andrographis paniculata. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
The Therapeutic Goods Administration has linked a herb in cold and flu supplements with the risk of life-threatening anaphylaxis.
The TGA has concluded stronger warning labels for products containing Andrographis paniculata would be inadequate.
The regulator will consult stakeholders on plans to remove the herb from a list classifying it as a "low-risk" ingredients.
A herb found in popular cold and flu supplements in Australia has officially been linked with life-threatening anaphylaxis.
The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) has completed a safety review of Andrographis paniculata , which is used in approximately 84 listed medicines that are marketed to relieve cold and flu symptoms and boost immunity.
Listed medicines are regulated as low-risk, can only contain ingredients that are classified as low-risk and can be bought from retail outlets including pharmacies.
The TGA's safety review has found that people taking supplements containing Andrographis have experienced "unpredictable" reactions to the herb, regardless of whether they have taken the product for the first time or have used it previously.
Since 2005, the regulator has received 1,368 reports of adverse reactions related to medicines containing Andrographis , including 287 incidents of anaphylaxis .
"The sustained high reporting pattern since 2019 indicates that Andrographis is associated with risk of life-threatening anaphylaxis which is inconsistent with the low-risk regulatory framework for listed medicines," the TGA's report stated.
"Current risk mitigation strategies, including stronger label warnings, have not reduced the risk of anaphylaxis from Andrographis for it to be suitable for use in listed medicines."
Andrographis is a herb commonly used in Indian and Chinese medicine and is known as the 'King of bitters'.
Connie Katelaris says products containing Andrographis paniculata should be removed from the shelves of pharmacies. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
Connie Katelaris AM is a professor of immunology and allergy at Western Sydney University and has been warning the TGA that the risk posed by the herb outweighs any perceived benefits.
"We've got data from many countries showing adverse responses to this product.
summary: Carmen Wells suffered liver damage after taking a cold and flu supplement containing Andrographis paniculata. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
author: Joanna Woodburn

title: Neuroscientist slams AFL's 'potentially dangerous' headgear move
content:
How effective is headgear in reducing concussions? ( Supplied: GameGear )
The AFL has backed new headgear despite no scientific evidence to yet prove that helmets can reduce concussions.
Leading sports neuroscientists are concerned the helmet is being sold to the public.
The move comes as former players take on the code in a class action lawsuit and the news that many current elite footballers will no longer be insured for head trauma from May 1.
Two world-leading sports neuroscientists have slammed the AFL's move to back a new helmet being tested to determine its capacity to reduce "the incidence of concussion", despite numerous previous studies dismissing headgear's ability to do so.
The helmet, known as GameGear, is already being sold online even though a study into its efficacy is yet to be completed.
GameGear is funding an $800,000 two-year Monash University-led trial on 600 athletes across Australian Rules football and rugby league, to see if and how it reduces concussion risk
GameGear is funding research on concussion. ( GameGear Headgear )
Selling the headgear before the study outcome is known has been branded as "potentially dangerous" by leading sports neuroscientist Alan Pearce.
"I am also concerned that parents are buying this headgear thinking it will protect their kid from concussion," Professor Pearce, of Swinburne University, told the ABC.
"Athletes can also get a false sense of safety from wearing headgear. I think it is potentially dangerous.
"The scientific study on the efficacy of this helmet has not been undertaken, which suggests to me bad science.
Alan Pearce predominantly studies sports-related concussion. ( ABC News: Billy Draper )
Another sports neuroscientist, Chris Nowinski, pointed out the number one sport for the degenerative brain disease chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), which is linked to repetitive hits, is American football, whose athletes wear helmets.
GameGear currently retails for $215. Its official website states the headgear will "Protect, Perform and Prevail".
summary: How effective is headgear in reducing concussions? ( Supplied: GameGear )
author: Jessica Halloran

title: NSW pharmacists will soon be able to prescribe the contraceptive pill
content:
Pharmacists will soon be able to prescribe contraceptive medication to new patients. ( Pixabay: GabiSanda: CC0 )
Specially trained pharmacists in NSW will soon be able to prescribe the contraceptive pill in a move designed to ease pressure on GPs.
It marks an expansion of an existing scheme that allows eligible pharmacists to reissue scripts for women aged 18 to 35.
The change will come into effect on June 1, with the service to be free for the first 5,000 patients who use the scheme.
People will soon be able to get the contraceptive pill without a trip to the doctor, as part of an expansion of pharmacist prescription powers in New South Wales.
Some pharmacists can already dispense refills without a new script if the patient has been on the same pill for at least two years and is aged between 18 and 35.
The state government now plans to expand the program from June 1 to allow select pharmacists to give contraceptive medication to patients over the age of 18 who have never been prescribed the pill by a doctor.
Not all patients will be eligible for the scheme, with those who have a history of blood clotting to be referred to a GP.
The consult service will be free for the first 5,000 patients who use the scheme as part of a $4.5 million state government investment.
NSW Premier Chris Minns said the change was "long overdue".
"It shouldn't be too difficult to get access to this basic health care," he said.
Mr Minns said consults were expected to cost between $20 and $60 after the free appointments were exhausted, with pharmacists to set their own prices.
Complex prescription cases will still be referred to a doctor. ( Flickr: Alex Proimos )
NSW Health Minister Ryan Park said the initiative aimed to reduce doctors' workloads and increase access to affordable health care.
"This is all about making sure that women will be able to access this type of contraceptive and health care in a way that's more convenient, and at the same time takes pressure off our GPs," he said.
summary: Pharmacists will soon be able to prescribe contraceptive medication to new patients. ( Pixabay: GabiSanda: CC0 )

title: Family hopes epilepsy Senate inquiry will shine light on funding gaps
content:
Kat Robinson says her son Leo's Dravet syndrome has resulted in other health complications. ( ABC Capricornia: Katrina Beavan )
Four-year-old Leo has a rare form of epilepsy and his family is fundraising to help pay for a specialised seizure alert dog.
Under the NDIS, epilepsy is primarily considered a medical condition covered under the health system, a classification that advocacy groups say leaves many in the lurch.
Epilepsy Australia hopes an upcoming Senate inquiry will shine a light on funding gaps for the condition.
Every night when Kat Robinson puts her son Leo to bed, she is terrified he will have a seizure and never wake up.
"That haunts my nightmares … knowing that if I go to sleep, I'm not keeping eyes on Leo," Ms Robinson said.
"We have anti-suffocation pillows, we have a video monitor on him, but all of that is not going to tell us if something is happening."
The rambunctious four-year-old was diagnosed with a rare, medication-resistant form of epilepsy, Dravet syndrome, when he was one.
"It's like living with a ticking time bomb, except you can't see the countdown."
Kat Robinson says Leo loves animals, reading and playing the drums. ( ABC Capricornia: Katrina Beavan )
Ms Robinson is hoping a specialised seizure alert dog will help, but it comes at a cost of more than $40,000, then likely thousands of dollars more for ongoing caring duties.
"As a parent of a medically complex child, if there's something that even has a small chance of helping your child live a more normal life, you'll do it," she said.
It is a high out-of-pocket cost for the Rockhampton mother of two, who said the price of medications and health appointments already stretched the budget.
Although she is able to receive some financial assistance for Leo's fortnightly speech and occupational therapy appointments from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), her application for a support worker was knocked back.
Epilepsy Queensland chief executive Sandi Rodiger said funding gaps for the condition were a common issue for many families.
summary: Kat Robinson says her son Leo's Dravet syndrome has resulted in other health complications. ( ABC Capricornia: Katrina Beavan )
author: Katrina Beavan

title: Ombudsman report into interaction between Queensland Health and boys with disabilities makes 13 recommendations
content:
Two brothers, known by pseudonyms Kaleb and Jonathan, were found in May 2020 severely malnourished and locked in a room naked. ( Supplied: Royal Commission into Violence, Abuse, Neglect, and Exploitation of People with Disability )
A report by the Queensland Ombudsman has been released on the interactions between health care workers and two siblings, known by the pseudonyms Kaleb and Jonathon, who were discovered living in squalor in May 2020.
The ombudsman's report found Kaleb and Jonathan — who were diagnosed with "significant global development delay" and intellectual disability — missed multiple appointments with Queensland Health specialist services between 2005 and 2020.
The Queensland ombudsman has conducted an independent review into the powers and responsibilities of all departments and agencies that engaged with Kaleb, Jonathon, and their father.
Queensland Health staff missed multiple opportunities — across 15 years — to report child safety concerns about two brothers with disabilities found severely malnourished and locked in a room naked, an official probe has found.
Warning: This story contains content that readers may find distressing.
A 48-page report by the Queensland Ombudsman has been released on the interactions between health care workers and the siblings, known by the pseudonyms Kaleb and Jonathon, who were discovered living in squalor in May 2020.
Emergency services also found their father, who was their carer, dead in the Brisbane home.
The brothers were found locked in a room. ( Supplied: Royal Commission into Violence, Abuse, Neglect, and Exploitation of People with Disability )
The ombudsman's report found Kaleb and Jonathan — who were diagnosed with "significant global development delay" and intellectual disability — missed multiple appointments with Queensland Health specialist services between 2005 and 2020.
However, health workers did not always follow up on their non-attendance.
Despite this, and reports to child protection services between 2000 and 2005, Queensland Health did not make any other referrals to Child Safety.
The shocking case was examined by the disability royal commission to determine how and why they experienced violence, abuse, neglect, and a deprivation of human rights during their childhood and adolescence.
The commission recommended that the Queensland government should apologise for omissions in preventing the harm they experienced.
A formal apology was delivered in September 2023.
summary: Two brothers, known by pseudonyms Kaleb and Jonathan, were found in May 2020 severely malnourished and locked in a room naked. ( Supplied: Royal Commission into Violence, Abuse, Neglect, and Exploitation of People with Disability )
author: Janelle Miles

title: WA government offers FluMist nasal spray vaccine free to children
content:
FluMist is a nasal vaccine designed for children who are anxious about needles. ( ABC News: Samantha Goerling )
The West Australian government is rolling out 130,000 doses of a needle-free influenza vaccine for children aged two to 11 years old.
Public health experts hope the nasal spray, called FluMist, will curb declining childhood vaccination rates.
FluMist is available from Tuesday, April 14, until the end of July.
Western Australia's government started to roll out on Tuesday a needle-free influenza vaccine for children, a step that could help prevent a repeat of last year's worst season on record.
The vaccine, called FluMist, is aimed at children who are scared of needles and is available free of charge for those aged two to 11 at pharmacies, GPs, Aboriginal Medical Services and community health clinics.
Around 130,000 doses of the needle-free vaccine are being distributed across Western Australia. ( ABC News: Samantha Goerling )
There are 130,000 doses available as part of the state's $6.6 million flu vaccination program.
Minister for Preventative Health Sabine Winton said she expects the needle-free alternative to be a "gamechanger" in helping reverse the decline in childhood vaccination rates nationwide.
Sabine Winton is helping shape WA's preventative health strategy. ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
"About 6 out of 10 parents experience distress when thinking about vaccinating their child against influenza and more than a quarter of young kids experience intense needle-phobia," she said.
"We also know that five to nine year olds is the age cohort with the highest influence and notifications last year.
Ms Winton said FluMist had been safely used in the northern hemisphere since 2003.
Similar vaccination programs are being rolled out in Queensland, New South Wales and South Australia ahead of winter.
It's been available in the northern hemisphere for years, now Australian children can receive a nasal spray vaccine for the flu.
summary: FluMist is a nasal vaccine designed for children who are anxious about needles. ( ABC News: Samantha Goerling )
author: Samantha Goerling

title: Bullsbrook COVID quarantine centre could be converted into prison
content:
Accommodation at the Bullsbrook quarantine centre is sitting empty at present. ( Supplied: Lisa Castellas )
The WA government has confirmed it is investigating the potential conversion of the Bullsbrook quarantine facility into a prison amid overcrowding across the state's jails.
But the union representing prison officers has poured cold water on the idea, and criticised the government for not building a prison sooner.
The ABC understands the WA government is in discussion with its federal counterparts to bring the facility into the state's hands.
The WA government has flagged the possible conversion of the Bullsbrook COVID quarantine centre into a prison amid an "unprecedented" rise in the number of inmates being crammed into cells across the state.
But the union representing prison officers has poured cold water on the idea, saying the site is not suitable for many inmates, while taking aim at the government for dragging its feet on the construction of a purpose-built jail.
The 500-bed quarantine facility, located on Commonwealth land 40 kilometres north-east of Perth's CBD, was completed in 2022 but has sat almost idle since then.
Some had suggested the centre could provide shelter for rough sleepers , but the government has confirmed it is investigating whether the site can be turned into a prison.
The Bullsbrook facility was returned to Commonwealth ownership in 2023. ( ABC News )
Speaking outside parliament on Tuesday, Corrective Services Minister Paul Papalia did not deny suggestions the government was eyeing off the site for that purpose.
"Shelter WA said that site is not suitable for rough sleepers because it's in the middle of nowhere, effectively," he said.
"Conversely, that might make it a good place for a prison."
WA Corrective Services Minister Paul Papalia says there has been a huge rise in the state's prison population. ( ABC News: Andrew O'Connor )
Mr Papalia pointed to an increase in WA's prison population and said the government was looking at "a range of measures" to meet demand.
"What's happened in the last two-and-a-half years is we have massively increased the number of people in our prisons, mostly on remand for mostly family and domestic violence offences," he said.
summary: Accommodation at the Bullsbrook quarantine centre is sitting empty at present. ( Supplied: Lisa Castellas )
author: Courtney Withers

title: Family of Bondi Junction Westfield attack victim call for quicker movement on inquest findings
content:
Elizabeth Young finds comfort in her family and dog following the murder of her daughter Jade Young two years ago at Bondi Junction Westfield. ( ABC News: Sarah Moss )
The bereaved family of one of the Bondi Junction stabbing victims is urging the NSW government to address "what has actually been done" in the wake of the inquest and the attack's second anniversary.
In February, NSW State Coroner Magistrate Teresa O'Sullivan handed down her coronial inquest findings on the attack at Sydney's Bondi Junction Westfield on April 13, 2024.
There were 23 recommendations directed towards varying agencies and organisations.
Seven of the recommendations were given to the NSW government, excluding recommendations to NSW Ambulance.
Elizabeth Young, the mother of 47-year-old victim Jade Young, contacted Premier Chris Minns, Health Minister Ryan Park and Mental Health Minister Rose Jackson for answers on March 21.
"What has the Minns Government actually done to implement the recommendations that belong to NSW?" she wrote.
"Not what has been acknowledged. Not what advice has been requested. Not what is being considered. What has actually been done."
A "floridly psychotic" Joel Cauchi murdered six people.
Elizabeth Young said families like hers did "not need more sympathy dressed up as action".
Jade's cousin Toby Halligan reiterated his family's focus on pursuing tangible change.
"They [the government] have not advanced on some of the really important things that the coroner raised," he told the ABC.
The Young family received a response from Mr Minns almost three weeks later on April 10, with media enquiries made in-between.
"I can only begin to imagine the immense pain and grief that you and your family are experiencing, and I acknowledge your passionate advocacy for change that could prevent similar tragedies," Mr Minns wrote.
He pointed to a $111.8 million package from the state government to support community mental health.
summary: Elizabeth Young finds comfort in her family and dog following the murder of her daughter Jade Young two years ago at Bondi Junction Westfield. ( ABC News: Sarah Moss )
author: Isabella Ross

title: Salty Dog Hotel the target of noise complaints as performers say Tasmanian live music is under threat
content:
Kim Phillips with Salty Dog Hotel co-owner Adriano Mignanelli at the venue's new outdoor stage. ( ABC News )
Tasmanian musicians say it is becoming increasingly harder to secure gigs because fewer pubs are hosting live music.
The drop in opportunities is being blamed on the cost of hosting acts and complaints about noise.
The state's peak body for contemporary music says policies need to be developed to support the future of live gigs.
Musicians say the future of live music in Tasmanian pubs is under threat as noise complaints and venue closures make it increasingly difficult to find gigs.
Kim Phillips, who has sung for decades in multiple bands based in the state's south, said the opportunities of the past "just don't seem to exist" anymore.
"What you have is less venues that are available that are actually hosting live music," she said.
Live music at the Salty Dog, before the stage was moved to point away from residences and towards the beach. ( Facebook: The Salty Dog Hotel )
Raymond Blizzard, who has been involved in the Hobart music scene for more than 20 years, said he was concerned for the future of the industry.
"I'm a bit luckier than some in that I've been around … but if you're young and just trying to get into the business, it's very, very difficult at the moment," he said.
The pair blamed dwindling live music opportunities on multiple key venues closing, as well as the pressure being put on pubs to manage complaints from neighbours.
Raymond Blizzard says it's "very difficult" for young performers to secure gigs at Tasmanian venues. ( ABC News: Jake Grant )
The closure of Uni Bar on the University of Tasmania campus last year due to a redevelopment was described as a "significant blow" for Hobart's music scene.
The sector was rattled even further when it was announced Hobart Brewing Co would close due to the approval of the $1.13-billion AFL stadium on Macquarie Point.
Bliss n Eso performing at the Uni Bar in Hobart in 2023. The venue has since closed. ( Supplied: Zoe Lowry )
summary: Kim Phillips with Salty Dog Hotel co-owner Adriano Mignanelli at the venue's new outdoor stage. ( ABC News )
author: Monty Jacka

title: Australian man Sam Amine wanted in Fiji over record meth import from cartel
content:
Fijian police are targeting the import of drugs bound for markets including Australia. ( Foreign Correspondent )
Police in Fiji want Australian man Sam Amine to face trial for his alleged role in a massive transnational drug importation.
A Foreign Correspondent investigation into Fiji's largest-ever meth bust reveals his alleged links to organised crime in Sydney and Fiji.
Authorities in both countries are now pursuing millions of dollars' worth of Amine's assets said to be proceeds of crime.
An Australian man is wanted by Fiji police for allegedly orchestrating the importation of more than four tonnes of methamphetamine — the largest drug seizure in the island nation's history.
Sydney man Sam Amine, 49, was labelled a "drug kingpin" by a Fiji High Court judge last year — when nine Fijians were given the most severe sentences ever handed down for drug crime — but has never faced charges over his alleged involvement.
"We have already rounded up the local crime syndicates," Fiji Police Assistant Commissioner Mesake Waqa told Foreign Correspondent.
"We will only rest once we charge those responsible, those international crime syndicates.
"It is my wish for Sam Amine to face trial here in Fiji."
Fiji police want Sam Amine to face trial for his alleged role in a transnational meth importation. ( Foreign Correspondent: Mark Hiney )
The massive consignment of drugs seized in January 2024 was supplied by the Sinaloa cartel in Mexico, stockpiled in Fiji, and destined for the lucrative Australian and New Zealand markets, authorities have confirmed.
Amine helped arrange and pay to get the drugs into the country before they were stored at his warehouse in the premier tourist hub of Denarau for several days, according to evidence accepted by a Fiji High Court judge.
Senior Australian law enforcement sources told the ABC they believed Amine was "a major regional target" and "a door" for large-scale drug importations from the Pacific to Australia.
Assistant Commissioner Waqa said some of the methamphetamine was exported via commercial air carriers bound for Australia before the larger consignment was seized by police.
Mesake Waqa is determined to bring Sam Amine to Fiji to face justice. ( Foreign Correspondent: Sissy Reyes )
summary: Fijian police are targeting the import of drugs bound for markets including Australia. ( Foreign Correspondent )
author: Stephanie March

title: Tasmanian student vaccinated without consent prompts council call for state government to run program
content:
The West Tamar Council says it employs "authorised immunisers", usually a nurse practitioner, to administer the vaccinations. ( Reuters: Annie Rice )
Tasmanian local governments will lobby for the state health department to take over primary responsibility for school-based immunisation programs.
The West Tamar mayor says a student was recently vaccinated without parental consent.
The local government body will write to Tasmanian Health Minister Bridget Archer.
A Tasmanian mayor is calling on the state government to take on responsibility for running school immunisation programs, after a student was vaccinated without caregiver consent.
West Tamar Mayor Christina Holmdahl said the incident highlighted the problem with having free school immunisation programs coordinated by local governments.
"I can tell you it was a minefield to try to sort out," she told ABC Northern Tasmania Breakfast.
Christina Holmdahl says council should be "adequately compensated" by the state government for the vaccination service. ( ABC News: Jessica Moran )
A West Tamar Council spokesperson said when it became aware that a student had been vaccinated through its program without consent, all the relevant mandatory referrals were made to the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA).
It said it could not comment further on the matter due to privacy laws.
But the Local Government Association of Tasmania (LGAT) is calling for the modification of the Public Health Act 1997, which requires councils to develop and implement immunisation programs in schools.
According to LGAT, Tasmania and Victoria are the only Australian states where councils are responsible for these programs.
The West Tamar Council said its immunisation program, which vaccinates students against human papillomavirus (HPV) and meningococcal ACWY, costs about $20,000 a year, with about $5,000 reimbursed by Tasmania's Department of Health.
Cr Holmdahl said it was an unfair cost to be shouldered by ratepayers.
"It has a financial impact on councils, and if government is going to require us to continue running these programs … the council should be adequately compensated for the service."
summary: The West Tamar Council says it employs "authorised immunisers", usually a nurse practitioner, to administer the vaccinations. ( Reuters: Annie Rice )
author: Hayden Smith

title: WA government's $1.5 billion spend on hospitals sparks questions on where money will go
content:
Premier Roger Cook made a huge commitment to health spending on Monday, but where the money would go was not immediately clear. ( ABC News: Courtney Withers )
WA's hospital spend was already large — and it has just got even bigger, although with very little detail.
On Monday morning, in front of a busy construction site for the new Women and Babies Hospital in Murdoch, the WA government announced an additional $1.5 billion in health infrastructure spending as part of its 2026-27 State Budget, due to be handed down next month.
It already scrapped its Perth Convention and Exhibition Centre revamp project last November, to free up and create a $1.5 billion Building Hospitals Fund.
The construction site for the new Women and Babies Hospital in Murdoch. ( ABC News: Courtney Withers )
That came after the August announcement of a $50 million infrastructure fund to maintain WA’s aging hospitals.
Now it's splashing an additional $1.5 billion, bringing the state's total hospital spend to $5.5 billion over the next four years.
It's a lot of money, without a lot of specifics.
A third of the new $1.5 billion is going into the Building Hospitals Fund, bringing that total to $2 billion.
Then $214.1 million is going towards increasing the $50 million hospital maintenance fund.
And $36.2 million will then establish a 'Central Commissioning Office' — something the government says is necessary to oversee and deliver current projects which will bring more than 900 beds online over the next four years.
Doing some quick maths, that leaves about $749 million unaccounted for.
WA Health Infrastructure Minister John Carey. ( ABC News: Courtney Withers )
So what's that money for? Cost blowouts or new projects? We'll see.
"Over the next few weeks, and in the budget, you will see ... where that additional expenditure is going," Health Infrastructure Minister John Carey said.
summary: Premier Roger Cook made a huge commitment to health spending on Monday, but where the money would go was not immediately clear. ( ABC News: Courtney Withers )
author: Courtney Withers

title: Youth mental health centre to close amid Kiama Council budget crisis
content:
Penny Rushby-Smith says her son Felix has benefited significantly from Kiama's SENTRAL Youth Services by confiding in staff on social issues. ( Supplied: Penny Rushby-Smith )
A NSW South Coast community says the proposed closure of a mental health and welfare centre could result in more youth suicides.
It is among several proposed staff and community service cuts endorsed by Kiama Council to address its multi-million-dollar deficit.
Council's draft budget is set to be put on public exhibition later this month.
Parents and mental health professionals on the New South Wales South Coast fear the proposed closure of a youth centre could result in an increase in suicides and leave young people worse off.
Her 15-year-old son Felix has accessed Kiama SENTRAL Youth Services over the past three years for help with his transition to high school.
Established in 1993, the council-owned and operated facility offers free mental health, domestic-violence, homelessness and extra-curricular support to dozens of local teenagers.
"[Kiama SENTRAL has] been a great option to have another trusted adult that he could get some advice from or turn to if there were some speed bumps along the way," Ms Rushby-Smith said.
"And I also feel like, as a parent, I can reach out to those youth workers and ask for a hand as well."
Free food and life lessons like cooking are some of the services offered by three employees at Kiama's SENTRAL Youth Services. ( ABC Illawarra: Mikayla McGuirk-Scolaro )
The centre's doors are likely to close in the coming months after Kiama Council adopted a range of budget cuts to make up for its $4.5 million deficit.
Council needs to balance its budget by June 2027 under a Performance Improvement Order (PIO) issued by the Office of Local Government (OLG) or face the prospect of administration.
Among the cuts are defunding the youth service, reducing library hours, leasing back vehicles and reducing sports field maintenance and non-essential staff.
Mum-of-two Penny Rushby-Smith was among dozens of Kiama ratepayers to protest the council's proposed budget cuts. ( ABC Illawarra: Mikayla McGuirk-Scolaro )
"This youth service is giving [kids] a place to go. And if they defund that, even as a temporary measure, they lose that relationship with the youth workers … and you can't restart that.
summary: Penny Rushby-Smith says her son Felix has benefited significantly from Kiama's SENTRAL Youth Services by confiding in staff on social issues. ( Supplied: Penny Rushby-Smith )
author: Brooke Chandler

title: Fuel crisis creating COVID-esque shocks for workers and businesses
content:
Lecia Louise is worried about rising fuel prices, as she prepares to go on tour in her diesel van. ( Supplied )
Musician Lecia Louise will hit the road soon on a month-long tour in her diesel van — travelling about 3,500 kilometres from gig to gig.
But the independent artist is unsure whether her shows will still go ahead, after organising them across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria.
Louise, who is based in Toowoomba in south-east Queensland, has her van packed with a travel bag, music equipment and merchandise.
Her boot is full of a selection of guitars, sound systems, vinyl records, CDs, posters and food supplies.
Iran war live updates: For the latest news on the Middle East crisis, read our blog
Now, days away from her first gig, she remembers the last crisis that stopped her from performing live — the coronavirus pandemic.
Louise said in contrast to COVID-19, travel was now not restricted — it was just very expensive.
This month, Anthony Albanese gave the first national address by a prime minister since the pandemic, saying the months ahead would not be easy on Australians grappling with spiralling petrol prices as a result of the Iran war.
In 2020–21, it was regular official updates on COVID-19 case numbers. Now, it is updates on the number of petrol stations without fuel.
"Chatting with other musicians, a lot are staying home or cancelling tours," Louise told the ABC.
"I don't really want to cancel mine because it took so many hours to put it together.
"[But] every day and every night I'm just extremely stressed. I'll wake up early in the morning on some nights for a couple of hours thinking, 'How's this going to work?'"
WedShed's Amy Parfett manages about 10 wedding venues and says bookings are drying up.
Ms Parfett said it took about 12 months on average to plan a wedding, which meant it could take years to recover after a drop in bookings.
summary: Lecia Louise is worried about rising fuel prices, as she prepares to go on tour in her diesel van. ( Supplied )
author: Ahmed Yussuf

title: Love story: When love means donating a kidney to your brother
content:
Codey Stow (right) says he has new-found motivation and energy since Sajarn's life-changing kidney donation. ( Supplied )
Despite a 10-year age gap, brothers Sajarn and Codey Stow, who share Juru, Bindal and Kaantju cultural heritage, have shared a close bond since their childhood in north Queensland.
Sajarn, 42, a producer and presenter for ABC Darwin, and Codey, 32, who lives in Central Queensland and works as a land and sea ranger on Bailai, Gurang, Gooreng Gooreng and Taribelang Bunda country, now also share Sajarn’s kidneys.
Whether it's romantic, platonic, heartbreaking or surprising, our Love Stories series is about love in all its forms. Share yours with us.
We've got that 10-year age difference, and Sajarn was about 21 when he left to go to Melbourne from Townsville, so at that time, I was about 11.
It was mainly just Mum raising us kids, and Sajarn was more of the man of the house at that time, so he was always the one I'd talk to about things and call or message if I needed advice and we talk quite often now.
Despite their age difference, Sajarn and Codey have had a close bond since childhood. ( Supplied: Michelle Stout )
I've always looked at him as my big brother, and he's always had that responsibility. He's still the big brother, but it's no longer, 'Oh, you're just my big brother.' He's my close friend. We can just talk about things that obviously he couldn't talk to me about when I was a kid, because I wouldn't have understood.
In 2018 or 2019 I was diagnosed with the degenerative kidney disease [IgA nephropathy]. At that point, I think my kidney function was sitting around 40 per cent. [When] I got to 2024, the doctors said, 'We need to start considering dialysis soon because your kidney function has decreased even more.'
Codey has always looked up to Sajarn as a big brother, but he also considers him a close friend. ( Supplied: Michelle Stout )
The question was raised about a transplant … At the time, the people we were talking to were like, 'Look, we really kind of wait until patients are on dialysis before we start looking into transplants.'
Mum talked to the family, and Sajarn and my younger sister, Shaquille, both put their hands up straight away and went, 'We'll donate.'
With Sajarn, he's always on the ball, and he just gets things done, so he was like, 'I'm onto it, all right, let's do this, let's do all these tests.' So we just went off and found out what we needed to do, and we just kept pushing for it, and the doctors gave in and went, 'OK, we'll work towards it.'
It was a big journey … Obviously, there were hurdles to overcome, so it was good to have Sajarn to talk to and we were just helping each other, and he had [his partner] Leigh, as well, who was a very big help. I think it just brought us all a lot closer.
Do you have, or know of, a surprising, funny or sweet love story? We'd love to hear from you for our Love Stories column ... because it's not all bad news. Share with us.
summary: Codey Stow (right) says he has new-found motivation and energy since Sajarn's life-changing kidney donation. ( Supplied )
author: Sajarn Stow

title: Regional Australia leads GP bulk-billing surge as costs to see doctors continue to rise
content:
Cleanbill has released its April 2026 Blue Report. ( Pexels : Alex Green / License )
Online GP directory Cleanbill has released its April Blue Report, indicating a higher percentage of regional clinics are switching to a full bulk-billing model than their metropolitan counterparts.
The report shows the out-of-pocket cost for non-bulk-billed appointments is still on the rise across the country.
A Melbourne university expert and GP says the "two-tier" system is jeopardising universal health care in Australia.
A new report from online GP directory Cleanbill shows Australian patients at clinics that do not fully bulk-bill are paying an extra 13.5 per cent to see a GP than in the past.
The April Blue Report, informed by the 6,900 clinics in Cleanbill's directory, indicates that a standard consult now costs $49.23 out of pocket.
The report also showed that a higher percentage of regional clinics were switching to a full bulk-billing model than those in metropolitan areas.
Public servant Matthew Lillywhite said he used to fly from his home in the Australian Capital Territory to his family general practitioner (GP) in Melbourne because it was easier than seeing his local doctor.
A standard consult at his doctor in Canberra costs $140 up front, including a $40 rebate through Medicare.
That price had increased $40 from $100 per session when he moved to the capital in 2023.
Matthew Lillywhite and his flight attendant friend, Alison Achter. ( Supplied: Matthew Lillywhite )
Mr Lillywhite lives with chronic health conditions that require regular monitoring and scripts.
A flight attendant friend hooked him up with $80 return tickets to get to his bulk-billing doctor in Melbourne.
While he no longer has access to those cheap flights he said that was how he survived the last few years, while juggling chronic illness and regular GP appointments.
In recent times, Mr Lillywhite's salary has increased significantly, meaning the percentage of his income spent on GP sessions has decreased overall.
summary: Cleanbill has released its April 2026 Blue Report. ( Pexels : Alex Green / License )
author: Tamara Clark

title: The children feared to be at highest risk of missing out under Thriving Kids
content:
Alice Tran (right) says while her boys may look "moderate" on paper", that label doesn't tell the whole story. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
The first phase of the $4 billion NDIS alternative for kids with "mild to moderate" developmental delays or autism is months away from starting.
Thriving Kids is a key measure of the government's aims to reign in the growth of the NDIS, which now costs more than $50 billion every year.
Parents and experts are concerned kids with "moderate" needs could slip between the cracks of both systems.
When Christian and Nik Tran joined the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), mum Alice was relieved they would finally get the support needed to live a life like any other kid.
Nik, 8, has been diagnosed with level two autism , while Christian, 5, has developmental delay and is waiting on confirmation of his own level two autism diagnosis.
Ms Tran said the boys needed support doing up buttons, tying shoelaces, regulating emotions and navigating complex behaviours which could quickly escalate if not managed.
She said they required constant supervision.
"I always try to make sure I've eliminated everything that could trigger them … but the world is a trigger," Ms Tran said.
Alice Tran supports her boys in a multitude of ways, including tying shoes. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
Christian and Nik receive NDIS funding for psychology, as well as occupational and speech therapy, all of which Alice said had made a huge difference.
"I've already seen some really positive changes with [Christian] being able to explain and word his emotions and how he's feeling," she said.
Ms Tran has quit work to care for her boys full time — including attending school with Christian to support him in class — and is grateful for the assistance from the NDIS.
Alice Tran says Christian's (left) therapy is delivering good results. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
But in the future, it is expected children like Christian and Nik will not be supported by the NDIS, but by Thriving Kids — a $4 billion pillar of the federal government's efforts to rein in the growth of the NDIS, which has been projected to cost more than $50 billion this financial year.
summary: Alice Tran (right) says while her boys may look "moderate" on paper", that label doesn't tell the whole story. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
author: Evan Young

title: Disabled artists challenge stereotypes in Acts of Inheritance at Bathurst Regional Art Gallery
content:
In Acts of Inheritance, disabled artists explore the idea that identity is layered and constantly evolving. ( Supplied: Silversalt Photography/Bathurst Regional Art Gallery )
"John Cena meets Jesus" is how artist and disabled advocate Jordan Valageorgiou describes the sheer variety of art on show at the new Bathurst Regional Art Gallery exhibition Acts of Inheritance.
The show, curated by Valageorgiou, features the work of 17 disabled artists from We Are Studios, an artist-led collective based in Blacktown, Western Sydney.
Animated by the idea of identity as something we both create and inherit, the works include a music video by Jane Thatcher, an installation exploring wrestling culture by Timothy W Martin and jewellery by Adrienne Proud.
"This show is a raw, gutsy and honest exposé by the artists that reveals deep truths in unexpected ways," Valageorgiou writes in the exhibition essay. ( Supplied: Silversalt Photography/Bathurst Regional Art Gallery )
Valageorgiou, a co-founder of We Are Studios, says it is important to include a range of perspectives and styles to capture the diversity of how people experience disability.
"For us, it's about the vitality of each of us as studio artists within a collective, being able to explore material, scale and emotions and to participate in a dialogue that otherwise we might not be a part of."
When an early visitor called Acts of Inheritance a "self-indulgent" exhibition, Valageorgiou was, perhaps surprisingly, delighted by the critique.
"That's the most beautiful thing; it's exactly what we're looking for," he says.
"It's vital that Disabled artists are taken at face value and are given the opportunity to demonstrate their worth. We welcome all feedback.
Miah Tito-Barratt contributed an artwork titled Nebulous to the exhibition.
Created with gesso, pencil and gouache on paper, it's a dreamy and non-didactic cloudscape, inviting viewers to form their own interpretation.
Tito-Barratt says they were inspired by how everyone looks at the same cloud formations but sees something slightly different.
"You might see a dog, an avocado or a starfish. The person next to you might not see any of those shapes, but they could also point out something you haven't noticed yet.
"I think that is the beauty of perspective, because when in our everyday lives do we allow for someone else's lived experience to change our perspective?"
summary: In Acts of Inheritance, disabled artists explore the idea that identity is layered and constantly evolving. ( Supplied: Silversalt Photography/Bathurst Regional Art Gallery )
author: Daniel Herborn

title: Low-sensory voting to be introduced statewide at 2026 Victorian election
content:
Neurodivergent voters in Victoria will be able to access low-sensory voting centres in November. ( Supplied: Victorian Electoral Commission )
The Victorian Electoral Commission (VEC) will host the nation's biggest trial of low-sensory voting at this year's State Election.
All 88 Lower House districts will have a dedicated low-sensory voting centre, with reduced sound, moderated lighting, subdued visuals and regulated crowd levels.
The VEC is identifying suitable locations to reduce sensory overwhelm for neurodivergent voters.
Neurodivergent voters in Victoria will be offered a dedicated low-sensory venue to cast their ballots during the upcoming November election.
The Victorian Electoral Commission (VEC) is rolling out low-sensory voting centres in every Victorian electorate.
The selected venues will be quieter, with a controlled temperature, extra space around voting booths and natural lighting instead of artificial lighting.
The sites will address barriers that make in-person voting overwhelming or inaccessible for some people, including interactions with political campaigners.
Providing a low-sensory option in all 88 Victorian electorates will make November's election Australia's biggest trial of low-sensory voting.
The option was first tested in Victoria at the Warrandyte by-election in 2023, by introducing low-sensory hours during early voting.
While feedback was overwhelmingly positive, the model revealed challenges surrounding campaigning activity outside voting centres.
There was also a lack of clarity about which voters could use the service during low-sensory hours.
Low-sensory voting centres will act to reduce barriers to voting for neurodivergent people. ( Supplied: Victorian Electoral Commission )
Low-sensory voting was tested again at the Prahran and Werribee by‑elections early last year, on the day before early voting commenced.
Voting centre accessibility was the subject of a recent Victorian Electoral Matters Committee inquiry.
summary: Neurodivergent voters in Victoria will be able to access low-sensory voting centres in November. ( Supplied: Victorian Electoral Commission )
author: Wade Stephens

title: Three Tasmanian MPs share their experiences with mental health and politics
content:
Ella Haddad, Jeremy Rockliff and Ruth Forrest want the parliament to be a safe place for those with mental health concerns. ( ABC News )
A national study has found 70 per cent of politicians across all three levels of government feel their job is affecting their mental health, and one in five thought about harming themselves due to the pressure of the job.
Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff, Labor MP Ella Haddad and independent MLC Ruth Forrest all say the job can be tough, both inside the parliament and outside of it.
All three say it's important that people with mental health conditions feel safe to run for office.
Politics has a mental health problem, or at least the politicians do.
A nationwide survey of almost 300 politicians across all three levels of government has found 70 per cent of politicians feel the job is impacting their mental health.
Forty per cent of them felt prevented from speaking out due to their lack of mental wellbeing.
One in five thought about harming themselves due to the pressure of the job.
And almost three-quarters said that compared with other workplaces, parliament is the worst they have experienced.
Conducted by the Better Politics Foundation, Compassion in Politics Australia and psychiatrist Patrick McGorry — a former Australian of the Year — the study aims to understand what is going on and how to address it.
In the wake of the results, the ABC has spoken to three Tasmanian MPs about social media, mental health and the culture in parliament
A former Lifeline counsellor, Jeremy Rockliff has been a strong advocate for mental health. ( Facebook: Jeremy Rockliff )
Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff sometimes wonders whether he will feel the effects of political life more when he has left it behind.
"I just don't know if I could have put up with it had I not had years and years [of] experience," Mr Rockliff said.
It was more than two decades ago that the former Lifeline counsellor first put his hand up to run for the Liberal Party in the north-west seat of Braddon.
summary: Ella Haddad, Jeremy Rockliff and Ruth Forrest want the parliament to be a safe place for those with mental health concerns. ( ABC News )
author: Lucy Macdonald

title: Adelaide GP Mark Rogers disqualified for professional misconduct amid 'blatant disregard' for previous orders
content:
Topic: Doctors and Medical Professionals
Adelaide GP Mark Rogers has been disqualified from seeking re-registration as a medical practitioner for 18 months. ( Supplied: Pexels )
A tribunal has cancelled the registration of Adelaide doctor Mark Rogers over several allegations of professional misconduct.
Dr Rogers was a proponent of low-level laser therapy and was previously suspended for two months and banned from offering or providing the service while practising medicine.
Dr Rogers has been reprimanded and disqualified from applying for re-registration as a health practitioner and prohibited from providing any health service for 18 months.
A doctor with an "unhealthy obsession" for low-level laser therapy that was "almost delusional" has had his registration cancelled after a tribunal found he demonstrated a "blatant disregard" of previous orders imposed on his registration.
The Medical Board of Australia referred a complaint to the South Australian Civil and Administrative Tribunal with six allegations against Adelaide GP Mark Rogers.
The allegations include that he failed to provide good patient care, failed to disclose a potential conflict of interest, made false and misleading statements to authorities and engaged in financial and commercial dealings with a patient.
The complaint also alleged that Dr Rogers breached a commitment to the board and failed to comply with the conditions of his registration.
Dr Rogers — a general practitioner of more than 30 years — was a proponent of low-level laser therapy (LLLT) and made claims that the therapy could heal Alzheimer's which he later admitted were false .
In 2019 he was reprimanded, suspended for two months and banned from offering or providing LLLT services while practising medicine.
In a recently published ruling, the tribunal noted Dr Rogers had given written undertakings to the board, including that he would only answer questions in relation to LLLT in "general terms" and would confine his interest in the therapy to "academic literature, debate and reported research".
The South Australian Civil and Administrative Tribunal found Dr Rogers engaged in professional misconduct. ( ABC News: Eugene Boisvert )
The ruling noted the allegations occurred between 2017 and 2020, and Dr Rogers "now realises that he has had an unhealthy obsession with LLLT which in retrospect he realises has jeopardised his medical vocation and has ruined his life and caused financial disaster".
"The respondent says that because of his unhealthy obsession with LLLT which was 'almost delusional' he found it difficult not to talk about that treatment with the patients … and that as a result, he did not make notes in their patient records of their conversations about that treatment because he knew that he should not have been having those conversations because of the assurances he gave the Board in 2017."
summary: Topic: Doctors and Medical Professionals

title: Sexual misconduct findings permanently added to health practitioners' records
content:
More than 100 health practitioners have now had findings of sexual misconduct added to their public record. ( ABC News: Robert Koenig-Luck )
More than 100 health practitioners have had previous sexual misconduct findings added to the public register.
Most have had their registration cancelled but 21 continue to practise.
AHPRA says more misconduct findings will be added over the coming months.
More than 100 past and current health practitioners have had findings of sexual misconduct added to their public-facing records under reforms to promote transparency.
Most of those practitioners have had their registration cancelled, but 21 are still practising.
The Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA) is reviewing 5,000 professional misconduct findings to identify which of those involved sexual misconduct.
So far, it has identified 107 but more practitioners are still under investigation and will not have their records updated until later this year.
Most identified so far are doctors and the highest proportion are practising, or did practise, in NSW and Victoria.
Up until now, it has not been easy for patients to see whether their practitioner had any past findings of sexual misconduct because the register only showed active disciplinary sanctions.
Once a practitioner served their sanction or suspension, any mention of their misconduct was removed.
Following an uptick in patient complaints about sexual misconduct, last year all health ministers agreed to change the law so that misconduct decisions would permanently appear on the record.
The law is retrospective and applies to all practitioners regulated by AHPRA, including doctors, nurses, dentists, psychologists, pharmacists and physiotherapists.
AHPRA CEO Justin Untersteiner says it was a massive task, but the process would be finalised by June. ( Supplied )
AHPRA CEO Justin Untersteiner said it was an important change to meet community expectations.
summary: More than 100 health practitioners have now had findings of sexual misconduct added to their public record. ( ABC News: Robert Koenig-Luck )
author: Paige Cockburn

title: Jog for Joe aims to raise awareness of male eating disorders in Australia
content:
Danielle Cappello said her brother Joe would go out of his way to help others. ( Supplied: Danielle Cappello )
Joe Cappello was 25 when he died after a 12-year battle with an eating disorder.
In Australia, one in three people diagnosed identify as male, with men being four times more likely to go undiagnosed.
Danielle Cappello is keeping her brother's memory alive by spreading awareness with community events.
Joe Cappello was a vibrant, selfless man who would go out of his way to make sure his friends and family were taken care of.
His sister Danielle said it would have been hard for people to understand the battle he was fighting behind closed doors.
"He just wanted to do everything for everybody else and was always the one to say hi to people around the neighbourhood," Ms Cappello said.
"He was a tutor, he was studying at university, he had a run club — he was just into everything."
Privately, the 25-year-old had been fighting a 12-year battle with an eating disorder, which led to his death in August last year.
Approximately 1.1 million people in Australia are affected by an eating disorder, according to the Butterfly Foundation.
While females make up the majority, one in three identify as male and they are four times more likely to go undiagnosed.
Ms Cappello hopes to raise awareness of this alarming statistic.
"Male eating disorders can present differently and they can be hidden a lot easier," she said.
"We talk about men's mental health a lot, which is awesome, but I really want to extend that to include eating disorders, as they are a mental health condition.
"It can be hard for people to understand it and talk about it because it doesn't fit that kind of stereotype for what an eating disorder looks like."
summary: Danielle Cappello said her brother Joe would go out of his way to help others. ( Supplied: Danielle Cappello )
author: Jake Kearnan

title: The NDIS's 'social licence' isn't just at risk of eroding – it's close to collapse
content:
Mark Butler has repeatedly invoked the NDIS's "social licence" over the last year. ( ABC News: Matt Roberts )
When federal NDIS Minister Mark Butler announced last August the government would be diverting a large cohort of children off the scheme to achieve further savings, he used two words that went somewhat under the radar.
He said reducing the scheme's growth wasn't just a question of budget sustainability, but also "social licence", with the NDIS approaching $50 billion a year and forecast to hit $100 billion in the next decade.
Since then, Butler and his colleagues have justified their generational cost-cutting changes by repeatedly referencing the potential for public support of the scheme to crumble.
They're right to suggest the reputation of the NDIS isn't what it used to be .
But they're wrong about another thing — the scheme's social licence isn't just at risk of coming under pressure, it's already close to collapse.
As reporters specialising in disability, people used to approach us in the street, excited about the opportunities the scheme provided for Australians to live, learn, work, or grow their business.
Now, people tell us they want it scaled way back or abolished entirely.
The NDIS is the country's fastest-growing government program. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
Resentment comes more easily in a cost-of-living crisis, and participants say they're feeling that more than ever from cash-strapped Australians, who expect their governments to eliminate wasteful spending.
More social media content creators are turning their attention to the NDIS, providing audiences with stunts or bite-sized takes on a complex topic.
Their posts often reference real issues that deserve more scrutiny — not least the scale of fraud in the scheme and whether it is being effectively regulated — but they don't always present the full picture.
Much of the recent commentary around the NDIS has been one-sided, writes Elizabeth Wright.
Recent discourse about a blowout of NDIS providers in south-western Sydney was debunked by independent fact-checking , but not before it was amplified by social media algorithms and picked up by commercial news in coverage that struck a chord.
Women with Disabilities Australia CEO Sophie Cusworth says the dominant narrative surrounding the NDIS now is that anyone associated with the scheme is rorting it, something that's left participants feeling unfairly framed as burdens.
summary: Mark Butler has repeatedly invoked the NDIS's "social licence" over the last year. ( ABC News: Matt Roberts )
author: Nas Campanella

title: Illegal and legal wildlife trade fuelling animal-to-human disease transmission, report finds
content:
The global wildlife trade has been called a hotspot for disease transmission in new research published this week. ( Getty Images: Artur Widak/NurPhoto )
The global wildlife trade — both legal and illegal — is fuelling animal-to-human disease transmission, according to new research published this week.
Of the 2,079 mammal species traded worldwide, 41 per cent shared at least one pathogen with humans.
In comparison, 6.4 per cent of non-traded mammal species shared human-infecting or "zoonotic" pathogens, according to the report.
The research, conducted by a team out of the University of Fribourg in Switzerland, also found traded mammals were about 1.5 times more likely to be hosts of zoonotic diseases.
The peer-reviewed report, published in the journal Science on Thursday , local time, noted inter species disease transmission was a "public health priority".
'"[But] the long-term impact of the wildlife trade in shaping pathogen exchange between humans and wild animals remains unclear."
That transmission, the report said, could happen across all stages of trade — including harvesting, breeding, transport, stockpiling, retail, consumption and companionship.
"For instance, a person buying three Finlayson's squirrels in a Laotian wildlife market has been estimated to have an 83 per cent chance of getting at least one leptospirosis-infected [animal]," it said.
Three separate datasets were used to analyse the legal and illegal wildlife trade, spanning from as early as 1975.
This was cross-referenced with the CLOVER database, the largest resource of mammal-pathogen associations to date.
Comparing the two sets of information, researchers tested whether mammals on the trade market were more likely to share pathogens with humans, compared to non-traded mammals.
The report looked at wildlife trading data from around the world. ( Supplied )
They also looked at whether illegal trade and live animal markets increased transmission risk, and how the amount of time in trade affected the level of disease shared.
The longer a wild mammal had been traded globally, according to the report, the more pathogens it shared with humans.
summary: The global wildlife trade has been called a hotspot for disease transmission in new research published this week. ( Getty Images: Artur Widak/NurPhoto )
author: Brianna Morris Grant

title: Fremantle convenience store crackdown to curb 'under the counter' illegal nicotine sales
content:
Illegal tobacco is being offered for sale in convenience stores across Perth. ( ABC News: Grace Burmas )
Fremantle's council has proposed changes to its laws that, if approved, would limit the number of convenience style stores that allegedly sell illegal cigarettes and vapes.
The port city already has more than a dozen such stores in the central business district, with more said to be opening up this week.
The proposed law changes need to go through several processes on the state level before they are approved.
A spike in the number of convenience-style stores allegedly selling illegal vapes and tobacco products in Fremantle's city centre has spurred the local council to try and change the laws to stop even more from opening.
A City of Fremantle report found there were at least 15 convenience stores operating in close proximity to each other, with the council's planning director alleging many were selling illegal products "under the counter".
Illegal tobacco is being offered for sale in convenience stores across Perth. ( ABC News: Grace Burmas )
Councillors on Wednesday night unanimously voted to beef up local planning laws, which if accepted by the state government would make it harder for those types of stores to open and operate.
But there are still bureaucratic hurdles to overcome before any action is taken.
Under the proposal, plans for the opening of a new convenience store will need to be advertised for public comment before requesting development approval from the council.
How did illegal tobacco take over what was once an attractive cafe and restaurant strip?
