Currently, the path to opening such stores in Fremantle was very straightforward — but Mayor Ben Lawver said residents have had enough.
He said between November 2024 and August 2025, the number of convenience stores in Fremantle had doubled.
"Some are right next door to each other," he told 102.5 ABC Perth.
Fremantle Mayor Ben Lawver says residents have had enough. ( ABC News: Grace Burmas )
summary: Illegal tobacco is being offered for sale in convenience stores across Perth. ( ABC News: Grace Burmas )
author: Ashleigh Davis

title: Melbourne Comedy Festival: Grace Jarvis on comedy as an autistic artform, chronic pain and working in a sex shop
content:
Rising star Grace Jarvis is performing Getting Dragged Backwards Through a Hedge at the Melbourne International Comedy Festival until April 19. ( Supplied )
Grace Jarvis is a comedian who works in a sex shop.
Yes, those jokes practically write themselves.
But look a little closer, and you'll see why Rolling Stone last year dubbed this rising Queensland comic a must-see at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival.
The self-described "autistic glamourpuss" ("it's just that I have autism and a lot of dresses, basically") is back for another run of performances at the 40th Melbourne International Comedy Festival (MICF).
The very first Melbourne International Comedy Festival launched 40 years ago, and while it's kept its scrappy charm, things look pretty different today.
Her latest offbeat autobiographical stand-up show, Getting Dragged Backwards Through a Hedge, is ostensibly about falling in love and the Edinburgh Fringe.
But there's also a darkness to it, with Jarvis offering the kinds of insights on life that can only come from a very specific set of lived experiences.
We caught up with the London-based comedian — who also hosts a podcast exploring her and others' experiences with pain — to find out how she got her start, what made her want to work in a sex shop, and why one element of her identity is noticeably absent from her work.
I was in Brisbane and it was the end of my first year of university, where I had not made a single friend.
I was following a bunch of comedians and writers I admired on Twitter and one of them was posting about doing an open mic near me, and I realised going was an option because I was 18 and allowed into bars.
It was 4:00pm on a Sunday in the back room of a bar in West End called The Bearded Lady and there were only other open mic comedians in the audience and I went: "I can do this."
Jarvis says her dad was "annoyed" she didn't let anyone see her first standup comedy performance because he'd watched her in every musical, play and choir she'd been in up to that point. ( Supplied )
I gigged in Brisbane until I finished university, then I moved to Melbourne and then there was a pandemic and now this is my sixth MICF, I believe.
There are very few comedians in Melbourne who are full-time outside of the festival because it's quite unsustainable to make a living; Australia still has quite a small arts industry.
summary: Rising star Grace Jarvis is performing Getting Dragged Backwards Through a Hedge at the Melbourne International Comedy Festival until April 19. ( Supplied )
author: Yasmin Jeffery

title: Darwin's 'dangerous' bus network will soon have officers armed with guns
content:
Passengers say safety on Darwin's bus network hasn't improved. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
Police public safety officers will start armed patrols on Darwin's bus network from June, with just four months of training under their belt.
The Transport Workers Union says antisocial behaviour on the NT's public transport is "the worst in the country".
NT government says some positive results have come from the interventions it's made to bring violence under control, while NT Police says guns will make buses safer.
In Darwin, catching a bus after dark is something Lismyati avoids at all costs.
As someone who doesn't drive, Lismyati relies heavily on her family or the local bus network to get around.
She has witnessed violent fights and public drunkenness — safety issues that have persisted for years and made Darwin's bus network one of the most dangerous in Australia.
Lismyati says catching buses after dark is too dangerous. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
As the conflict in the Middle East continues , more people than ever are turning to public transport as petrol prices climb — but in Darwin, that choice comes with its own unique cost.
In December last year, the NT government shut down Darwin's major bus terminals, altered routes and increased security in an effort to bring antisocial behaviour under control.
Months before that, buses became free of charge to reduce conflict.
The Country Liberal Party (CLP) government also announced officers would begin patrolling the bus network , armed with guns.
The NT government increased security to curb crime. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
Assistant NT Police Commissioner Brendan Muldoon said the armed officers — officially known as police public safety officers (PPSOs) — would create "safer public spaces".
The first group of 24 is expected to begin patrols in June, following four months of training.
summary: Passengers say safety on Darwin's bus network hasn't improved. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
author: Roxanne Fitzgerald

title: Fuel price hikes force families to reconsider travel for medical treatment
content:
Paige Edgar with her sons Wiley (holding) and Digby. ( Supplied: Paige Edgar )
Rising fuel costs are putting increasing pressure on regional health support services, with charities warning patients could miss out on vital care.
Some patients are cancelling appointments because they cannot afford to travel.
Dubbo MP Dugald Saunders is calling for a review into the NSW Isolated Patients Travel and Accommodation Assistance Scheme (IPTAAS).
Paige Edgar never imagined the hardest part of her one-year-old son's brain cancer diagnosis would be getting him to treatment.
Every month, her family makes a five-hour-long trip from Dubbo to Sydney so Wiley can receive specialist medical care not available locally, a journey now made even more difficult by rising fuel costs.
"We are able to give him his trial drug orally at home, but every four weeks we have to travel to Sydney to have him checked over," Ms Edgar said.
"The petrol [price] rises and closure of the [Great Western] Highway over the mountains has completely changed our plans as to how we would travel.
"We are now looking to seek flights rather than driving [to Sydney]."
Wiley (left) has been travelling to Sydney for treatment since he was six weeks old. ( Supplied: Paige Edgar )
Ms Edgar is supported by charity Country Hope, which helps families with a child diagnosed with cancer or other life-threatening illnesses.
"Country Hope is amazing, they cover the difference of what [the Isolated Patients Travel and Accommodation Assistance Scheme] covers," she said.
Her family is one of a growing number of regional families turning to charities for help as rising fuel prices force more people to ask for assistance to access essential medical care.
"Families are very stressed," Country Hope regional coordinator Donna Falconer said.
Little Wings flies children from regional NSW and Queensland to metropolitan centres for treatment. ( Supplied: Little Wings )
summary: Paige Edgar with her sons Wiley (holding) and Digby. ( Supplied: Paige Edgar )
author: Jen Mccutcheon

title: 'Ketamine Queen' jailed for 15 years over death of Friends star Matthew Perry
content:
Jasveen Sangha, who is also known as "Ketamine Queen", will spend 15 years behind bars for her connection to the death of Matthew Perry. ( Supplied: @jasveens on Facebook )
Jayvee Sangha, also known as the Ketamine Queen, will spend 15 years behind bars in connection to the fatal overdose of Friends star Matthew Perry in 2023.
She faced up to 65 years in prison for her five felony drug counts.
Perry was found by his live-in personal assistant floating face down and lifeless in a hot tub at his Los Angeles home on October 28, 2023. He was 54.
A drug dealer dubbed the "Ketamine Queen" has been sentenced to 15 years in prison in connection with the fatal overdose of Friends star Matthew Perry, including her role in supplying the dose of the powerful anaesthetic that killed the actor.
Jayvee Sangha, who admitted to running a "stash house" for illegal narcotics out of her home in the North Hollywood district of Los Angeles, pleaded guilty in September to five felony drug counts stemming from Perry's 2023 death.
Sangha, 42, a dual US-British citizen, had faced a sentence of up to 65 years in prison.
US District Judge Sherilyn Garnett imposed a 15-year sentence, harsher than the penalties received by any of her four co-defendants in the case, including two physicians.
Federal prosecutors had recommended a 15-year prison term. The defence had urged the judge to limit Sangha's sentence to time already served. Sangha has been incarcerated since August 2024.
Matthew Perry died in 2023 after taking ketamine and losing consciousness in a hot tub. ( Reuters: Phil McCarten, file photo )
Perry was found by his live-in personal assistant floating face down and lifeless in a hot tub at his Los Angeles home on October 28, 2023. He was 54.
An autopsy report concluded Perry died from the "acute effects of ketamine," which combined with other factors in causing the actor to lose consciousness and drown.
Ketamine is a short-acting but potent anaesthetic with hallucinogenic properties that is sometimes prescribed to treat depression and other psychological disorders. It has also gained popularity for abuse as an illicit party drug.
Perry had publicly acknowledged decades of substance abuse that overlapped with the height of his fame playing the sardonic but charming Chandler Bing on the 1990s hit NBC television comedy Friends.
Allegedly dodgy doctors teamed up with an LA dealer known as the "Ketamine Queen" and the Friends' star's personal assistant to use his addiction issues to "enrich themselves", US authorities say
summary: Jasveen Sangha, who is also known as "Ketamine Queen", will spend 15 years behind bars for her connection to the death of Matthew Perry. ( Supplied: @jasveens on Facebook )

title: St John Ambulance NT reaches 'critical system failure' for second time in a week, calls for more resources
content:
St John NT's services reached operational capacity again on Friday, with six life-threatening cases left outstanding for periods of up to 90 minutes. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
The Northern Territory's ambulance service, St John NT, has reached operational capacity twice in the space of one week.
The service says under-resourcing means its operations are at risk of reaching a critical failure point "all the time".
St John NT is currently in contract negotiations with the Northern Territory government, which staff are hoping could lead to an increase in resources.
The director of the Northern Territory's ambulance service says its operations are teetering on the brink of a "critical system failure" on an almost daily basis.
St John Ambulance NT, which is contracted by the government to provide ambulance services in the territory, called a second "code white" for the Darwin region in less than a week on Friday morning.
During a peak demand period, six life-threatening cases were left outstanding for periods of up to 90 minutes until an ambulance arrived.
Craig Garraway says St John NT doesn't have enough resources to keep up with demand. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
St John NT's director of ambulance services Craig Garraway said some non-life-threatening emergencies had been left waiting for more than six hours.
Mr Garraway said poor patient flows at Royal Darwin Hospital and a paramedic being stood down after being abused by a patient were among the contributing factors.
"There were a whole lot of issues just accumulating for about a two-hour period," he said.
Craig Garraway says St John NT is constantly on the verge of reaching operational capacity. ( ABC News: Peter Garnish )
The alert was raised just days after St John NT's first "operational capacity white" , during which 61 Triple Zero (000) calls were abandoned, and one life-threatening case was left waiting more than five hours for an ambulance.
The ABC rode along with St John Ambulance NT paramedics to hear the impact under-resourcing is having on the workforce, including stories of staff being assaulted on the job.
Mr Garraway acknowledged the government had provided St John NT with some additional resources following the first code white, but said it had not been enough to prevent the service from reaching operational capacity a second time.
summary: St John NT's services reached operational capacity again on Friday, with six life-threatening cases left outstanding for periods of up to 90 minutes. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
author: Joseph Hathaway Wilson

title: When is the best time to get your flu shot? Two infectious diseases experts explain
content:
Each year the flu vaccine is designed to protect against a mix of different virus strains, depending on which ones are circulating. ( Unsplash, CDC )
We usually have to wait until winter approaches before we see an increase in cases of influenza, or the flu. But we have already seen a lot of flu this year, with 25,000 cases reported from January to March — and that's only a fraction of actual case numbers.
Most people with the flu recover without treatment. But it can cause serious complications in older people, young children, pregnant women and those with underlying chronic diseases such as asthma or heart disease. Influenza kills around 3,500 Australians a year and lands 18,000 in hospital.
There were more deaths associated with influenza last year than any other year this century, according to the Australia Bureau of Statistics.
Vaccination is the best way to protect against flu and is recommended for everyone over the age of six months.
Flu vaccines are free for certain risk groups . Others can access them (usually for a fee) at pharmacies, GP clinics and local council clinics in some states and territories. Some employers also offer vaccinations for staff.
Influenza has been unpredictable since the COVID pandemic, with off-season circulation. There are also concerns that protection may lag at the end of winter. So when is the best time to get vaccinated?
Flu symptoms include a cough, sore throat, fever, body aches and fatigue.
Flu spreads from person to person via small respiratory droplets when you talk, cough and sneeze. It may also spread by touching a surface or object where infected droplets have landed, but this is less common.
So if you have flu-like symptoms , it's important to stay at home.
Flu spreads more in the winter months due to increased contact between people and time spent indoors. Some studies also suggest influenza viruses transmit better in the cold .
Typically, human flu cases are caused by four virus strains, A(H3N2), A(H1N1), B/Victoria, and B/Yamagata.
So far this year in Australia , almost 98 per cent of cases have been influenza A(H3N2), and the remaining have been influenza B/Victoria.
Theoretically, the best time to get the flu vaccine is about two weeks before flu cases start to rise. ( ABC News: Kym Agius )
In late 2025 , a new variant of the H3N2 strain known as subclade K or "super-K" emerged in the Northern Hemisphere winter.
summary: Each year the flu vaccine is designed to protect against a mix of different virus strains, depending on which ones are circulating. ( Unsplash, CDC )
author: Meru Sheel and Allen Cheng

title: Gary Woodland details PTSD struggles ahead of US Masters
content:
Gary Woodland has opened up about his mental health challenges ahead of the US Masters. ( Getty Images: Maddie Meyer )
Former US Open champion Gary Woodland has opened up about his mental health struggles during an interview before this week's US Masters.
Woodland won the PGA Tour's Houston Open last month, but admitted during one of his rounds he thought "people were trying to kill me".
Woodland is playing in this week's US Masters, which begins tomorrow.
Gary Woodland was playing the back nine at the Houston Open a couple of weeks ago, an event he would ultimately win to secure his return trip to the Masters, when the former US Open champion began to feel what he described as "hypervigilant".
The exact opposite, in fact: a powerful admission of Woodland's mental health struggles, and one he hopes will help others dealing with trauma, anxiety and post-traumatic stress disorder in their own lives.
The inner turmoil that Woodland feels, even at Augusta National, one of the most bucolic places in the world, has its roots in a scan that uncovered a lesion on his brain.
The American needed surgery, which involved cutting a baseball-sized hole in the side of his head. In September 2023, he wrote letters to his wife and kids in the event something went wrong, then had surgery to remove as much of it as possible.
Last month, Woodland revealed in an interview with the Golf Channel that, though successful, the surgery, located near his amygdala, the part of the brain that controls fear and anxiety, had left him with post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).
However, Woodland returned to the course in January 2024, slowly working his way back into form, making enough cuts to keep him motivated.
The following year, he finished second at the Houston Open, the same tournament he would win last month, when Woodland got through his Friday bout of hypervigilance and closed with a 3-under 67 on Sunday for a five-shot win over Nicolai Hojgaard.
Few knew that Woodland was still struggling, though.
Gary Woodland won the Houston Open last month, which earned him a place in this week's US Masters. ( AP: Ashley Landis )
He had become crippled by PTSD to the point that he would rush to bathrooms to break down in tears, and it always felt as if people were out to get him; one symptom of PTSD is a heightened state of sensory sensitivity, which causes the nervous system to continually stay in an on-guard state.
"I talked to (PGA) Tour security that night, and I told them what I was going through, and every time I looked up on the weekend, my security team was behind me," Woodland said of that Friday at the Houston Open.
summary: Gary Woodland has opened up about his mental health challenges ahead of the US Masters. ( Getty Images: Maddie Meyer )

title: Nursing union says Royal Darwin Hospital staff face violent threats, fear for safety
content:
Some staff have been assaulted while on the job at Royal Darwin Hospital. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
Nurses in the Northern Territory allege they are being attacked and threatened on a daily basis at Royal Darwin Hospital.
Their union says the hospital's emergency department is regularly trying to operate at 150 per cent of its capacity.
NT Health is promising to improve security measures for staff and says new wards, opening later this year, will ease emergency department pressures.
The head of the NT health department has rejected calls for a security review at Royal Darwin Hospital's busy emergency department (ED) following allegations it is running dangerously over capacity and that staff working there receive "daily" threats of violence.
Australian Nursing and Midwifery Federation (ANMF) secretary Heidi Crisp said nurses in the department "feel unsafe every day".
"On a daily basis they are receiving threats of violence and actual assaults that need multiple people to assist and that are leaving nurses traumatised," she said.
There's concerns the NT's health system, which is already strained, could face further challenges if staff safety isn't prioritised. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
Ms Crisp said one union member told her: "I have extreme concerns — and I am not the only one — that a worker is going to be stabbed within the department."
She said nurses had reported that police were often unable to attend incidents at the hospital, or were unable to do so quickly, while its private security guards were also sometimes slow to respond when nurses pressed the ED's duress alarms.
"It can take 15 minutes for the security staff to respond to the alarms and often the nurse team leader may not even be aware of that alarm," she said.
"So we are asking for that system to be changed so that nurse team leaders are alerted in real time every time a duress alarm is pressed."
Heidi Crisp says workers don't feel safe, with violence inside the hospital leaving some "traumatised". ( ABC News: Dane Hirst )
NT Health chief executive Chris Hosking told the ABC that the department took staff safety "seriously" and denied the hospital had "a systemic problem with slow response times".
"In my view those would be isolated occurrences," he said.
summary: Some staff have been assaulted while on the job at Royal Darwin Hospital. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
author: Jane Bardon

title: Swimming Australia's support dog experiment wins plenty of fans at Australian Open
content:
Shayna Jack had a superb swim after a pup talk on the pool deck. ( Supplied: Swimming Australia/Delly Carr )
Anyone who has ever been to a major swim meet will be familiar with the scene.
Hundreds of swimmers mill around in various states of stress, rushing from warm-up pool to change rooms, while coaches hover nearby, clipboards in hand and concern on their faces.
Eventually, they all converge on the staging area behind the main competition pool, corralled into their heat-based groups.
But as they get there, there's a distraction. Three of them, in fact.
Meet Clover, Elton and Evie — three four-legged ambassadors for Guide Dogs Queensland.
Clover, Elton and Evie have been busy at the Australian Open. ( ABC Sport: Simon Smale )
Poolside at the national championships might not be the most obvious location to find three retrievers — one golden, two black — walking around.
Yet their role at the Gold Coast Aquatic Centre could be groundbreaking and is further evidence of the lengths athletes will go to improve performance.
"I've been so excited about doing this," Linley Frame, Swimming Australia's national wellbeing and engagement manager told ABC Sport on the Gold Coast.
"Just to come in this morning and see the reaction with the athletes, coaches, staff that have gone in and just had a pat and had a moment and their shoulders just drop and they smile.
Mollie O'Callaghan has been an enthusiastic adopter of the guide dogs' support. ( Supplied: Swimming Australia/Delly Carr )
"It's really nice because we know in high-pressure environments, the more relaxed you can be, the better performance outcomes."
Frame, a former world champion swimmer and Barcelona 1992 Olympian, is no stranger to the pressures of competition.
And given the status of swimming in Australia, the athletes are under pressure like few others in any other individual sport.
summary: Shayna Jack had a superb swim after a pup talk on the pool deck. ( Supplied: Swimming Australia/Delly Carr )
author: Simon Smale

title: Sydney healthcare workers push for refugee Medicare access amid growing health concerns
content:
Medical workers have reported poor health outcomes for refugees without access to Medicare. ( Pexels: Alex Green )
Doctors at a Sydney health clinic have estimated one in three of the centre's refugee patients do not have access to Medicare.
Many refugees can access free health care before they are granted permanent residency in Australia, but others are ineligible due to visa conditions.
Healthcare workers say patients often present with serious symptoms preventable with early intervention.
A growing number of people seeking asylum in Australia are missing out on lifesaving preventative health care due to being ineligible for Medicare, a group of Sydney doctors and nurses say.
Many refugees can access free health care while waiting for permanent residency — a process which can take years — but some are deemed ineligible due to the nature of their visa.
For example, people who have a temporary protection visa can work and access Medicare for up to three years while they wait for permanent residency, while someone granted a bridging visa can face work and health care restrictions.
Others may initially be eligible for Medicare but later lose access while waiting for visa determination.
Raj, whose name has been changed for safety concerns, fled danger in his home country about 10 years ago and is yet to receive permanent residency in Australia.
Raj is waiting for permanent residency and does not have access to Medicare. ( AAP: Tracey Nearmy )
The conditions of his temporary visa mean he is ineligible for Medicare, alongside his wife and 10-year-old daughter, who was born in Australia.
Raj came into difficulty three years ago when he was hospitalised with severe chest pain and diagnosed with heart failure.
He was told he would require an automated implantable cardioverter defibrillator, which is a small device placed in the chest to detect and manage irregular heartbeats.
But without access to Medicare or private health insurance, Raj said he could not have the surgery.
After experiencing thoughts of self-harm, he was referred to the Asylum Seekers Centre in Sydney, which offers health care to refugees.
summary: Medical workers have reported poor health outcomes for refugees without access to Medicare. ( Pexels: Alex Green )
author: Phoebe Pin

title: Leg amputee forced to wait two months for rehabilitation services in Tamworth
content:
Phillip Norris waited months for rehabilitation services after his surgery. ( ABC New England: Brigitte Murphy )
After having his leg amputated, Phillip Norris spent almost two months in four different regional NSW hospitals awaiting rehabilitation services.
The New South Wales Rural Doctors Association says regional hospitals can drop to less than 50 per cent staffing during holiday periods.
The Royal Australasian College of Rehabilitation Medicine says there are many issues with attracting physicians to regional areas.
After taking his first steps in about two months, Phillip Norris said receiving a prosthetic leg was one of the greatest feelings.
"I stood up and went, 'This is just so good,'" he said.
The radio announcer from Inverell in NSW's New England region had his leg amputated in December.
He was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes 30 years ago and suffered painful ulcers and the loss of some toes before the decision was made to amputate his leg.
Phillip Norris was preparing to return to work in a wheelchair. ( supplied )
The 66‑year‑old said his treatment saw him in and out of hospital six times in four different locations, requiring him to travel more than 2,000 kilometres across the Hunter New England Local Health District.
Following surgery in Newcastle, Mr Norris was unable to get a place at his nearest accredited amputee rehabilitation clinic in the Tamworth Base Hospital.
"They don't take any patients over the Christmas period," Mr Norris said.
Hunter New England Health declined an interview with the ABC, but said in a statement that Tamworth Hospital ran on reduced staff over Christmas.
Instead, Mr Norris spent three weeks in Armidale Hospital, where he received physiotherapy and was measured for his prosthetic leg.
After that, he spent another three weeks in Inverell Hospital as the wait continued for a place at the Tamworth Rehabilitation Unit.
summary: Phillip Norris waited months for rehabilitation services after his surgery. ( ABC New England: Brigitte Murphy )
author: Peter Sanders

title: Perth parents found guilty of starving teenage daughter granted bail ahead of possible retrial
content:
The girl's parents will face court again in April for a hearing for a possible retrial. ( ABC News: David Weber )
A Perth court has granted bail to a man and woman who were found guilty of starving their teenage daughter, and set aside their convictions.
The man and woman had appealed the verdict of their jury trial in November 2024.
The parents are due to appear in court on April 24 for a hearing for a possible retrial.
WARNING: This story contains details that some readers may find distressing.
The Perth parents jailed for starving their teenage daughter to the point of severe malnutrition have been granted bail after successfully appealing their convictions.
The couple's daughter was admitted to Perth Children's Hospital in 2021, aged nearly 17 and weighing about 28 kilograms. Doctors had warned at the time that she would have died without urgent medical intervention.
The girl's parents enrolled her in ballet classes with dancers two to three years younger than she was, after lying about her age. ( Supplied: District Court )
The parents, who had lived with their daughter in Perth's affluent western suburbs, were each charged with two counts of engaging in conduct that caused their child to suffer, and were found guilty after a jury trial in November 2024.
The man and the woman had appealed the verdict , and on Tuesday, Western Australia's Court of Appeal set aside their convictions and granted them bail, each with a $50,000 personal undertaking and $50,000 surety.
The court has yet to publish the full reasons for its decision.
The now-young woman (centre) attended court when her parents were sentenced in January last year. ( ABC News: David Weber )
The man and woman are scheduled to appear in the District Court on April 24 for a hearing for a possible retrial.
In January 2025, the father was sentenced to more than six years in prison, and the mother was given five years, both with eligibility for parole.
During their trial, the parents were accused of deliberately starving their daughter over years to keep her physically and socially child-like.
summary: The girl's parents will face court again in April for a hearing for a possible retrial. ( ABC News: David Weber )
author: David Weber

title: Darwin midwife's $1.1 million NT government contract under review
content:
Claire Marks was barred from working unsupervised by the Nursing and Midwifery Board of Australia last November. ( ABC News: Marcus Kennedy )
A $1.1 million NT government contract awarded to Claire Marks, a midwife with conditions imposed on her registration, is being reviewed.
Despite that, and concerns raised by doctors, Midwives in Darwin — the company Ms Marks is director of — recently announced its team had expanded.
Australian Medical Association NT president John Zorbas says health staff remain concerned as the acute shortage of maternity services in the territory continues to impact families.
The Northern Territory government is reviewing a $1.1 million contract it awarded to a midwife barred from working without supervision, as doctors continue to raise concerns.
Claire Marks received the government funding last September to provide a private midwifery service, Midwives in Darwin, for a 12-month period.
It came after the collapse of private maternity care in Darwin , which has left the NT with critical gaps in services and forced women to either give birth at the public Royal Darwin Hospital or travel interstate.
A growing number of women in the Northern Territory are travelling interstate to give birth — or leaving the territory altogether — following the sudden closure of the only private hospital maternity ward. Now, the NT's last private obstetrician is closing her practice.
But the contract was signed while Ms Marks was under investigation by the Nursing and Midwifery Board of Australia — an investigation that concluded with the regulator imposing restrictions on her practice.
The Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency (AHPRA), which oversees the Nursing and Midwifery Board, has not disclosed the specific grounds for the restrictions, but said conditions are imposed on a practitioner's registration when a national board deems it "necessary to protect the public".
Under the restrictions, Ms Marks is required to engage an approved mentor to address her management of care and maternal observations — critical safety tools where delays or missed observations can mean the difference between early intervention and an emergency.
She is also barred from practising unsupervised.
NT Health Minister Steven Edgington last month declined to respond to questions about if and why the government was continuing to honour the contract.
However, a Department of Health spokesperson defended the procurement process as operating "strictly in accordance with NT government requirements" and said the department does not comment on "matters that are commercial in confidence".
The spokesperson however confirmed that the contract is now under review.
summary: Claire Marks was barred from working unsupervised by the Nursing and Midwifery Board of Australia last November. ( ABC News: Marcus Kennedy )
author: Roxanne Fitzgerald

title: SA authorities confirm Port Lincoln tuna deaths not caused by harmful algal bloom
content:
A boat carrying tuna to Port Lincoln passed through discoloured water earlier this week, resulting in thousands of fish deaths. ( Supplied: Des Woolford )
SA environmental authorities say a fish mortality event off the coast of Port Lincoln is unrelated to the state's harmful algal bloom, and that locally caught seafood is safe to eat.
A number of tuna, estimated to be in the "low thousands", were killed by a naturally occurring phytoplankton called Vicicitus globosus.
In what has been a difficult year for the industry, seafood businesses are hoping Easter demand for fish will help.
After reports of mass tuna deaths near Port Lincoln, scientists and the seafood industry have been quick to allay fears that the mortality event has any link to the state's toxic algal bloom or that locally caught produce is unsafe.
The Australian Southern Bluefin Tuna Industry Association reported a significant number of tuna deaths in March when a tow cage carrying the fish passed through discoloured water south of Port Lincoln on the Eyre Peninsula.
The number of deaths was estimated to be in the "low thousands".
It comes at a time of low demand for seafood from consumers concerned by the bloom , as fishers demand protection for their stock, as well as fuel supply disruptions, rising costs, and extreme weather events placing significant pressure on the industry.
The southern bluefin tuna industry in Port Lincoln is estimated to be worth upwards of $300 million. ( Supplied: Al McGlashen )
The recent Port Lincoln mortality event has stirred up those concerns and left some seafood producers nervous, despite findings from the South Australian Research and Development Institute (SARDI) that it was entirely unrelated to the harmful algal bloom.
SARDI has identified the culprit as Viciciclus globosus, a naturally occurring phytoplankton that is "not Karenia bloom related", executive director Mike Steer said.
Mike Steer says the tuna mortality event is not related to the algal bloom. ( ABC News: Daniel Taylor )
"[It's] very separate to the Karenia species, which everybody is aware of," he said.
"This looks like a different situation, not in line with the current harmful algal bloom and Karenia species, which is further up in the gulf."
Vicicitus globosus has been identified in South Australia before — Mr Steer said the last time it impacted the industry was "back in 1996" — and is a "feature of our marine ecosystem".
summary: A boat carrying tuna to Port Lincoln passed through discoloured water earlier this week, resulting in thousands of fish deaths. ( Supplied: Des Woolford )
author: Declan Durrant

title: NDIS provider registrations in spotlight as Labor eyes options to make budget savings
content:
NDIS reform is expected in the upcoming budget with the government already in talks with states and territories about options. ( ABC News: Luke Bowden )
Major providers are calling for stricter NDIS registration rules, arguing it would improve quality and reduce costs.
The federal government is weighing options to slow the scheme's growth, including eligibility and pricing changes.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has indicated Labor will outline its NDIS reform plan at the federal budget in May.
Stricter registration rules would weed out low-quality NDIS providers and result in better services for Australians and budget savings, major operators say.
Further reforms to the National Disability Insurance Scheme are expected to be a key feature of Labor's budget in May, with registered providers calling for tighter controls to stop unscrupulous businesses profiting from the $50 billion program.
The federal government is currently developing a plan to reduce the scheme's growth rate to 5 or 6 per cent a year, with Health and NDIS Minister Mark Butler promising to bring the disability community "with us" on any changes.
This includes weighing up the trade-offs of options like reducing the number of people on the NDIS or maintaining current participant levels at a lower cost.
It comes as new research has found the rate of autism diagnoses in Australia since the NDIS began has increased beyond what could be explained by a "catch-up" in awareness alone.
Ahead of the budget, there is a growing push from major providers to expand registration requirements across the NDIS.
Registered providers must meet formal government standards and have ongoing compliance rules, with greater oversight from the NDIS Quality and Safeguards Commission.
But the latest NDIS report for the December quarter shows 94 per cent of all providers, or almost 260,000 people, in Australia are unregistered.
They are able to provide services to the 26 per cent of NDIS participants who either fully or partly self-manage their plan, as well as the 68 per cent of participants who have their plans managed by a third party.
Providers must be registered with the NDIS Commission to deliver specialist disability accommodation, specialist behaviour support services and plan management service.
Labor is introducing mandatory registration for supported independent living accommodation providers from July 1.
summary: NDIS reform is expected in the upcoming budget with the government already in talks with states and territories about options. ( ABC News: Luke Bowden )
author: Clare Armstrong

title: Aboriginal leaders Naomi Mayers and Ann Weldon remembered as trailblazers after dying days apart
content:
Tributes are flowing for Naomi Mayers, who helped establish Aboriginal medical services across the country. ( ABC News: Timothy Ailwood )
The name and image of Naomi Mayers and Ann Weldon have been published with the permission of their families.
Naomi Mayers, also known as Aunty Nay, changed the landscape of Aboriginal healthcare across the country.
She is credited with turning self-determination into a model of care that is still used today by Aboriginal-controlled medical services from WA's Kimberley region to the bustling streets of Sydney.
Dr Mayers spent decades advocating for the rights of Aboriginal people. ( Suppleid: Tamara Bellear-Mayers )
The prominent health leader, activist and original member of The Sapphires singing group died aged 84 on Friday.
The Yorta Yorta woman was born in 1941 at Erambie Mission near Cowra, in the New South Wales central west region.
She became heavily involved in the Aboriginal rights movements of the 1960s in Melbourne with the Aborigines Advancement League.
At the age of 18, Dr Mayers worked as a nurse at the Royal Women's Hospital and Royal Children's Hospital in Melbourne.
She eventually moved and became one of the founding members of what is considered Australia's first Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Service, located in the Sydney suburb of Redfern.
Dr Mayers was prominent within the medical, legal and land rights spaces. ( Supplied: Tamara Bellear-Mayers )
She started as the Aboriginal Medical Service (AMS) Co-operative Limited's first administrator in 1972 and then became the CEO until 2017, spending more than 45 years with the organisation.
Current AMS Redfern CEO and Bundjalung woman LaVerne Bellear worked with Dr Mayers for more than a decade, which was initially set up because racism was rife within the unaffordable healthcare system.
Ms Bellear said she was "a visionary" who "touched many lives" by setting up community-controlled health services, which was a model of care she worked on with others across the country.
Ms Bellear [right] worked with Dr Mayers for more than a decade at AMS Redfern. ( Supplied: Tamara Bellear-Mayers )
summary: Tributes are flowing for Naomi Mayers, who helped establish Aboriginal medical services across the country. ( ABC News: Timothy Ailwood )
author: Shannon Corvo

title: Police investigate 'suspicious' death on Barkly Highway after spate of Easter road crashes in NT
content:
NT Police are investigating the incident. ( ABC News: Joseph Hathaway-Wilson )
Police are investigating the death of a woman in the Barkly region, which occurred over the Easter weekend and is being treated "as suspicious".
A 33-year-old man remains in custody, assisting police investigations, after he was arrested on Monday in relation to the incident.
The fatality is in addition to the five lives already lost on NT roads this year, with police appealing to the public for more information.
Police are investigating "a suspicious death" that occurred in the Northern Territory, on a remote stretch of highway, in the early hours of Easter Sunday.
About 5:50am, authorities "received a report a female had stopped breathing in the vicinity of Barkly Homestead".
As of Tuesday morning, the woman had yet to be formally identified and no charges had been laid.
The woman died in the Barkly region. ( Supplied: Barkly Homestead )
NT Police said she was on the Barkly Highway in the Avon Downs district when the "vehicle she was travelling in allegedly hit a kangaroo".
"The woman died at the scene and the death is being treated as suspicious," NT Police said in a statement on Monday.
"A 33-year-old male was arrested and conveyed to Tennant Creek where he has been assisting police with enquiries."
Investigations are ongoing and police are calling on the public for more information.
The Barkly Highway runs across the Northern Territory-Queensland border. ( ABC News: Gemma Ferguson )
Road trauma is a significant issue in the Northern Territory , and the Barkly region crash adds to the five lives lost on NT roads already this year.
St John Ambulance NT's Craig Garraway said crews were "flat-out" over the Easter long weekend, rushing eight people to hospital as a result of seven car crashes recorded across the territory.
summary: NT Police are investigating the incident. ( ABC News: Joseph Hathaway-Wilson )
author: Gemma Ferguson

title: WA man considering legal action after being sent home from hospital when he needed surgery
content:
Jack had multiple surgeries and lost a large chunk of his calf muscle as a result of compartment syndrome. ( ABC News: Daryna Zadvirna )
A Perth man is considering legal action after delays in diagnosis and treatment of his compartment syndrome by two hospitals.
An internal investigation has shown the man was sent home from Armadale Health Service and not diagnosed until he returned four days later.
East Metropolitan Health Service says the case has been the subject of a clinical review, and apologised for the distress the man suffered.
An Australian Navy candidate says he nearly lost his leg following delays in diagnosis and treatment of his emergency condition by two Perth hospitals.
Jack* was suffering from compartment syndrome — which occurs when excessive pressure builds up inside a muscle — when he presented at the Armadale Health Service emergency department with excruciating pain in August last year.
The condition can lead to permanent muscle and neurovascular damage or even amputation if not treated immediately.
An internal investigation showed the 21-year-old was sent home that night and not diagnosed until he returned to the hospital four days later.
It took a further 24 hours to complete the surgery he urgently required at Royal Perth Hospital.
Jack was not diagnosed with compartment syndrome until he returned to hospital four days after first going. ( ABC News: Daryna Zadvirna )
Jack has been unable to continue with his recruitment as a Navy submarine engineer and is now considering taking legal action.
Jack was running late to a soccer match and tripped up the stairs on the day he was injured.
He recalls being almost unable to walk when he got to the ED that Friday afternoon.
"There was numbness and a tingling in my toes, and then it felt like pressure was building up," he said.
He claimed the nurse who examined him was dismissive of his pain and made him feel like he was overreacting.
summary: Jack had multiple surgeries and lost a large chunk of his calf muscle as a result of compartment syndrome. ( ABC News: Daryna Zadvirna )
author: Daryna Zadvirna

title: Canberra paraplegic woman stranded in hospital for 19 months due to aged care bed block
content:
Mary Wansey says she's lost hope of ever finding a bed in an aged care facility. ( ABC News: Lily Nothling )
Mary Wansey has spent 19 bleak months stranded in a Canberra hospital room while she waits for a place in aged care.
"We've fallen into a black hole," she said.
"It just seems it doesn't matter what we do or where we apply."
In August 2024, while she was living on the New South Wales South Coast , a misdiagnosed tumour destroyed the 84-year-old's spinal cord at T4, causing paralysis from the armpits down.
Mary Wansey has spent 19 months in a hospital room while she waits for a place in aged care. ( ABC News: Lily Nothling )
"It just happened overnight — by morning, I was paralysed," she said.
"It was devastating, absolutely devastating."
The otherwise active, healthy and social woman who had carefully planned her twilight years was suddenly facing a very different future.
"Life in hospital every day is monotonous and it's sad," Mrs Wansey said.
People over 65 who acquire a disability aren't eligible for support from the National Disability Insurance Scheme.
Instead, they are supposed to be supported by the aged care sector.
Mary Wansey says life stuck in hospital is monotonous. ( ABC News: Lily Nothling )
Mrs Wansey's family has lost track of the number of applications they have lodged with aged care providers as they search for a local facility that will accept her.
"I can't understand it, because the cost of keeping me in the hospital, I think, is a lot more than it would cost to keep me in an aged care facility," she said.
summary: Mary Wansey says she's lost hope of ever finding a bed in an aged care facility. ( ABC News: Lily Nothling )
author: Lily Nothling

title: Feral animals, biosecurity and careers on agenda for SA Aboriginal Land and Sea Rangers
content:
Far west coast rangers photographing a little dunnart as part of their monitoring work. ( Supplied: Alinytjara Wilurara Landscape Board )
The first ever South Australian Aboriginal Land and Sea Ranger Conference has brought together more than 200 rangers from across the state.
There are now more than 29 ranger groups in the state as part of the federal government's Indigenous Rangers Program.
The rangers will continue to work to maintain Country and combat the shared problem of feral animals and plants.
When you bring more than 200 Aboriginal rangers together from mobs across nearly all of South Australia, you get a lot of laughter and a fair bit of good-natured teasing.
But also, an invaluable shared learning experience to inspire those taking on the job of caring for Country.
The first ever South Australian Aboriginal Land and Sea Ranger Conference took place in Hahndorf last month, organised jointly by the Alinytjara Wiluṟara Landscape Board and Daryle Clarke of the Ngarrindjeri Aboriginal Corporation's Yarluwar-Ruwe Rangers.
The conference was held in Hahndorf in the Adelaide Hills but brought together rangers from across the state. ( Supplied: Alinytjara Wilurara Landscape Board )
Many of the young rangers have three years or more of formal recognition and experience under their belts.
Others are just starting out in their careers and will be joined by more newcomers following the announcement late last year that the federal government will fund another 82 ranger projects across the country.
Currently there are 29 Indigenous ranger groups in South Australia, and Mr Clarke said the conference was a chance for them to network, and understand their shared challenges.
Kim Krebs is the general manager of the Alinytjara Wiluṟara Landscape Board.
The area the board covers stretches for more than 250,000 square kilometres; covering the Aṉangu Pitjantjatjara Yankunytjatjara (APY) lands, Maralinga Tjarutja, the far west coast, Yalata and "a little bit" of Antakirinja Matu-Yankunytjatjara country.
Daryle Clarke and Kim Krebs at the conference. ( ABC South East: Caroline Horn )
"If you think about the Stuart Highway up the middle (of South Australia) … more or less, we're the west of that," she said.
summary: Far west coast rangers photographing a little dunnart as part of their monitoring work. ( Supplied: Alinytjara Wilurara Landscape Board )
author: Caroline Horn

title: Hundreds of flying fox pups starving to death in wake of ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle
content:
Rescuers say baby flying foxes are falling out of trees, and many of them have died. ( ABC News: Tilda Colling )
Hundreds of dead and dying flying foxes are lining the streets in Katherine and Pine Creek, south of Darwin, after a cyclone swept across the NT last month.
Wildlife rescuers are trying to save as many of the native fruit bats as possible, though a bid for specialist care interstate has been knocked back due to biosecurity risks.
Veterinarians are concerned the "unprecedented" mass casualty event could have wider consequences for the environment, but NT government officials say the population is "highly likely to recover".
Wildlife rescuers are scrambling to try to save hundreds of flying fox pups after the Northern Territory's native fruit bat population suffered "mass casualties" in the wake of ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle.
Hundreds of dead and dying juvenile flying foxes have recently covered the streets of Katherine and Pine Creek, south of Darwin, after their parents abandoned them ahead of last month's extreme weather event passing over the Northern Territory.
In response, more than 20 wildlife workers and volunteers pulled together to save as many of the surviving pups as possible, completing almost daily rescue missions between Darwin and the small outback towns.
Kacey Farris says hearing the baby bats scream was devastating. ( ABC News: Tilda Colling )
"Hundreds are dead; they're falling around you while you're trying to rescue other ones, falling to the ground screaming like we just missed them," she said.
With the veterinary hospital's staff working long hours — covering their own travel costs and care expenses for the dozens of surviving bats — the Darwin team reached out to NT Parks and Wildlife for help.
But Ms Farris said the Country Liberal Party (CLP) government had declined the chance to offer any additional workers or funding, instead taking a few carcasses for autopsies to determine their cause of death.
Rescuers have managed to save dozens of the flying foxes, with one volunteer caring for up to 45 at a time. ( ABC News: Tilda Colling )
The local vets also applied for a permit to send surviving bats over the border for specialist care, with interstate volunteers ready to take in the pups, but the request was denied.
In a refusal notice seen by the ABC, an NT Parks and Wildlife representative said "the potential animal welfare and biosecurity risks" of transferring the bats across state lines outweighed "the benefits to a species that is abundant and not classified as threatened".
They said the NT's chief veterinary officer Rob Williams was investigating the high mortality rates and had not ruled out diseases — including lyssavirus — but, according to the letter, the deaths were likely attributable to natural causes.
summary: Rescuers say baby flying foxes are falling out of trees, and many of them have died. ( ABC News: Tilda Colling )
author: Tilda Colling

title: Dance classes providing connection, creativity and movement for older Australians
content:
Every Thursday morning in the main theatre of a north Queensland dance company, a community gathers.
Under the guidance of dance teachers, women over the age of 55 come together to move and connect with one another.
The dance class is designed to promote creativity and movement in 'experiential' ways.
Judy Taylor, 79, has been going to the Still Dancing classes for four years.
Judy Taylor, 79, came to dance five years ago. ( Supplied: Aaron Ashley, Dancenorth )
"At the beginning I had no idea that this would be a thing for me. I didn't know how to move and in fact I didn't like the way I moved," Ms Taylor said.
Andi Gillham, Dancenorth's community class coordinator, said she has seen the community grow from classes of half a dozen regulars to 30 people.
"It is basically just a slower-paced class for people that want to revisit dancing, or if they're injured, or just want a general experience in a dance environment," she said.
"We used to do a lot of the class seated, but it has moved into being a little bit more physical."
Dancenorth's Andi Gillham and Amber Haines say a beautiful community has evolved around the classes. ( ABC News: Baz Ruddick )
Unlike Ms Taylor, 84-year-old Peggy Robertson has been a dancer for most of her life, having studied ballet in her younger years.
The classes have allowed her to move outside the boundaries of traditional dance.
"There [in ballet] you have to concentrate very much on all your movements, here you can just follow your body," Ms Robertson said.
"Nobody judges you. It just makes you feel so good."
Amber Haines, the co-artistic director of the company, described the group as "open and explanatory".
summary: Every Thursday morning in the main theatre of a north Queensland dance company, a community gathers.
author: Baz Ruddick

title: Terminal diagnosis hasn't stopped the final exhibition for Joan Humble
content:
Joan Humble says she could "just sit in a chair and mope", but "where would that get me?" ( ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke )
Award-winning Tasmanian artist Joan Humble has a terminal illness, but is determined to keep painting as long as she can hold a brush.
Humble is known for her works in miniature, capturing vast landscapes in tiny form.
Humble's final exhibition is now on at a gallery outside Hobart.
After a lifetime of capturing Tasmanian landscapes and winning international recognition for her miniature works, artist Joan Humble is holding her final exhibition.
The 88-year-old has a terminal cancer diagnosis.
But she is determined to keep painting as long as she can still hold a brush.
"I want to make the most of the rest of my time. I've got five commissions to do, so I've got to keep going," Humble said.
In 2014, Joan Humble won the Golden Bowl, the highest international prize for miniature artwork. ( ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke )
"You can't think about your body at all when you're painting, you've got to concentrate on what colour you've got on your brush," she said.
Humble says painting helps to keep her alive. ( ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke )
Humble is known for her works in miniature, capturing vast vistas of wilderness in tiny works of art.
"When you are looking at a miniature, you're looking at the whole subject, so everything has to work," she said.
"Your eyes are not travelling around the painting; you can see exactly what's in front of you, so it's got to be right."
In 2014, Humble won the Golden Bowl, the highest international prize for miniature artwork.
summary: Joan Humble says she could "just sit in a chair and mope", but "where would that get me?" ( ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke )
author: Fiona Blackwood

title: New research shows regional Australians move on foot more in 'walkable' towns
content:
A new study shows regional Australians who live in walkable towns and centres walk more than those who do not. ( ABC News: Jean Bell )
New research shows regional Australians living in walkable towns walked 75 minutes more each week.
The study's authors say governments at all levels should prioritise building footpaths and improving pedestrian accessibility.
A Victorian mayor says councils need funding to help build footpaths due to rate caps.
How much do you walk each week, and would you walk more if the infrastructure in your town allowed?
New research from the Menzies Institute for Medical Research at the University of Tasmania found that regional Australians living in "walkable" towns walked 75 minutes more each week than those who did not have access to footpaths.
The Institute's senior author Verity Cleland said walkable towns had footpaths and pedestrian access that allowed residents to walk between home, work, public transport, and the shops with ease.
"Are you able to get around easily on foot or a bike to get to the places you need to go, or do you run into dead ends?" Professor Cleland said.
Aside from providing a health boost and combating preventable chronic diseases, including heart disease and type 2 diabetes, Professor Cleland said people would be able to save money on fuel if they had the option to walk more.
Verity Cleland says walkable towns have footpaths and pedestrian access that allow residents to walk between home, work, public transport, and the shops with ease. ( Supplied: Verity Cleland )
She said rural and regional communities needed safe and accessible footpaths, so that people were not forced to walk on roads, particularly for vulnerable members of a community, such as children and those with mobility issues.
Professor Cleland said government planning had traditionally prioritised roads and driving at the cost of developing footpaths and other infrastructure.
"In the 60s and 70s, it was very much a car-dominated mentality when we were planning and designing our towns," she said.
Warrnambool in south-west Victoria is among the regional centres with pedestrian crossings throughout the CBD. ( ABC News: Jean Bell )
She said a cultural shift was needed to see more people choose to move on foot.
summary: A new study shows regional Australians who live in walkable towns and centres walk more than those who do not. ( ABC News: Jean Bell )
author: Jean Bell

title: Alleged cocaine smugglers' three-month journey from Panama to Sydney ends in arrest
content:
The MV Raider was escorted into Sydney Harbour in March after leaving Panama in December. ( ABC News: Cordelia Brown )
It all started just before Christmas last year.
The MV Raider, a US-based merchant ship built in 1991 that had disappeared from shipping tracking websites in 2021, suddenly reappeared.
The boat was in Cristobal, a port town in Panama, Central America.
After being sold off to an "undisclosed buyer" four years earlier, the MV Raider was now registered under a new flag, the African nation of Togo.
It also had a new destination — Sydney, Australia.
According to the International Transport Workers' Federation, workers were told the MV Raider was to be delivered across the Pacific Ocean to its listed new owner, a Brisbane-based manufacturer called Rosenbauer.
The problem was Rosenbauer had no idea it was coming.
"We sell big fire trucks," a company representative told the ABC from their Brisbane office.
It is just one element in a three-month saga involving the MV Raider — a ship that traversed multiple Pacific countries and the Australian coast in an alleged plot to import cocaine to Australia.
Police have charged six crew members of the high-profile MV Raider vessel with drug smuggling.
According to the Australian Federal Police (AFP), in the four years the MV Raider disappeared it was being rebuilt by its "undisclosed owner" to incorporate secret smuggling tunnels, what the AFP called "smuggling hides".
On Monday, after almost three months at sea, six members of its crew were charged with drug trafficking , with police alleging the boat, at one stage, had almost six tonnes of cocaine aboard.
Experts say the journey itself shows the lengths alleged drug traffickers are going to bring product to Australia — and the amount of product being seized in Pacific waters — as cartels continue to target the cash cow that is Australia.
According to the International Transport Workers' Federation, an advertisement was put out on social media in December last year for crew to join the MV Raider for a 25-day journey to Australia.
summary: The MV Raider was escorted into Sydney Harbour in March after leaving Panama in December. ( ABC News: Cordelia Brown )
author: Lucy Cooper

title: FluMist nasal spray vaccine is available for use in Australia, but only some children will get it for free
content:
Children in Australia can now have a nasal spray vaccine for the flu rather than an injection. ( ABC News: Mark Leonardi )
Australia's 2026 peak flu season will be unlike any other.
For the first time, children and teens aged from two to 17 years old can have a nasal spray vaccine for influenza, rather than an injection.
The flu spray has been available overseas for years — the UK introduced it in 2013. But it was only recently approved for use in Australia because drug companies had not applied earlier.
Some states are offering the spray for free to certain age groups as part of their flu vaccination programs. It is also available to buy privately nationwide.
Last flu season, just one in four Australian children aged under five were vaccinated against influenza. For five to 15-year-olds, the figure was only one in seven, according to the National Centre for Immunisation Research and Surveillance.
Health authorities hope the nasal spray will help boost vaccination rates to prevent severe illness and reduce transmission.
Sold in Australia as FluMist, it's a needle-free vaccination against influenza.
Children are given one spray in each nostril.
Queensland's Chief Health Officer, Dr Marianne Gale, said the spray contains protection against the same flu strains that are in injectable vaccines.
FluMist is approved in Australia for children aged from two to 17 years old. ( ABC News: Mark Leonardi )
One difference is that FluMist is a live attenuated influenza vaccine.
"That means it contains tiny amounts of weakened influenza strain and that helps train the body's immune system to work out how to respond," Dr Gale said.
"It's important to note that cannot cause the flu and there have been no documented cases of influenza developing as a result of that particular vaccine."
Dr Gale said there was "extensive evidence" from overseas showing the nasal spray was as effective as injectable vaccines.
summary: Children in Australia can now have a nasal spray vaccine for the flu rather than an injection. ( ABC News: Mark Leonardi )
author: Emma Pollard

title: Thinking of changing your private health insurance? Ask these five questions
content:
Health insurance premiums rose on April 1. ( ABC News )
Private health insurance premiums rose on April 1 by an average of 4.4 per cent, the highest increase in nearly a decade.
It's another hit to the hip pocket for families struggling with rising petrol prices, interest rates and general inflation.
Some people may choose to drop their extras or mix and match their hospital and extras between insurers to try to get a better deal.
Others might elect to lose their hospital cover and rely on the public system. But be warned: there are tax implications and long-term penalties after age 31 if you want to get back into private health.
In some circumstances, downgrading to a more affordable policy might be an alternative, but there will be exclusions.
For those thinking about a change, we've compiled a list of questions to ask based on our series looking at some of the pitfalls patients are telling us they're experiencing.
While most consumers are aware of checking for waiting periods and excesses, these are some of the other complexities that are worth investigating as well.
Joint replacements, like knee and hip replacements, only legally have to be covered under expensive gold-level private health policies.
However, some insurers voluntarily offer them under silver plus policies.
As our stories reported, some patients mistakenly thought joint replacements on their policy only referred to elective replacements that could be needed from wear and tear.
Since 2020, 360,000 people have left gold health insurance policies. But some have been left out of pocket for surgeries they thought they were insured for.
As a result, they chose to downgrade from gold to save money and joint replacements were no longer covered.
Patients were under the belief they could simply upgrade later in life, with some failing to consider they might need a joint replacement in the case of an accident.
Orthopaedic surgeons tell us there also can be a fine line between a joint reconstruction, covered under cheaper policies, and joint replacements.
summary: Health insurance premiums rose on April 1. ( ABC News )
author: Alison Branley

title: Port Macquarie family travels overseas for unproven stem-cell treatment as experts urge caution
content:
When diagnosed, Reid Mowle was one of just 54 Australians living with septo-optic dysplasia with optic nerve hypoplasia. ( ABC Mid North Coast: Marina Trajkovich )
Parents turning to unproven stem cell therapies to treat rare diseases hope new treatments will one day be available in Australia.
Reid Mowle lives with a rare eye condition and his parents say they have seen improvements after travelling to Thailand for treatment.
Experts say many stem cell treatments offered internationally have not been clinically proven and there is still a long way to go before they are used in Australia.
When Sarah Hardy's son Reid was diagnosed with a rare eye condition in 2021 doctors told her "the best case scenario" was that her baby would be blind.
"We were always told Reid could be under an LED light and tell you if it was on or off and that would be as good as his eyesight got," Ms Hardy said.
The now five-year-old Reid, who lives with his family in Port Macquarie on the NSW Mid North Coast, has autism and was born with septo-optic dysplasia (SOD) with optic nerve hypoplasia.
The rare condition, which affected just 54 Australians when he was diagnosed, hindered development of his optic nerve.
Reid underwent stem cell therapy to help treat his rare eye condition. ( ABC Mid North Coast: Marina Trajkovich )
In 2023, after consulting with specialists and other families navigating the condition, Ms Hardy made the decision to take her then-two-year-old son to Thailand for stem cell therapy unavailable in Australia.
Stem cell treatments are strictly regulated in Australia and many therapies have not undergone clinical trials to prove they are safe and effective.
"[Doctors] said just to be open to new technologies and things coming out in the future, but obviously, we wanted to do as much as we could, so we started doing our own research," Ms Hardy said.
Ms Hardy hopes Reid will one day repeat the treatment she said was "life-changing" for their family. ( ABC Mid North Coast: Marina Trajkovich )
Ms Hardy said after several rounds of the procedure in Bangkok, she noticed a change in her son.
"We were laying in the bed and my husband had walked into the room and was just standing at the door, and Reid noticed someone was there and looked up and asked who was at the door," she said.
summary: When diagnosed, Reid Mowle was one of just 54 Australians living with septo-optic dysplasia with optic nerve hypoplasia. ( ABC Mid North Coast: Marina Trajkovich )
author: Marina Trajkovich

title: 'Rare parasitic fungus' behind outbreak of severe conjunctivitis in Top End wet season footballers
content:
The NT's Centre for Disease Control has identified Vittaforma corneaea as the cause of an outbreak of keratoconjunctivitis. ( Supplied: NTFL/Patch Clapp )
A rare water-borne parasite likely spread on Darwin's sodden wet season playing fields is behind an outbreak of severe conjunctivitis that has so far affected more than 100 people.
The Northern Territory Centre for Disease Control (CDC) has identified the microscopic parasitic fungus, vittaforma corneaea , as the cause of the recent spike in mystery eye infections linked to the NT Football League (NTFL) .
Aussie Rules players in Darwin with symptoms of conjunctivitis are being urged to contact health authorities amid an outbreak of unknown origin affecting dozens of players.
The CDC is telling members of the public to stay vigilant and take precautions at a number of Darwin playing fields identified as high risk.
An investigation was launched last month after teams in the NTFL raised the alarm about dozens of suspected conjunctivitis cases reported since the start of the wet season, with symptoms ranging from mild to severe.
On Wednesday, the CDC said the number of reported cases of keratoconjunctivitis linked to the NTFL had climbed above 100.
In March, a public health alert was issued over an increase in conjunctivitis cases among Darwin football clubs. ( Supplied: NTFL/Celina Whan )
Keratoconjunctivitis is similar to conjunctivitis but more severe, affecting the cornea as well as the lining of the eye and the mucus membrane covering it.
After "extensive" testing and work with a laboratory interstate, the CDC announced the results of its investigation on Wednesday, naming the microsporidia species, vittaforma corneaea , as the cause of the outbreak.
Microsporidia are tiny, single-cell parasites that are widespread in the environment and can infect both humans and animals, according to the US Centre of Disease Control .
They most commonly cause gastro-like sickness in people with compromised immune systems, with contaminated water or soil considered a major transmission route.
Vittaforma corneaea is a less common sub-type of microsporidia parasite — or as NT Health described it, a "rare", "specialised parasitic fungus" — in which the parasite's tiny spores infect the eye.
Eye specialists will often diagnose conjunctivitis by using a slit lamp to scrutinise a patient's eye. ( ABC News: David Weber )
NT Health has advised keratoconjunctivitis can cause a range of symptoms in and around the eyes.
summary: The NT's Centre for Disease Control has identified Vittaforma corneaea as the cause of an outbreak of keratoconjunctivitis. ( Supplied: NTFL/Patch Clapp )
author: Jacqueline Breen

title: Increasing ACL injuries partly attributed to early specialisation in sport
content:
Grace has injured her ACL three times. ( ABC: Saskia Mortarotti )
On a rainy Monday night at soccer training, Grace, 18, slipped and heard a crack.
"I instantly went to the floor and sort of knew that I'd torn it," she says.
Due to surgery, and the laborious rehab that follows, an ACL injury can keep an athlete sidelined for an entire year, sometimes longer.
"Especially through the middle, like the three-month mark, it didn't really seem like I would get to that stage where I'd play again," Grace says.
Oscar, 22, tore his first ACL at 18 years old and his second at 19. The rehab was gruelling.
Oscar says rehabilitation was difficult mentally and physically. ( ABC: Saskia Mortarotti )
"You think you're invincible until you're not," he says.
"You don't realise how much muscle mass you lose from surgery.
"Getting back into rehab, I sat on the leg extension machine crying because I physically, well, I mentally couldn't — I couldn't do it.
"It didn't matter how much weight it was, I'd just sit there and my brain wouldn't let me do it."
Ava, 16, is 11 months post-op, and is struggling to make her way back to footy.
Ava is trying to return to football after an ACL injury. ( ABC: Thomas Midena )
"I had problems fuelling my body properly, so instead of moving forward and gaining more muscle, I was actually losing it," she says.
"I'd just started running again and that was going really well and then recently I had a pre-stress fracture in my back — that set me back like a month — and now I'm experiencing shin pain.
summary: Grace has injured her ACL three times. ( ABC: Saskia Mortarotti )
author: Michelle Wakim

title: Albanese tries to get fuel message out as Chalmers's budget countdown begins
content:
Anthony Albanese had a plan and a message to send to Australians. ( ABC News: Callum Flinn )
Welcome back to your weekly federal politics update, where Courtney Gould gets you up to speed on the happenings from Parliament House.
When it comes to the early days of the pandemic, voters have long memories.
So as Anthony Albanese told people to go off and enjoy their Easter holiday, you can't blame them for drawing a link to Scott Morrison's declaration just before Australia shut down in March 2020.
The then-prime minister had just chaired a meeting of state and territory leaders, where they'd agreed to restrictions on non-essential gatherings to stop the spread of the virus. But Morrison said he was still going to the footy because the restrictions weren't kicking in until Monday.
"The fact that I would still be going on Saturday speaks not just to my passion for my beloved Sharks. It might be the last game I get to go to for a long time," he said. Morrison quickly backtracked.
Equally the prime minister wants you all to have a great long weekend. Spend your money during your holidays. But also, conserve fuel. And catch the bus.
Albanese is desperate for you not to compare the fuel crisis to the pandemic. This is a different crisis, he says. The government has stressed supplies have been secured until May and this week signed off on a national plan and offered relief through the halving of the fuel excise.
So when news of Albanese's prime-time takeover landed on Wednesday, there seemed to be a general understanding from voters that this could be significant. The last time he did so, the Queen had died. Morrison before him beamed into our homes to talk directly about the pandemic.
Reports the UK's Keir Starmer and US President Donald Trump were to deliver their own national addresses in the hours after Albanese only fevered speculation. The prime minister's repetition of messages he's been sending all week fell flat.
Albanese backed up his national address with a speech to the National Press Club. He was asked if he misread the moment. He doesn't think so.
"I'm taking every opportunity to put the facts out about where we're at and to show people that we do have a plan," he says.
Anthony Albanese wasn't bothered by the criticism of his national address. ( ABC News: Dan Sweetapple )
That didn't stop the memes which are clearly making fun of the prime minister. But hey, at least his office can tell themselves their message is being read. And they're probably not wrong. The memes have a better likelihood of reaching people who were watching the Secret Lives of Mormon Wives or doomscrolling on TikTok over the nightly news.
But one has to wonder if next time Albanese wants to delay the start time of Home and Away to directly talk to Australians, people won't believe that he actually has something to say
summary: Anthony Albanese had a plan and a message to send to Australians. ( ABC News: Callum Flinn )
author: Courtney Gould

title: COVID vaccine injury victims speak out about slow claims process and threats over complaints made to Services Australia
content:
Sandra Byron experienced serious medical complications from a COVID vaccine in 2021. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
The paperwork is overwhelming, stacked across Sandra Byron's kitchen table — hundreds of pages of medical reports, legal letters, and correspondence with the federal government.
It is the outcome of her adverse reaction to a COVID-19 vaccine, and the four-year battle with the government's vaccine compensation scheme that followed — a process Ms Byron and her husband have described as "absolute torture".
"There are still hundreds of us left, apparently, facing this with no seeming accountability," Ms Byron told 7.30.
She suffered medical complications following an AstraZeneca vaccination in 2021.
"My hands have been rated as functionally useless," she said.
Doctors diagnosed Sandra with Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Capillary Leak Syndrome. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
Doctors confirmed the vaccine triggered Guillain-Barré Syndrome, an autoimmune disorder that attacks peripheral nerves, and Capillary Leak Syndrome, a rare but serious disorder impacting blood vessels.
Both are recognised — but rare — side effects of the AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine.
In Australia’s initial vaccine rollout in 2021, Guillain-Barré Syndrome was reported at a rate of around one in 100,000 people following vaccination, while capillary leak syndrome was considered extremely rare.
Ms Byron says she supports vaccination but was "just very unlucky to be very catastrophically vaccine-injured".
Over 74 million vaccine doses were administered nationally during the COVID pandemic, and there were 23,080 reports of 'serious' adverse events following immunisation.
To support the vaccine rollout, the federal government set up a compensation scheme. ( ABC News: Steve Cavenagh )
"My brain doesn't work properly; my speech comes and goes, so I do a lot of nodding because it's easier than talking," Ms Byron said.
But Ms Byron says that, as "horrific" as the complications have been, it's no longer the worst part.
summary: Sandra Byron experienced serious medical complications from a COVID vaccine in 2021. ( ABC News: Jerry Rickard )
author: Tom Hartley

title: More SA general practices charging extra administration fees to cover payroll tax
content:
Some GPs that do not bulk bill have been charging patients administration fees on top of consult fees. ( Adobe Stock )
An increasing number of private general practices in South Australia are introducing new "administration fees" to cover rising operating costs, blaming payroll tax changes for the additional cost to patients.
The levy is typically $5 to $10 and is billed as a separate transaction to consult fees in mixed billing or private billing practices.
One mixed billing practice wrote on its website that it had no choice but to introduce a "$5 administration levy on all private consultations" after trying to absorb the costs of "government-mandated payroll tax changes".
A group with more than a dozen practices across Greater Adelaide is charging a $6.60 admin fee in response to the payroll tax, which it said online followed an "incorrect and inappropriate classification of the relationship a practice has with a practitioner".
Another health care provider in Adelaide's East also said it had lifted its fees in response to rising operational costs, but singled out the SA government's payroll tax as being a key factor, despite their doctors operating with "significant independence".
National uncertainty around payroll tax for medical practitioners began in 2023 following a NSW Supreme Court ruling finding that payments to contracted professionals under the standard service fee were taxable as wages.
One in two Australians missed out on health care they needed last year mainly because they could not afford it, according to a new report by the peak healthcare consumers group.
The then-SA treasurer gave the state's health care providers an amnesty for paying payroll tax relating to contracted GPs, medical specialists and dentists in 2023, and made them retrospectively exempt for undeclared wages from July 1, 2019 to June 30, 2024.
But they had to be registered for payroll tax from July 1 2024, after which any exemption to payroll tax ended.
Employee and contracted GPs that bulk-billed were made permanently exempt as an incentive .
