The changes followed what the state government described as a past "lack of awareness or misinterpretation" about GP tax obligations under the Payroll Tax Act 2009.
Health professionals warn that higher GP costs can lead more people to present at hospital. ( ABC News: Jake Grant )
Australian Medical Association SA president Peter Subramaniam said general practices were being hit with rising costs like all businesses, but if they could not absorb them it could result in barriers to primary health care.
"A patient who doesn't go and see the doctor or the podiatrist or the allied health person because of what appears to be a small increase to out-of-pocket costs — if they end up in hospital, that's a large downstream cost," he said.
summary: Some GPs that do not bulk bill have been charging patients administration fees on top of consult fees. ( Adobe Stock )
author: Malcolm Sutton

title: No Easter opening for WA's landmark Gloucester Tree climb with rebuild delayed
content:
The climb has attracted thrillseekers from all over the globe. ( ABC Open: Ivan Gibbs )
Southern WA's landmark Gloucester Tree climb will not reopen in time for Easter.
The attraction, which previously saw visitors climb 61 metres to the top of a former fire lookout, has been closed since late 2023.
The Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions says safety improvements and other work have taken longer than expected, and is now targeting a June re-opening.
Hopes that one of southern Western Australia's top tourist attractions could reopen in time for the Easter long weekend have been dashed, with safety and structural works ongoing.
For decades, the 61-metre climb up the Gloucester Tree — without a harness or supervision — along with the neighbouring Dave Evans Bicentennial Tree, served as a major visitor drawcard for Pemberton, around 325 kilometres south of Perth, and surrounding communities.
The combination of structural issues and safety concerns saw WA authorities close the climb in 2023, eventually flagging a redeveloped ladder and climb of 37m up the native Karri tree.
The climb was closed in 2023 due to safety concerns. ( Supplied: DBCA )
But while work on the Gloucester Tree began last year, the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions confirmed the climb would not reopen in time for the expected rush of long weekend travellers.
"Work to re-establish the climb and viewing platform at the Gloucester Tree has taken longer than anticipated," a DBCA spokesperson said.
"Installing new infrastructure onto a living tree is a highly complex task, and ensuring the final experience is safe for visitors remains DBCA's highest priority."
The delay has disappointed local tourism operators. ( Supplied: Eli Sutherland )
The spokesperson said arborist advice and additional surface work for built components had extended the project timeline.
"DBCA appreciates the public's patience and looks forward to welcoming visitors back to the Gloucester Tree soon," the spokesperson said.
Pemberton Visitor Centre chair Mark Hudson said the status of the climb was one of the first questions asked by people arriving in town.
summary: The climb has attracted thrillseekers from all over the globe. ( ABC Open: Ivan Gibbs )
author: Chela Williams

title: Outback GPs warn delays in patient data across services put lives at risk
content:
Outback GPs say important patient information is taking weeks and sometimes months to reach treating doctors. ( ABC News: Lee Robinson )
Outback GPs warn that slow and inconsistent access to hospital and specialist records are leaving clinicians in the dark and patients exposed to preventable harm.
Discharge summaries and specialist notes help GPs understand a patient's condition, treatment and next steps.
Queensland Health say additional resources are being rolled out across the state to address slow information exchange.
An outback doctor says GPs are being forced to wait more than a month in some cases for critical patient information, putting lives at risk.
Although patient histories and medication notes are now digital, most medical information is managed by each region's hospital and health service and often hidden to other doctors.
Gidgee Healing chief executive Dr Manjit Sekhon said doctors have been left waiting for essential clinical handovers and denied access to basic patient information due to privacy barriers.
Manjit Sekhon believes doctors are flying blind and patients are at risk under the current information sharing systems. ( ABC North West Qld: Tim Little )
"There's quite often a delay in receiving clinical correspondence. I'm talking a few weeks, over a month at times," Dr Sekhon said.
"You're hiding patients' data from health care professionals.
"It puts patients at risk and just leads to unnecessary interventions."
Dr Sekhon said GPs rely on discharge summaries and specialist notes to understand a patient's condition, treatment and next steps.
But in regional areas that information often comes too late to treat the patient while they are in the clinic.
The experienced GP believed local hospital health services did exceptional work but were hamstrung by Queensland Health systems.
"We've got no visibility. We rely on receiving that letter [discharge summary] to get any information out on the patient," Dr Sekhon said.
summary: Outback GPs say important patient information is taking weeks and sometimes months to reach treating doctors. ( ABC News: Lee Robinson )
author: Tim Little

title: Parents demand action after falling concrete closes entire floor of Collingwood College
content:
Parents are frustrated by delayed building repairs at Collingwood College. ( ABC News )
A Melbourne school has closed an entire floor of its main building after concrete fell through the ceiling of a science classroom during the summer holidays.
Parents, students and the local MP have written to the state government to demand immediate repair work after years of issues with the roof.
The Department of Education says it is assessing the condition of the building to work out how to address the issue.
A Melbourne high school is sending students to another campus for some classes after concrete fell from a water-damaged roof and forced the closure of an entire floor in its main building.
Concrete chunks split off and fell from the underside of the Collingwood College's terrace roof, smashing through the ceiling, during the January school holidays.
A piece of concrete that fell from the roof of Collingwood College. ( Supplied )
The frustrated school council told the ABC the incident — which it feared could have seriously injured students had they been in the classroom at the time — followed long-term issues with water leaks.
Parent and school council vice president Hannah Marshall said students had already been dealing with "scaffolding everywhere" for half of last year as work to fix the roof was underway.
"The work was happening, so we endured," she said, adding that falling chunks of concrete were the final straw.
Parent Hannah Marshall says falling bits of concrete were the last straw for the school community. ( ABC News: Darryl Torpy )
The indefinite closure of the school building's second floor is also having an effect on students.
Year 10 student Sam Rankin is one of 65 students the department has arranged to send to a more junior campus for science classes as an interim measure.
"All of our work has had to be paper-based because we can't run experiments," Sam said.
Greens MP for Richmond Gabrielle de Vietri said concrete was falling from the roof in "chunks large enough to seriously injure or even kill a student".
summary: Parents are frustrated by delayed building repairs at Collingwood College. ( ABC News )
author: Shannon Schubert

title: Anthony Albanese's national address contained reassurance on the fuel crisis — and a warning shot
content:
The prime minister's address came with a reassuring message and a warning shot. ( Supplied )
Anthony Albanese used his address to the nation — broadcast across every television and radio network in the country — to send one unambiguous message to Australians.
The message was this: his government has your back and will shield you in every possible way it can from the war in the Middle East and the economic tsunami it is causing.
But it was also a warning shot, reckoning with every single Australian that the crisis will lead to some bleak economic months ahead.
The prime minister addressed the nation in a live broadcast tonight, promising: "We will do everything we can to protect Australia from the worst of it."
But he also used the address to lean into a different strategy to the one used during COVID, which has left a negative sting on Australians who are tired of heavy-handed government interventions.
The key emphasis here was choice. The prime minister is asking, not telling, Australians to change the way they behave in the coming weeks. It is a request, an appeal to people's best instincts, to preserve our fuel supplies as much as we can for those who need it most.
Now, it's the Australian way that people want to do their bit — and there are simple ways that you can. You should go about your business and your life, as normal.
Enjoy your Easter. If you're hitting the road, don't take more fuel than you need — just fill up like you normally would.
Think of others in your community, in the bush and in critical industries.
But it is the part of the address where the PM talked about the weeks after the Easter break that sends the strongest message about the real pain point of this crisis, expected to last well beyond the school holidays.
The economic shocks caused by this war will be with us for months.
And over coming weeks, if you can switch to catching the train or bus or tram to work, do so.
That builds our reserves and it saves fuel for people who have no choice but to drive.
Farmers and miners and tradies who need diesel every single day.
summary: The prime minister's address came with a reassuring message and a warning shot. ( Supplied )
author: Patricia Karvelas

title: Algal bloom tracks further north up the Yorke Peninsula, sparking concern for stingrays
content:
Point Turton started feeling the effects of the harmful algal bloom late January. ( ABC North and West SA: Isabella Kelly )
Moonta Bay and Port Hughes on the Spencer Gulf have seen the impacts of SA's harmful algal bloom for the first time.
The detection comes after dozens of stingrays were seen swimming in shallow waters at Moonta.
There are hopes the area's oceanography will help the bloom dissipate quickly.
Evidence of South Australia's harmful algal bloom has been detected in previously untouched parts of the Spencer Gulf days after rays were seen gathering in shallow waters.
Marine scientist and co-founder of the Great Southern Reef Foundation Stefan Andrews said he first noticed indicators of the bloom at Port Hughes, about 70 kilometres south of Port Broughton, on Sunday.
Stefan Andrews has been diving to investigate the impacts of algal bloom. ( Supplied )
"I've been in the water very regularly there over the last couple of weeks, ever since Point Turton and then Port Victoria got hit," he told ABC North and West SA's Breakfast program .
"You could sort of predict that it might be heading further north up there, but I was happy to see that things were holding on pretty well up until the weekend."
Mr Andrews saw what he called "initial signs of the bloom".
While parts of the state celebrate what could be the end of a devastating algal bloom, nearby communities are seeing the effects for the first time.
"The sponges on the [jetty] pylons start falling apart," he said.
"But then, strikingly, when you do move around under water a bit you do start seeing a few dead things.
"It's the octopus and squid that are some of the most sensitive species to the bloom and I saw at least a dozen octopus through the length of the jetty underwater there that had just recently died.
"From what I've seen in previous areas, once you get those first signs, then things will kind of cascade."
summary: Point Turton started feeling the effects of the harmful algal bloom late January. ( ABC North and West SA: Isabella Kelly )
author: Tom Mann

title: Internal memos show NT government warned of 'significant risks' in watch houses
content:
Internal documents seen by the ABC show executives from the NT Department of Corrections were warned of "significant" health and safety risks inside police watch houses during the height of the territory's prison crisis.
The documents detail several incidents, including a prisoner who suffered vision loss as a result of not receiving essential medication.
The NT government says watch house numbers have dropped significantly, but legal advocates say the issues surrounding access to health care are ongoing.
A senior Northern Territory Health department official sounded the alarm about "significant clinical and reputational risks" in Top End watch houses during the height of the territory's 2024 prison crisis , internal memos seen by the ABC have revealed.
WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised this article contains the name of an Indigenous person who has died, used with the permission of her family.
The memos — marked "urgent" — warned NT Corrections executives about overcrowding and medication management issues, while showing a significant proportion of prisoners in the Palmerston watch house, outside Darwin, were not reviewed on arrival, meaning their clinical health status was "unknown".
The documents reveal a woman attempted suicide in the Darwin Correctional Centre in Holtze. ( ABC News: Gemma Ferguson )
Several incidents are detailed in the memos, including a prisoner who experienced vision loss after they did not receive medically prescribed eye drops and another person with chest pain who could not speak English and was unable to be identified by health staff.
The documents also flagged "difficulty providing emergency care or identifying individuals in need of urgent treatment" at the Darwin Correctional Centre, which was also experiencing significant overcrowding .
They describe a "near miss" at the prison in Holtze, on Darwin's outskirts, in which a female prisoner tried to take her own life .
"An initial review indicated growing overcrowding, medication management and screening as salient factors in her attempted suicide," the document reads.
An investigation report by Acting NT Ombudsman Bronwyn Haack in November last year laid bare the "terrible" conditions inside police watch houses during the period the memos were sent.
Internal memos show a senior NT Health official raised the alarm over the safety of prisoners in Top End watch houses. ( ABC News: Michael Donnelly )
At the time, the number of prisoners in watch houses — typically used by police to manage new arrests — was ballooning amid a crime crackdown by the NT's newly-elected Country Liberal Party (CLP) government .
"Some complainants alleged [prisoners] were unable to see a nurse and others complained that they were unable to access their medications," Ms Haack wrote.
summary: Internal documents seen by the ABC show executives from the NT Department of Corrections were warned of "significant" health and safety risks inside police watch houses during the height of the territory's prison crisis.
author: Olivana Lathouris

title: Thermal imaging and night-time shooting key to Kangaroo Island corella control, authority says
content:
Little corellas mainly eat grains and seeds, but will also eat bulbs and fruit. ( ABC South East SA: Caroline Horn )
The Kangaroo Island Landscape Board says a trial of night-time shooting has shown corella populations can be managed humanely and effectively.
More than 2,600 birds were shot over the past two summers in an attempt to reduce the species' overall population.
The board says long-term little corella management would require co-ordination and resourcing across multiple agencies.
A trial involving skilled marksmen and thermal technology has demonstrated little corella numbers can be reduced both humanely and effectively on Kangaroo Island off the South Australian coast, according to a government environmental body.
The damage done by flocks of little corellas is a common concern for councils in both regional and metropolitan SA, with many deploying gas guns, spotlights and other methods of disruption to try and move the birds from urban areas.
The Local Government Association of South Australia (LGASA) and the South Australian Primary Industries Department (PIRSA) have also jointly funded a $95,000 research effort to develop a new bait for "pest birds" in the grain industry.
Kangaroo Island residents say their children are at risk of disease from the amount of little corella poo splashing across play equipment and school lunch areas.
The Kangaroo Island Landscape Board general manager Will Durack said the trial undertaken by the board had provided evidence and data that night-time shooting could be both humane and effective if scaled to the size of the population of little corellas.
While it was not a legislative requirement for the landscape board to manage little corella numbers, Mr Durack said the organisation decided to undertake the trial because of the impact large flocks of the birds were having on the community, including health concerns at the local school triggered by bird droppings.
Little corellas are intelligent, live in large, noisy flocks and mate for life. ( Supplied: Kangaroo Island Landscape Board )
The increasing little corella population has also impacted the island's ecology and farming industry.
"Over two summer seasons, we've taken out 2,640 birds … we have the data on it to feel really confident [the method] is both humane and effective."
The control work was carried out between midnight and 4am when the corellas were roosting and "effectively asleep".
After undertaking a full survey of the area, a marksman used a thermal scope and noise suppression to remove the birds, one by one.
summary: Little corellas mainly eat grains and seeds, but will also eat bulbs and fruit. ( ABC South East SA: Caroline Horn )
author: Caroline Horn

title: Qld's hospitals tackle health of patients and planet in emission reduction efforts
content:
All health services across Queensland are implementing sustainability measures to tackle their contribution to climate change. ( ABC News )
A focus on environmental sustainability by Queensland's public healthcare system has birthed innovative programs aimed to reduce the sectors carbon emissions.
The state's public healthcare system contributes to nearly 50 per cent of Queensland government emissions.
Healthcare services across the country look to reduce their impact on climate change, with new healthcare safety standards being considered.
Humanity is facing what is being described as its greatest health challenge, and those charged with keeping us healthy are grappling with their own contribution to the problem.
The World Health Organisation (WHO) lists climate change as the single biggest health threat facing humanity, responsible for the deaths of 13 million people annually.
In Australia, the healthcare sector accounts for 7 per cent of the nation's total emissions and, in Queensland, nearly 50 per cent of government emissions are from the public health system, primarily driven by electricity consumption.
It is why all 16 of the state's public health services are implementing sustainability measures, from procurement to design, recycling and energy reduction.
Children's Health Queensland is implementing environmentally sustainable measures. ( Supplied: Children's Health Queensland )
Children's Health Queensland (CHQ) has been slowly introducing environmentally sustainable initiatives and has just published its third sustainability plan.
Sustainability consultant Renae McBrien said it was showing the next generation that things could change.
"We're trying to map sustainability, not be a burden on the system, but an opportunity," she said.
"We're showing Queensland that you can actually save money and generate money."
It is a promising step forward in an industry known for its consumption of electricity and water and reliance on single-use plastics — in fact, if the global health industry were a country, it would be the fifth-largest emitter on the planet.
Predicted changes for hazards in Australia from climate change. ( Supplied: Australian Government National Health and Climate Strategy )
summary: All health services across Queensland are implementing sustainability measures to tackle their contribution to climate change. ( ABC News )
author: Nikki Sorbello

title: Free dental clinic helps Adelaide residents access care in cost-of-living crisis
content:
Lawford Brown took two years to receive dental treatment. ( ABC News: Luke Pike )
Dental care often takes the back seat for vulnerable people who are struggling with the cost of living.
Demand is high for an Adelaide dental program that offers free services to patients who have experienced homelessness or who have trouble accessing conventional care.
The clinic hopes to see an increase in dental funding nationwide for vulnerable people.
After a motorbike accident in the 90s, musician Lawford Brown relied on a dental plate to hold in two false teeth.
She said that plate broke in 2022 after one of the wires got snagged on her cardigan; from then on, she found it tough to eat, speak and perform — and never smiled.
Despite this impact, it took her two years to get dental treatment.
Ms Brown said she was unable to afford it on her disability pension, and the expense was her last priority when she faced difficulties with housing.
"Dental, unfortunately, goes to the back seat … you have to have food," she said.
Lawford Brown found it difficult to eat or speak when her dental plate broke. ( ABC News )
Then Ms Brown found her smile at the Community Outreach Dental Program at Adelaide University.
The dental school has been providing free services to vulnerable patients since 2011, including those who have experienced homelessness or who have trouble accessing conventional care.
"The reality is when you've got a lot going on in your life … oral health really does slip down in terms of the priorities," said Poppy Anastassiadis, who leads the program.
Dr Anastassiadis said while patients might be aware of a dental issue, their life circumstances could see them put off a trip to the dentist, often causing minor problems to become severe.
"In some cases, the treatment options are reduced because of that delayed presentation and increased complexity and extent of disease," she said.
summary: Lawford Brown took two years to receive dental treatment. ( ABC News: Luke Pike )
author: Aiden Mcnamara

title: 80,000 Australian kids aged five and under not up to date with vaccinations
content:
Naomi Steenson doesn't want to see other parents go through what she did with her son Blake. ( ABC News: Steve Johns )
New immunisation data shows childhood vaccination coverage declined for a fifth year in 2025.
More than 80,000 Australian children aged five and under were not fully up to date with their vaccinations.
Experts warn the decline in coverage will see more severe illness and death from preventable diseases.
Naomi Steenson should be celebrating her son Blake's 29th birthday this year.
But she says it feels like little time has passed since she last held baby Blake in her arms.
"I can relive it like yesterday," she said.
As a newborn in 1997, Blake contracted whooping cough and died after a six-week battle in intensive care.
"You go from a very healthy child to virtually a baby that's blue and just gasping for breath. There was just no way of coming back," she said.
Blake was too young to be immunised, and a vaccine was not yet available for pregnant women in Australia to protect their newborns.
Ms Steenson said no parent should have to endure the heartache of losing a baby.
Vaccine experts are urgently sharing the same message as new data shared exclusively with the ABC shows more than 80,000 children aged five and under were not up to date with their immunisations by the end of last year.
New data shows tens of thousands of children aged five and under aren't fully immunised. ( ABC News: Billy Cooper )
The National Centre for Immunisation Research and Surveillance (NCIRS) has analysed 2025 vaccination coverage and found the proportion of one-year-olds up to date with vaccinations was at its lowest point in 12 years.
Overall, childhood vaccination coverage at the one, two and five-year milestones declined for the fifth year in a row.
summary: Naomi Steenson doesn't want to see other parents go through what she did with her son Blake. ( ABC News: Steve Johns )
author: Rachel Carbonell

title: Inquest into Mackay grandmother's death hears of 'shortcomings' in private hospital's after-hours staffing
content:
Robyn Beale died in 2021 at Mater Private Hospital Mackay after a complication arose following surgery. ( ABC Tropical North: Liam O'Connell )
Mackay woman Robyn Beale died on August 3, 2021, following thyroid surgery at Mater Private Hospital Mackay.
The Queensland coroner criticised the hospital for "systemic shortcomings" but found Ms Beale would have died from a bleeding complication regardless of the facility she was in.
The hospital says it has made a number of changes to improve monitoring after surgeries.
A coroner has found a 78-year-old woman who died at a private hospital in Queensland did not receive appropriate overnight care for a "very rare" complication from surgery.
Robyn Ann Beale's airway became blocked after an operation to remove part of her thyroid at the Mater Private Hospital Mackay (MPHM) on August 3, 2021.
Ms Beale died less than 24 hours after the hemithyroidectomy.
The inquest heard she suffered an acute post-surgical arterial bleed in her neck when she "coughed after swallowing a pain relief tablet".
The coroner said the hospital had been "transparent" about its shortcomings. ( ABC Tropical North: Liam O'Connell )
Coroner Carol Lee found the hospital relied on after-hours doctors who were not confident or equipped to handle the complication.
In findings handed down on Tuesday, Magistrate Lee said MPHM had inappropriate staffing, inadequate patient monitoring and inadequate medical coverage between 10pm and 8am.
But she found Ms Beale would have died even if this were not the case.
General surgeon William Fitzgerald, who operated on Ms Beale, told the inquest there had been no significant bleeding or concern when he finished the surgery.
William Fitzgerald (back left) performed thyroid surgery on Robyn Beale on August 2, 2021. ( ABC Tropical North: Liam O'Connell )
However, the coroner heard Ms Beale's condition "suddenly deteriorated" and she told a nurse she could not breathe at 2:25am the following day.
summary: Robyn Beale died in 2021 at Mater Private Hospital Mackay after a complication arose following surgery. ( ABC Tropical North: Liam O'Connell )
author: Liam Oconnell

title: New research finds Tasmanians are 40 per cent more likely to die from MND
content:
Kelli Skipper with her granddaughter, Hazel. ( Supplied: Kelli Skipper )
New research has found people living in regional Australia are more likely to die from motor neurone disease (MND) than people living in major cities.
A possible explanation for this difference is a higher exposure to chemicals due to agricultural activity.
Researchers are calling for a national MND registry to be established to allow them to better study the risk factors associated with regional living.
Kelli Skipper knows all too well the difficulty regional Australians face when it comes to getting diagnosed with motor neurone disease (MND).
Mrs Skipper was last year diagnosed with a form of MND known as progressive bulbar palsy.
It came after 18 months of tests, many of which required her to travel to Hobart, more than three hours' drive from her home in Shearwater.
She said it caused her a great financial burden, as she could not work due to the rapid onset of symptoms, but she also could not get any assistance until she had a formal diagnosis.
"It makes it very, very, very hard when you clearly need the help."
According to MND Australia, MND is an umbrella term for a group of diseases that affect nerve cells known as motor neurones, which are responsible for controlling basic bodily movements.
The organisation said MND was incurable, with the cause of most cases still unknown and treatment focused on helping people live better for longer.
New research led by Macquarie University has found Australians living in regional communities face higher mortality rates from MND than those living in major cities.
They found Tasmanians were 40% more likely to die from MND than people from New South Wales.
The study also found nationwide mortalities from MND had tripled in the past 40 years, climbing from 238 in 1986, to 781 in 2023, with 1 in 200 Australians dying from MND annually.
While the study did not confirm why regional communities were the most susceptible, it did put forward some possible factors.
summary: Kelli Skipper with her granddaughter, Hazel. ( Supplied: Kelli Skipper )
author: Crystal Mckay

title: Northern Territory paramedics face record rates of assault amid critical resourcing shortage
content:
Paramedics in the NT are facing ongoing resourcing challenges and a surge in assaults on the job. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
At about 8pm on a Wednesday night at the St John Ambulance NT call centre in Darwin, a queue of incidents from across the Northern Territory bank up on Alice Crooman's computer screen.
As territory deployment manager, Ms Crooman has a limited number of ambulances at her disposal, and the response time beside every incident is only growing.
Whenever a low-priority incident finally nears the top of the list, a high-priority incident cuts in line — a stabbing, a suspected heart attack, something that cannot wait.
The ABC shadowed Alice Crooman as she highlighted the limited ambulances available to respond to emergencies. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
"Tonight is one of those times where we are under-resourced from an ambulance perspective," Ms Crooman says.
It's an increasingly common circumstance, with the service recently calling its first "operational capacity white" in 50 years of operating in the NT, meaning dozens of calls had to be abandoned.
At the weekend, St John Ambulance NT service reached an "operational capacity white" for the first time. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
St John's data indicates the number of calls to NT paramedics has doubled over the past 10 years, from less than 50,000 to more than 100,000 per year.
Ms Crooman said in her 23 years working in paramedicine the number of ambulances on territory roads has grown by just two, and the impact of the workload on staff has been clear.
"It takes a huge psychological toll as well as a physical toll on the crews," she says.
"When you've had an exceptionally busy 12-hour shift — give or take occupational violence — you begin to question whether you want to come in the following day or whether you've even got the capacity, mentally and physically, to turn up."
On a recent ride-along with St John Ambulance staff, the ABC got a firsthand look at just how much pressure crews are continuing to face.
Alice Crooman says St John Ambulance staff are struggling with the growing workload. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
St John NT director of ambulance services Craig Garraway said there had been 169 incidents of occupational violence against paramedics over the past year, compounding issues of burnout and attrition.
summary: Paramedics in the NT are facing ongoing resourcing challenges and a surge in assaults on the job. ( ABC News: Tristan Hooft )
author: Joseph Hathaway Wilson

title: The 'miracle' drugs giving children with spinal muscular atrophy another shot at life
content:
Reena, Willow and Tully McIntosh. Reena and Willow both have spinal muscular atrophy type 1. ( Supplied )
Willow McIntosh takes a few wobbly steps, clutching her father's fingers for support.
She's nine months old and close to walking on her own.
It's a common scene in thousands of homes across Australia every day, a family event worthy of being preserved on video.
But for the McIntosh family, Willow's first tentative steps are a triumph of science and medical research. And in the words of her mother Katie, "a miracle".
Willow McIntosh was born with spinal muscular atrophy type 1. Before treatment was available, children with the condition typically died before their second birthday. ( Supplied )
Willow was born with spinal muscular atrophy type 1, a neuromuscular disorder that had a life expectancy of less than two years until, a decade ago, the first effective treatment was approved in the US.
On a wall in the McIntosh house at Springfield Lakes, west of Brisbane, is a framed vial of that therapy, marketed as Spinraza.
The drug saved the life of Willow's eldest sister, seven-year-old Reena. Three-year-old middle sister Tully does not have the genetic condition.
Reena McIntosh was diagnosed with spinal muscular atrophy type 1 at five months old. ( Supplied )
"If Reena had been born a year or two earlier, she may not have had access to treatment," her mum says.
"At that time, it would have just been symptom management and most likely she would have passed away very young.
When Reena arrived in 2019 — after her parents had tried for six years to have a baby — spinal muscular atrophy was not included on the list of conditions Queensland's newborn screening program tested for.
She was not diagnosed until she was five months old.
Before that day, her parents Katie and Clayton had never heard of spinal muscular atrophy, which affects an estimated one baby in every 10,000 live births.
summary: Reena, Willow and Tully McIntosh. Reena and Willow both have spinal muscular atrophy type 1. ( Supplied )
author: Janelle Miles

title: 50 per cent of Australians avoid health care they need due to cost, according to Consumers Health Forum report
content:
Ella Helman said it was getting increasingly difficult to afford her children's specialist appointments. ( Supplied )
One in two Australians missed out on health care they needed last year mainly because they could not afford it, according to a new report.
The Consumers Health Forum surveyed more than 5,100 Australians, with the results revealing a health system that has become increasingly unaffordable.
More health workers, lower costs for care and medicines, and better access were key areas for immediate reform, according to the report.
Sandwiched between the unrelenting pressures of caring for three young children and an aging and sick mother, Ella Helman has been struggling to ensure everyone in her family receives the health care they need.
The 36-year-old public servant lives in a dual-income household, but said mounting cost-of-living pressures had forced her to cut back.
"We have a mortgage and we're really struggling to pay for everything that we need to. My children don't have complex medical issues, but they do have medical issues — all three of my children have several food allergies that require us to see specialists fairly regularly," she said.
"It feels like every single year it's getting tighter and tighter, insurance premiums are going up, everything's going up, and we are having to make those difficult decisions about — do we delay that appointment?"
Some Australians with private health insurance will face premium increases of as much as 25 per cent from next month, according to new analysis from CHOICE.
Those decisions are even harder when it comes to her 60-year-old mother Cathy, who has a complex cluster of health issues, lives in public housing and is on the disability pension.
"She struggles daily just to meet her basic needs, let alone preventive health … it's the things that are bubbling under the surface but there's no money there to get them addressed and then eventually they become major, major problems," she said.
One in two Australians missed out on health care they needed last year mainly because they could not afford it, according to a new report by the peak healthcare consumers group.
The Consumers Health Forum (CHF) surveyed more than 5,100 Australians, with the results revealing a health system that has become increasingly unaffordable.
As the cost-of-living crisis intensifies, people from all walks of life are struggling. ABC Your Say asked ordinary Australians how they were making ends meet.
The survey showed entrenched inequities that meant Australians with lower incomes, chronic illness or diverse backgrounds were more likely to miss out on care.
summary: Ella Helman said it was getting increasingly difficult to afford her children's specialist appointments. ( Supplied )
author: Stephanie Dalzell

title: Super Netball Round-Up: Kiera Austin creates period shorts, Kate Moloney collision raises safety questions and Mary Cholhok must lift
content:
Kiera Austin hopes her new period shorts will keep girls playing sport. ( Instagram: Belle_Netball )
Round three showcased the best and the worst of Super Netball.
Saturday night featured two blockbuster matches that went down to the wire, while both Sunday clashes were extremely lopsided.
We had our first instance of extra time for 2026 in the Melbourne Derby, as the Vixens remained undefeated against the the Mavericks by claiming a 72-71 win.
The Mavericks were in control before they were dealt a heartbreaking blow late in the final quarter, when defender Tara Hinchliffe ruptured her right anterior cruciate ligament.
The Sunshine Coast Lightning and NSW Swifts clash had eight lead changes before the home side recorded a 66-65 win. It is the second time in consecutive weeks that the Lightning have secured one-goal victories in Sippy Downs and speaks to the new shooting combination that's beginning to flourish between Donnell Wallam and Cara Koenen.
These matches were highly entertaining because they were so tight and physical. Bodies were flying and several players hit the deck causing viewers to hold their breath. At the same time, we mustn't lose sight of our responsibility to keep player safety paramount.
Unfortunately, the two remaining games did not live up to the same hype and were decided by huge margins of 27 and 19 goals. These featured teams stuck at the bottom of the ladder, who really struggled to compete.
After claiming the wooden spoon last season, the Queensland Firebirds are once again not up to scratch despite their roster boasting five imports. They fell to the Adelaide Thunderbirds 73-46 in Brisbane, handing the visitors their largest winning margin in Super Netball history.
Meanwhile, the Giants' inexperienced playing group are still trying to find their feet. Frustrated coach Nerida Stewart gave them a spray in the second quarter as they fell further and further behind the West Coast Fever, before ultimately losing in Perth, 69-50.
If you missed it, don't worry — we'll get you up to speed with our Super Netball Round-Up.
Goal attack Kiera Austin was key for the Vixens in the Melbourne Derby, slotting five two-point super shots to keep her team locked in a close tussle with their cross-town rivals.
But it's the work off the court that Austin has done over the past week that is gaining praise.
Diamonds star Kiera Austin says she wishes innovation like period undershorts were available when she was growing up playing sport. ( AAP: Dan Himbrechts )
Partnering with Eltee Sydney, the Aussie Diamonds star has co-designed a performance period undershort she hopes will help to address the alarming rates of teenagers dropping out of sport.
summary: Kiera Austin hopes her new period shorts will keep girls playing sport. ( Instagram: Belle_Netball )
author: Brittany Carter

title: Coroner recommends inclusive language program after dying migrants repeatedly sought help from Adelaide EDs
content:
Sachintha Battagodage was coughing and vomiting up blood before he died in November 2020. ( Supplied )
Inquest findings into the deaths of two men, who separately sought help from Adelaide hospitals on multiple occasions, have been handed down.
Deputy State Coroner Ian White has made multiple recommendations, including that the South Australian Ambulance Service consider implementing an inclusive language program.
He also recommends photographs and videos provided by patients to be "incorporated into a patient's electronic medical record".
A coroner has recommended the South Australian Ambulance Service (SAAS) consider implementing an inclusive language program after an inquest heard an Indian national was told he was overreacting with the "man flu" in the days before he died of chronic pneumonia.
Deputy State Coroner Ian White, now a District Court judge, today handed down his findings after holding an inquest into the deaths of father of two Hemant Chadha, 38, and Sachintha Battagodage, 23, in 2024.
Mr Chadha died in April 2020 after seeking help at Adelaide emergency departments several times before his death.
The inquest heard evidence from Mr Chadha's neighbour, Irma Crews, who said paramedic Paul Vinar — who visited the 38-year-old's house twice in the two days before he died — had told Mr Chadha he was overreacting with the "man flu" and "was being a naughty boy" .
In his evidence, Mr Vinar denied using the word "naughty" or insinuating that Mr Chadha was "causing drama".
He had told the inquest he had not used the term "man flu" to infer an exaggeration, but as an attempt to overcome an alleged language barrier.
"Mr Vinar, I find, failed to understand or totally ignored the nature of who he was dealing with concerning the Chadha family," Mr White said in his published findings.
"On either proposition, he failed to meet the necessary standards of SAAS.
"It was unsurprising that the Chadha family was upset and felt like they were subject to racism.
"In the end, irrespective of his true state of mind, I find the effect of what he said and did was a major influence on Mr Chadha's decision not to seek SAAS's help in the final hours of his life.
"The small hope that he had of survival at that point was lost."
summary: Sachintha Battagodage was coughing and vomiting up blood before he died in November 2020. ( Supplied )
author: Eva Blandis

title: NSW coroner calls for new gun laws after deaths of Wayne and Noah Smith
content:
The NSW Coroner has recommended a suite of firearms safety measures at the conclusion of an inquest into the deaths of Noah and Wayne Smith (pictured). ( Supplied. )
The NSW Coroner has recommend new gun laws after an inquest into a murder-suicide in Yamba.
Wayne Smith used a gun to kill his son Noah Smith then himself six months after having his firearms licence restored.
NSW Coroner Teresa O'Sullivan recommended doctors be legally required to contact police with concerns about patients who own guns.
The NSW Coroner has recommended doctors be legally obliged to alert police with concerns about patients who hold firearms.
Magistrate Teresa O'Sullivan made a suite of recommendations at the conclusion of a coronial inquest into the murder-suicide of a father and son on the NSW north coast.
Warning: This story contains details that readers may find distressing.
The inquest heard Wayne Smith fatally shot his 15-year-old son Noah Smith before killing himself at their home in Yamba on June 1, 2023.
The coronial inquest found that Wayne Smith's deteriorating mental health had deeply impacted his relationship with his son, Noah Smith. ( Supplied: Facebook )
The court was told that while Wayne Smith's firearms licence had been previously suspended over mental health concerns — his guns were returned to him six months before the incident.
Magistrate O'Sullivan said both father and son deeply loved and cared for each other in a complex relationship strained by Wayne Smith's mental health.
"Wayne's deteriorating mental health necessarily affected Noah," she said.
"At times, he accepted responsibilities far beyond what would be expected of a young person.
"Noah was a much-loved son, grandson and friend who lived an active and full life."
The coroner noted that, unlike the majority of filicide cases, the inquest found no evidence that Wayne Smith had threatened or mistreated his son prior to the shooting.
summary: The NSW Coroner has recommended a suite of firearms safety measures at the conclusion of an inquest into the deaths of Noah and Wayne Smith (pictured). ( Supplied. )
author: Charles Rushforth

title: NT paramedics warn weekend 'critical failure point' will 'happen again'
content:
Samantha Cooper says she was forced to make "really difficult decisions" during the weekend's "critical failure point". ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
A senior Darwin paramedic says St John Northern Territory staff are "going to break" if operational capacity issues are not addressed urgently.
It comes after the service reached a "critical failure point" over the weekend, when it could not respond to all calls for help.
NT Health Minister Steve Edgington said his government remained "committed to addressing growing demand" but paramedics say high staff turnover is only likely to get worse.
First responders on shift on the night the Northern Territory's ambulance provider reached a "critical failure point" at the weekend worked 12-hour shifts without a break, with a senior paramedic warning "it will happen again".
In the early hours of Saturday morning, St John Ambulance NT became overwhelmed with requests for help , receiving 144 Triple Zero (000) calls between midnight and 7am.
Of those calls, 43 per cent were "abandoned" and 29 patients needing life-saving or urgent treatment waited up to five hours for paramedics to arrive.
A number of calls to St John NT paramedics at the weekend were "abandoned" due to lack of capacity. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
At one point St John NT duty manager Samantha Cooper was tasked with dividing 26 pending jobs among five crews.
"Demand for our resources was through the roof," she told the ABC.
Unable to meet the surging demand, the senior paramedic said she was forced to make "really difficult decisions".
"Unfortunately, it got to the point where we had to ask our crews to forego their meal breaks and their fatigue breaks", she said.
"We're asking people to almost give up their human right to a 30-minute meal break".
Paramedics who had already worked 12-hour shifts were also asked if they could stay back to help alleviate the pressure.
Staff turnover at St John NT is sitting at about 25 per cent, the highest in Australia. ( ABC News: Nibir Khan )
summary: Samantha Cooper says she was forced to make "really difficult decisions" during the weekend's "critical failure point". ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
author: Courtney Barrett Peters

title: Foreign crew charged after police say 'smuggling hides' used in cocaine plot
content:
One of the secret tunnels police allege was used in the plot to import cocaine. ( Supplied: AFP )
Six crew members of the MV Raider have been charged with drug smuggling.
Australian police allege they attempted to bring 1 tonne of cocaine into Australia after being intercepted by French Polynesian authorities earlier this year.
The crew members will face a Sydney bail court later today.
Police have charged six crew members of the high-profile MV Raider vessel with drug smuggling, saying they attempted to bring 1 tonne of cocaine into Australia after being intercepted by French Polynesian authorities earlier this year.
The MV Raider was boarded in January by French Polynesian police, who dumped almost 5 tonnes of cocaine found on board into the sea before releasing the crew.
The vessel was escorted into Sydney in March after the crew said they were struggling with mechanical issues and running low on critical supplies.
French armed forces seized 5 tonnes of cocaine from the MV Raider on January 16. ( Facebook: French Embassy in Australia )
Almost a week later the crew members were taken into immigration detention, but lawyers said they were unlikely to face any criminal charges over the cocaine seized near French Polynesia because the events occurred well outside Australia's territorial waters.
But in an extraordinary development, the AFP has revealed that French Polynesian authorities failed to find a further tonne of cocaine, which they allege remained hidden in one of the "professionally built and installed smuggling hides" on the vessel.
The AFP found secret tunnels during its inspection of the vessel. ( Supplied: AFP )
It's not clear exactly what happened to the drugs, but the joint statement said police suspected "an Australian-based crew operating on behalf of a larger criminal syndicate was looking to rendezvous with the MV Raider to conduct an at-sea transfer within Australia's Economic Exclusion Zone."
The drugs were not onboard the vessel when it was inspected by the AFP.
Six of the 11 crew have now been charged with trying to smuggle a commercial quantity of illegal drugs into Australia, and will face a Sydney bail court later today.
In a joint statement the AFP, NSW Police and Border Force said that when they searched the crew's electronic devices they found "evidence consistent with the allegation the MV Raider had further drugs on board".
summary: One of the secret tunnels police allege was used in the plot to import cocaine. ( Supplied: AFP )
author: Stephen Dziedzic

title: How to approach your employer about working from home and flexible work arrangements
content:
Negotiating flexible arrangements is not limited to those who have a right under the Fair Work Act. ( Adobe Stock )
With rising fuel prices adding further strain to already stretched household budgets, improving access to flexible work arrangements — including working from home — is increasingly being discussed as one way to help employees combat the rising cost of living.
But what are your rights when it comes to working from home — and what are the best ways to approach your employer about providing more flexible work arrangements?
According to the Fair Work Ombudsman, there are a few instances in which full-time and part-time employees who have worked for the same employer for at least a year are legally entitled to make a request to work from home.
Working from home may become the presumed status of Australia's white-collar workforce as part of a new push from the Australian Services Union.
These include if a worker is the parent or carer of a child who is school aged or younger, is a carer, a person with a disability, pregnant, or experiencing family and domestic violence.
"So, you have a legal right to request [work from home] after 12 months of continuous service, but some of those restrictions are still quite narrow," explains Libby Sander, an associate professor of Organisational Behaviour at Bond University on the Gold Coast, in the Yugambeh language region.
Libby Sander is a Bond University future of work expert. ( Supplied )
But negotiating flexible arrangements "is not necessarily limited to the people who have a right under the Fair Work Act", says Fiona Macdonald, acting director of the Australia Institute's Centre for Future Work.
"A lot of people negotiate working from home who don't fit into those categories," Dr Macdonald says.
"About one in three employees regularly work from home and there are a lot of employees who can't work from home just because of the nature of their job, so [one in three] is a pretty big number."
The Fair Work Ombudsman says, "best practice employers" will offer workers "flexibility where possible to help them balance their work and personal lives".
It says benefits for both employees and employers can include greater job satisfaction, lower levels of workplace stress and absenteeism, increased productivity, and more ability to attract and retain skilled staff.
If high fuel prices are affecting your work-life balance, it may be worth raising with your employer.
"If a big part of your petrol cost is commuting to work, then I think it makes good sense on a lot of fronts, if we can adopt flexibility," Dr Sander says.
summary: Negotiating flexible arrangements is not limited to those who have a right under the Fair Work Act. ( Adobe Stock )
author: Daniel Johnson

title: Health insurers are expanding into GP services. Here's why it could be a threat to Medicare
content:
A pilot project in preventive health at three Myhealth medical centres was launched in 2024. ( ABC News:John Gunn )
In the United States, it is not uncommon to see GP clinics with signs saying, "No Medicaid patients" or, "Insured patients only".
It is indicative of a problem-plagued healthcare system littered with tales of insured patients denied legitimate care and worse health outcomes for the uninsured.
In Australia, with GPs long complaining that Medicare rebates do not keep pace with costs, the threat of "No Medicare" doctors in the telehealth space has become very real.
In the US, only some clinics accept Medicaid patients. ( ABC News: Supplied )
Medicaid is not the same as Medicare, but it is the government-subsidised healthcare system in the US for low-income earners.
In the US, doctors can opt out of Medicaid and sometimes do because of its low reimbursement rates.
In Australia , major insurers like Medibank Private and Bupa have been open about their moves to buy up and open GP clinics and are quickly becoming major healthcare providers.
Similarly, all four major insurers are now offering some free or heavily discounted GP telehealth-style visits to their members.
The important thing to note about this is that these telehealth consultations are not covered by Medicare and insurers are picking up the tab in their entirety.
It is the result of the confluence of two laws that essentially make it legal.
That is why doctors are flagging concerns about a potential two-tier system.
The insurers say anybody can access these telehealth services, and that is true, but the problem is the price point.
By making it cheaper for members, insurers are starting to create a second tier where the privately insured have easier access to care, because it is more affordable to them.
As Australian Medical Association vice president Julian Rait points out, in a situation where Australia has a shortage of GPs, there is potential for these insurer-led GP services to start to attract GPs to their organisations, especially if they are better paid.
summary: A pilot project in preventive health at three Myhealth medical centres was launched in 2024. ( ABC News:John Gunn )
author: Alison Branley

title: Adelaide 'deployment exercise' tests emergency services with details drawn from recent events
content:
The event started with a ute crashing into the footpath near the Adelaide Convention Centre. ( Supplied: Russell Millard/The Advertiser )
Details from recent events have been incorporated into a training exercise in Adelaide.
The event included a simulated car crash, an explosion, an active armed offender, hostages and negotiation.
Lessons learnt from the exercise will be passed on to interstate agencies.
South Australian emergency services have conducted their largest mass casualty exercise in history in a bid to test coordination between agencies.
SA Police Deputy Commissioner Linda Williams said the "deployment exercise" held at the Adelaide Convention Centre and the area in front of it on North Terrace included details drawn from recent events, without naming the Bondi massacre in particular.
"The exercises are always adaptive to what's been happening around not only Australia but around the world, so, yes, there have been nuances that have been learnt from live events and it's really important for us to simulate that as much as possible into the exercise today," she said.
A robot from SA Police's bomb squad detonates a device during the exercise. ( Supplied: Russell Millard/The Advertiser )
The exercise involved about 300 first responders from SA Police, the SA Ambulance Service (SAAS), the Metropolitan Fire Service (MFS) and SA Health, as well as volunteer actors.
The training exercise started with a simulated car crash, then an explosion on North Terrace.
Later, it involved an active armed offender, hostages, casualties, intelligence-gathering and negotiation.
The exercise included officers from a range of SA Police branches, including forensics. ( Supplied: Russell Millard/The Advertiser )
Deputy Commissioner Williams said it was larger than a similar exercise in South Australia in 2008 , when the scenario was an explosion at Football Park, which has since been demolished.
"We will be looking very carefully at the debrief and the lessons learnt and, as the exercise was funded by the Australia-New Zealand Counter-Terrorism Committee, we will be taking that back nationally as well to other law enforcement and first responders in terms of the lessons learnt as well," she said.
MFS chief officer Jeff Swann said it was "really quite outstanding" to be able to collaborate with other agencies.
summary: The event started with a ute crashing into the footpath near the Adelaide Convention Centre. ( Supplied: Russell Millard/The Advertiser )
author: Eugene Boisvert

title: This 'one stop shop' for families says its model could be scaled up for Thriving Kids
content:
The Our Place Frankston North hub is constantly abuzz with activity. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
The first phase of Thriving Kids, the new NDIS alternative for some children with developmental delay or autism, will begin in October.
Part of the Thriving Kids model involves therapies being delivered in schools and early childhood centres.
While questions remain about how the already-stretched health and education workforces can cope, there are existing programs that some believe could be scaled up.
It's all happening at this community centre in Frankston North in outer Melbourne.
In one corner, giggling children gather for dance and music lessons, while in another, parents have their babies measured and weighed by a maternal child health nurse.
Some meet with therapists, while others just come for a cuppa.
The hub is one of nine across the state run by an organisation called Our Place, each located at a local primary school.
As kids play and learn at the hub, health and education workers help identify and treat developmental issues. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
Services here include early intervention playgroups, occupational and speech therapy sessions, maternal and child health nurses, mental health support, community counselling and support with the NDIS.
The clientele centres on hard-to-reach kids such as those from Indigenous or migrant backgrounds to ensure help can be provided to families that might otherwise go without.
The idea is that these parents would be more likely to engage with a less formal place like this, that is not overly clinical.
"They can get all the services and supports they need … from the time they're pregnant right through to school," Ms Taylor said.
"We engage families early because the sooner we can engage families into the system, they can get the supports that they need when they need them."
Elfie Taylor describes Our Place as "glue". ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
summary: The Our Place Frankston North hub is constantly abuzz with activity. ( ABC News: Patrick Stone )
author: Nas Campanella

title: Ask the Leyland Brothers' Mal Leyland planning 'one last trip' as he reveals dementia diagnosis
content:
Mal Leyland traces a weathered finger across a map of Australia, keenly planning "one more trip" through the heart of the rugged country that helped make him famous.
If his health allows, Mal, now 81, will be on the road again next month, retracing the epic Wheels Across a Wilderness filmmaking journey that he and his late brother Mike completed 60 years ago.
Carmen says her father has always been "her hero". ( Australian Story )
Mal knows this trip with his only child Carmen, son-in-law Jon Evans and a bunch of Leyland Brothers enthusiasts could be his last outback adventure.
He's had some issues with his health, he says, and was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease two years ago.
"One more trip would be pretty good," Mal tells Australian Story. "Maybe it'll be my last trip, I don't know. One day there will be a last trip, because one day I'm going to fall off my perch."
Mal Leyland is hoping he will be in good enough health to travel across Australia on his last big trip. ( Australian Story )
For many Australians in the 1970s, watching the Leyland Brothers forge their vehicles through swollen creeks and set up camp on the edge of vast red deserts was their first exposure to the far reaches and stunning beauty of remote Australia.
The brothers' mild-mannered nature juxtaposed with the wild risks they took helped cement their television programs, Off the Beaten Track and Ask the Leyland Brothers, as prime family viewing in the 1970s and 1980s.
The Ask the Leylands Brothers theme song became the earworm of a generation. ( Supplied: Mal Leyland )
But before the television programs, the two brothers produced four documentaries, using the skills they'd picked up when Mike worked as a news cameraman and Mal as a newspaper photographer.
Carmen says the trip her dad loved the most was Wheels Across a Wilderness, in which the brothers travelled from Steep Point, the most westerly point in Australia to Cape Byron, the most easterly, taking in the Simpson Desert on the way.
"They were the first to cross from one side to the other by a vehicle, which was an astounding achievement in that time," Carmen says. Their film and photographs of the waterfalls on Uluru during the wet season were the first ever captured.
"It was the most successful production that they ever did and the most memorable," Carmen says.
In 1966, Mal and Mike Leyland, together with a group of mates, did something no-one had documented before — a four-wheel-drive trek across the country. ( Supplied: Mal Leyland )
summary: Mal Leyland traces a weathered finger across a map of Australia, keenly planning "one more trip" through the heart of the rugged country that helped make him famous.
author: Leisa Scott

title: St John Ambulance NT reaches 'critical failure point' during 'extraordinary event' in Darwin
content:
Five ambulances operate in Darwin and Palmerston, home to more than 150,000 people. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
St John Ambulance Northern Territory says its Darwin service reached a "critical failure point" overnight as 42 per cent of Triple Zero (000) calls were abandoned.
Due to high demand, 29 life-threatening or urgent emergencies were left outstanding for periods up to five hours.
The NT's health minister says additional resources and support staff have been provided to St John.
Darwin's ambulance service reached a "critical failure point" overnight as 61 Triple Zero (000) calls were abandoned and 29 life-threatening or urgent emergencies were left outstanding for periods up to five hours.
St John Ambulance Northern Territory chief executive Abigail Trewin said shortly after midnight its service reached "operational capacity white" for the first time.
By definition, an operational capacity white is a "critical system failure point where service demand cannot be met despite all mitigation strategies".
"There is a high likelihood of harm to patients and/or staff, and significant risk to service continuity and organisational reputation," the definition states.
Between midnight and 7am, 144 Triple-Zero calls were received from Darwin and Palmerston, of which 61 were "abandoned", Ms Trewin said.
More than 40 per cent of Triple Zero calls were abandoned overnight. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
When a call is abandoned, it is not able to be answered within 10 seconds and is reverted to Telstra.
There were 14 priority one life-threatening cases that ambulances could not attend within the required 15 minutes, with one person waiting more than five hours.
Fifteen priority two cases were also left outstanding. Priority two cases require a response within 30 minutes due to a potential risk to life.
Abigail Trewin has been St John's chief executive for six weeks. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
Ms Trewin said no-one died as a result of the delays, but the conditions of patients deteriorated as they waited.
summary: Five ambulances operate in Darwin and Palmerston, home to more than 150,000 people. ( ABC News: Pete Garnish )
author: Jack Hislop

title: Services Australia urges patients to check if they are owed some of $272 million from Medicare
content:
Nearly 1 million Australians have unclaimed Medicare money waiting for them. ( Getty: Lisa Maree Williams )
More than 300 Australians are owed $10,000 or more from Medicare as part of a $272 million pool of unclaimed money.
The money is owed to people who have paid to go to the doctor, but not registered their bank details with myGov, so their rebate does not automatically land in their account.
Services Australia is urging Australians, particularly young people, to check if they've registered their details.
Nearly 1 million Australians have not received their share of $272 million in unclaimed Medicare rebates because they have not registered their bank details.
The money is owed to people who have visited the doctor but not claimed their rebates, with more than a quarter of the affected people aged between 18 and 24.
The average amount owed is $280, but 309 Australians are owed $10,000 or more, according to government figures.
Strengthening Medicare was one of Labor's big commitments in the 2025 election campaign. ( Getty/Bloomberg )
A handful of people are owed more than $30,000, but the government acknowledged that those people could be dead.
Services Australia has been trying to rectify the issue for years, but the amount of unclaimed money has only grown, from $230 million in 2023.
General manager of Services Australia Hank Jongen says, for affected people, once they registered their bank details, the money would automatically land in their account.
"Believe it or not, it's simply because we don't have their bank account details," he told triple j Hack.
"Unfortunately, a lot of people with Medicare enter into 'set and forget' mode."
"In addition to that, we contact doctors and say … we don't have bank account details, [so] when the patient next comes in, could you just encourage them to update their bank account details," he said.
"But if the trail runs out, in terms of up-to-date address details or bank account details, then we have very few options available to us."
summary: Nearly 1 million Australians have unclaimed Medicare money waiting for them. ( Getty: Lisa Maree Williams )
author: Evelyn Manfield

title: Running influencers are making big money and inspiring many to get active — but there are risks
content:
Some online commentary has focused on a spate of running injuries. ( Supplied: Instagram )
A woman checks in with her phone at various intervals along her 30-kilometre run route.
As the video flashes back it shows her getting ready, putting on her running belt and shoes.
On another account, a man speaks directly to the camera explaining what his next race will be. He's training for an ultramarathon where he'll try run for days around a loop course, until there's no one left standing.
"In life, all the things you want are on the other side of doing the hard work," he says.
He's tagged a popular exercise gear brand in the post.
They're both part of the online runfluencer community that finds engagement promoting exercise, but has also sparked debate about injury risk and the quality of advice offered.
Brisbane-based run coach and personal trainer Katie Dall has had a front row seat to the explosion of running's popularity both in-person and online.
Katie Dall is a run coach and personal trainer based in Brisbane. ( ABC News: Lottie Twyford )
One of the weekly run clubs she hosts now attracts crowds in the hundreds. It's so popular they've had to get marshals involved.
Runners at another of the clubs she hosts said consuming content online, as well as posting it themselves, helped with motivation and inspiration.
"Anything that helps get young women out and about and gives them confidence and gets them healthy, I think that's always a good thing," one said.
"It's when you're sitting about lazy, doomscrolling on the phone and then come across a video of somebody running and you're like … ah, just get my shoes on," another added.
"I get a lot of inspiration from others as well … and use that for my own motivation and drive," a third said.
The run clubs hosted by Ms Dall attract big numbers. ( Supplied: Club KT )
summary: Some online commentary has focused on a spate of running injuries. ( Supplied: Instagram )
author: Lottie Twyford

title: Measles on the rise across NSW, concern community infections spreading undetected
content:
Measles is on the rise across NSW with more than 33 cases detected this year alone. ( Reuters: Lindsey Wasson/File Photo )
A serious outbreak of measles in Asia has fuelled a surge in cases in NSW — but what concerns health authorities most are signs the highly infectious disease is spreading undetected in the community.
In the first three months of the year, the state recorded 38 cases of measles, five of which have no known infection source.
Vicky Sheppeard, director of South Eastern Sydney Local Health District's Public Health Unit, said the majority of cases were linked to returned travellers from south-east Asian countries.
However, Dr Sheppeard said the five infections identified between January 1 to March 7 cannot be traced back to exposure sites or overseas travel, which may indicate the virus was being locally transmitted.
"That is of concern because there must have been undetected cases," Dr Sheppeard said.
Dr Sheppeard says unknown infection sources are a cause for concern to health authorities. ( ABC News: Monish Nand )
Measles, which can cause a fever, cough and a characteristic rash that spreads to the whole body, is a potentially fatal but vaccine-preventable disease.
Severe complications can lead to middle-ear infections, diarrhoea and pneumonia.
In rare cases, patients can develop a fatal, progressive brain disease known as subacute sclerosing panencephalitis.
Dr Sheppeard said cases usually increased in the first few months of the year as travellers returned from overseas.
She said infections for 2026 had risen compared to the number of cases in previous years, as south-east Asian countries like Indonesia, Vietnam and the Philippines experienced significant outbreaks since the COVID-19 pandemic.
"Australians love to travel to south-east Asia and do that frequently, particularly over the summer period," Dr Sheppeard said.
She said cases were detected among Australians in their 20s and 40s, likely due to a change in the vaccine schedule dose and less vigorous childhood immunisation protocols.
A first dose of measles-mumps-rubella (MMR) vaccine was recommended at 12 months of age and a second at 18 months.
summary: Measles is on the rise across NSW with more than 33 cases detected this year alone. ( Reuters: Lindsey Wasson/File Photo )
author: Miriah Davis

title: Katherine residents call for hospital relocation after flood evacuations
content:
Katherine residents are calling for the hospital to be moved following floods. ( ABC News: Dane Hirst )
Katherine Hospital, located by the edge of the Katherine River, was evacuated twice over the past month after the regional NT town was hit by severe flooding.
The closures put additional pressure on strained hospitals in Darwin and Palmerston, and led to a federally funded temporary field hospital being stood up.
A long-term Katherine resident and retired GP is among those calling for the Katherine Hospital to be shifted away from the river to safeguard it from future floods.
During a tumultuous month of flooding in the Northern Territory town of Katherine, its hospital was evacuated twice, and patients were shifted hundreds of kilometres to Darwin.
Dozens of the hospital's high-risk patients and pregnant women from its maternity ward were shifted out during the emergencies.
Katherine Hospital was evacuated in the recent floods. ( ABC News: James Elton )
As the town's flood risk now subsides, questions are being raised about whether a long-term proposition to move the Katherine Hospital out of harm's way should finally be realised.
Retired long-term Katherine GP PJ Spafford said "the Katherine Hospital really does need to move into a flood-free zone" and away from the river's edge "so this situation doesn't re-occur".
PJ Spafford says the hospital needs major investment. ( ABC Katherine: Roxanne Fitzgerald )
"I think a major investment would be in order," Dr Spafford said.
Dr Spafford said this month's evacuation efforts would have put an "unbelievable amount of stress" on both the patients and staff involved.
They also put additional pressure on the already-strained Royal Darwin Hospital and Palmerston Regional Hospital.
The recent floods have been the worst in Katherine in 28 years. ( ABC News: Dane Hirst )
"They are overwhelmed already by local demand," Dr Spafford said.
summary: Katherine residents are calling for the hospital to be moved following floods. ( ABC News: Dane Hirst )
author: Matt Garrick

title: Dementia is Australia's top killer — yet most still miss the early signs
content:
The number of Australians with dementia is projected to increase to 1.1 million by 2065. ( Pixabay: Sabinevanerp )
When Sanna Sartawy's mother was diagnosed with dementia, she and her siblings were shocked.
Even though Mrs Sartawy came from a large Lebanese Australian family with five sisters and two brothers, they still struggled.
"We didn't know what to do with it," she said.
"We had no knowledge of dementia or what is dementia."
Sanna Sartawy, right, thought her mother's forgetfulness was part of normal aging. ( Supplied: Sanna Sartawy )
Mrs Sartawy said the family initially misinterpreted Samiha Elmerhebe's behaviours as ordinary signs of aging.
"She kept on repeating herself and forgetting things," the 57-year-old from Sydney said.
Their lack of understanding of dementia was compounded by stigma around the disease within the community.
Mrs Sartawy recalled the time she took her mother to a large community festival and Mrs Elmerhebe did not recognise family and friends who came to greet them.
"She reacted violently," Mrs Sartawy said.
Mrs Sartawy felt she had to hide her mother from the public to avoid comments like "Samiha has gone crazy now" or "she's got that crazy disease".
Her mother passed away at the age of 83 in 2024 — 12 years after she was diagnosed with dementia.
Mr He grew up with his grandma and shared a very close bond with her. ( Supplied: He Zihan )
Dementia is the leading cause of death in Australia, overtaking heart disease in 2024, according to the Australian Bureau of Statistics.
summary: The number of Australians with dementia is projected to increase to 1.1 million by 2065. ( Pixabay: Sabinevanerp )
author: Xiaoning Mo

title: TGA head defends handling of Australia's sunscreen scandal over SPF test results
content:
The TGA proposed a raft of changes to sunscreen regulation this week. ( ABC News )
The TGA has released a raft of proposed changes to the regulation of sunscreen in Australia after months of recalls over SPF levels.
CHOICE testing revealed 16 out of 20 widely used sunscreens did not meet their label claims in June last year.
The draft proposals are open for public consultation, with the TGA head hoping the shake-up will restore consumer confidence in sunscreens and the regulator.
Head of the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) Professor Tony Lawler has defended its handling of Australia's sunscreen scandal, saying the regulator had not "been sitting on our hands".
In his first interview since the sunscreen controversy erupted last year, Professor Lawler said he hoped the proposed shake-up of sunscreen regulations announced this week would help to restore Australians' confidence in sunscreens and the regulator.
"The very public consultation that we're undertaking is around rebuilding that public trust, not only in sunscreens but also the function of the regulator," he said.
Tony Lawler says the proposed changes would amount to a complete overhaul of the regulations in Australia. ( AAP: Mick Tsikas )
The TGA released a raft of options that could see a complete overhaul of sunscreen regulation in Australia.
Professor Lawler said the regulator had been cracking down on sunscreens while the new proposals were being drafted.
"I think it's important to note that where we have found the need for regulatory activity, which includes mandatory recalls, voluntary recalls and public notices and alerts, we've taken that action.
"We've also been balancing the need for prompt action versus considered and proportionate action."
The TGA says it has undertaken important action on the industry, including recalls, while it was drafting the proposed rule changes. ( ABC News: Jack Ailwood )
In its reform consultation paper, the TGA said the proposed changes came after monitoring international and domestic sunscreen developments, including SPF testing by consumer group CHOICE and investigative reporting by the ABC.
In June last year, CHOICE testing revealed 16 out of 20 widely used sunscreens did not meet their label claims.
summary: The TGA proposed a raft of changes to sunscreen regulation this week. ( ABC News )
author: Rachel Carbonell

title: AFL players to lose insurance coverage for brain injuries
content:
Former Melbourne Demons star Angus Brayshaw was forced into retirement because of concussion issues. ( AAP: Tracey Nearmy )
AFL players were told this week that the AFL Players Association super fund would not pay TPD benefits for claims after May 1 relating to brain injuries.
Currently, former AFL players can access up to $600,000 lump sums as part of a $54 million injury and support fund.
Class-action lawyer Michel Margalit says more needs to be done to care for former players.
More than 500 AFL players will soon no longer be covered by insurance for brain injuries in their superannuation, after Zurich Insurance moved to cut their ability to claim for concussion and head trauma from May 1 this year.
Zurich Insurance confirmed the move and told the ABC there was "widespread uncertainty" around the potential magnitude of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE) — the brain disease linked to repetitive hits — and the long-term effects of concussion.
In a statement to the ABC, Zurich Insurance said it was recently requested to put forward "a revised proposal for the provision of insurance" within the AFL Players Association (AFLPA) superannuation fund, the trustee for which is AMP.
A Zurich spokesperson confirmed AFL players were informed earlier this week that no total and permanent disability (TPD) benefit would be payable for any claim made after May 1 for traumatic head injury, concussion, CTE, post-concussion syndrome or any neurological impairment linked to brain injury.
"We understand that the trustee [AMP], in consultation with the AFLPA, determined Zurich's proposal was the most appropriate when compared with other available market offerings," a Zurich spokesperson said.
"There remains continued widespread uncertainty about the long-term health impacts and risks associated with concussion events from playing high-contact sports, including the subsequent development of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE).
