"Beyond individual or group insurance arrangements we acknowledge the establishment of the Severe Injury Benefit by the AFL and AFLPA in May 2025 to support players who have suffered a significant cognitive or bodily impairment from playing football."
Currently AFL footballers are entitled to as much as $1.1 million in death and TPD cover via their superannuation.
However on May 1, TPD cover will fall by $650,000 and death cover will fall by $350,000.
Such is the state of concern around insuring AFL players the ABC understands that Zurich Insurance was the only group to make an insurance offering in a recent tender process.
AFL players including Richmond's Shane Tuck, St Kilda great Danny Frawley, Eagles premiership player Adam Hunter and AFLW premiership player Heather Anderson have all been diagnosed with CTE post-mortem.
summary: Former Melbourne Demons star Angus Brayshaw was forced into retirement because of concussion issues. ( AAP: Tracey Nearmy )
author: Jessica Halloran

title: Mould infection cluster report identifies RPA balcony as likely exposure site, one patient remains in ICU
content:
Two patients died after being diagnosed with the mould infection at Royal Prince Alfred Hospital last year. ( ABC News: Keana Naughton )
The final report into the Royal Prince Alfred mould cluster has identified a hospital balcony as the probable infection site.
Two patients infected later died and a third remains in the intensive care unit three months after being diagnosed.
NSW Health has announced a raft of reforms in response to the outbreak that infected six transplant patients late last year.
NSW Health has identified a Royal Prince Alfred Hospital balcony as the likely source of a fungal infection linked to two patients who died and another fighting for life three months after being infected.
Doctors diagnosed six Ward 9E organ transplant patients with a fungal infection linked to the aspergillosis mould in November and December last year.
NSW Health on Friday released a report into the infections, which found a balcony that could have been accessed by all six patients as the probable exposure site.
"While the investigation team was unable to determine a single conclusive cause of the outbreak, the balance of evidence indicates the balcony and nearby construction activities posed the greatest exposure risk," NSW Health said in a statement.
A report found the balcony, located near construction works, likely posed the greatest exposure risk for a fungal outbreak in late 2025. ( Supplied: NSW Health )
Aspergillus is a spore forming mould often found in soil, water, decaying vegetation and buildings.
Most forms of the mould are harmless, but immunocompromised patients are at an elevated risk of serious health issues if the spores are inhaled.
The infection does not spread from person to person, but NSW Health's report said it became airborne during construction works.
Sydney Local Health District Chief Executive Deb Wilcox. ( ABC News: Julia Andre )
"The Aspergillus infection did impact on two patients directly who passed away, the panel is clear on that," Sydney Local Health District Chief Executive Deb Wilcox told reporters on Friday.
"We will never absolutely know the cause [of death], it's not possible to do so and it's really just on the balance of the facts in front of us."
summary: Two patients died after being diagnosed with the mould infection at Royal Prince Alfred Hospital last year. ( ABC News: Keana Naughton )

title: Mudgee Medical Centre secures six doctors to reopen books after 18 months
content:
Topic: Doctors and Medical Professionals
GP Lauren Dunstan (second from left) and five registrars have joined the Mudgee Medical Centre. ( Supplied: Doctors 4 Mudgee Region )
The Mudgee Medical Centre has reopened its books to new patients for the first time in 18 months.
The clinic secured the services of a GP and five registrars with the support of a community organisation.
Doctors 4 Mudgee Region wants to attract a further five GPs to the area in the next two years.
A GP clinic in Central West NSW has welcomed 350 new patients in just three weeks after reopening its books for the first time in 18 months.
In late 2024, the Mudgee Medical Centre joined the town's only other GP clinic in declaring it could no longer accept new patients.
But the arrival of a general practitioner and five new registrars has meant new arrivals can now get the medical advice they need without having to visit the local hospital's emergency department.
"We were just turning patients away in their droves," practice manager Colleen Best said.
"There is a backlog of health issues … [it] has been horrendous for them.
"Now they can ring up in the morning and actually get an appointment — it's just amazing."
Colleen Best says it is a relief for the community to see the clinic reopening its books. ( ABC News: Billy Cooper )
General practitioner Lauren Dunstan spent the past eight years working in western Sydney before making the move to Mudgee.
"In the Parramatta Westfield centre we've probably got more GPs than we have in the entire [Mudgee] region," she said.
The Mudgee Medical Centre has reopened its books to patients within the local postcode. ( Supplied: Mudgee Medical Centre )
summary: Topic: Doctors and Medical Professionals
author: Hamish Cole

title: Male victim-survivors of childhood sexual abuse overrepresented in suicide statistics, but seldom spoken about
content:
Warning: This article contains details of child sexual abuse and references to suicide.
A "shadow cast across his life" that put him in a "constant state of battle" is how Craig Hughes-Cashmore describes the effect childhood sexual abuse had on his adulthood.
Throughout his twenties and thirties, he lived with daily suicidality, making three attempts to end his own life that landed him in hospital.
He is a victim-survivor of childhood sexual abuse.
The perpetrator, he says, was a workmate of his father and used Mr Hughes-Cashmore's love of horses to lure him away from safety.
When he tried to disclose the abuse to a teacher at his school, he says that same teacher started abusing him and introduced him to another who did the same.
Craig Hughes-Cashmore says sexual abuse put a dark shadow across his life. ( ABC News: Sharon Gordon )
Mr Hughes-Cashmore is the founder and chief executive of SAMSN — the Survivors and Mates Support Network — a group which helps men like him move beyond their past and into a positive future.
His advocacy work came after years of his own personal struggles.
"Even in my twenties I had difficulty remembering chunks of my childhood. I tried hard to block it out," Mr Hughes-Cashmore said.
He used drugs and alcohol as a coping mechanism to try to block out the trauma.
"I just thought I was a f***ed up unit and I was hopeless, helpless … I had very low self esteem, if any at all, and no confidence," he said.
Craig Hughes-Cashmore says he was unable to have functioning relationships as a young man. ( Supplied/ABC News: Sharon Gordon )
Professor Patrick O'Leary from Griffith University's Disrupting Violence Beacon recently led research exploring the intersection of male suicide and childhood sexual abuse.
The study analysed and collated information from 47 international studies.
summary: Warning: This article contains details of child sexual abuse and references to suicide.
author: Baz Ruddick

title: Eight-fold increase in EPA odour complaints in Melbourne's west linked to small number of businesses
content:
The environmental watchdog has its eye on businesses including CSF Proteins. ( ABC News: Kyle Harley )
Residents in Melbourne's west have reported a large increase in bad smells this summer.
The environmental watchdog says animal processing plants are responsible and has issued compliance notices.
A company that turns non-edible animal waste into pet food has been ordered to fix its air filtration.
An animal waste rendering plant in Melbourne's west could be hit with fines, and other companies have received compliance notices, after an eight-fold increase in the number of reports of foul smells from nearby residents.
Residents across Melbourne's west noticed a foul smell intermittently blanketing the suburbs over the summer months, with 61 complaints to the Environment Protection Authority (EPA) on January 30 alone.
Sunshine resident Adam Pattison spent two years doing up his backyard with a deck, a barbecue area, a spa, and a large grassed area backing onto Kororoit Creek.
But he said it had been impossible to enjoy a backyard barbecue this summer, with "sickening" smells taking over the air between three to five times a week.
Adam Pattison says it's embarrassing to have to move his barbecue inside because of a bad smell. ( ABC: Kyle Harley )
"Just rotting flesh, rotting meat, you know, like dead rats; it's pretty vomit-inducing," he said.
"We just want to sit out and enjoy clean air and enjoy our house in the suburbs — we shouldn't have to put up with this."
Mr Pattison said he had complained to the EPA several times over the past few months, but the smell had not improved.
EPA West Metropolitan regional manager Jeremy Settle said the increase in odour reports this summer had been significant.
A blitz over the summer resulted in orders being issued to 10 companies in the west to strengthen their odour controls, for example by fixing gaps in buildings or improving air filtration.
Jeremy Settle says the EPA is investigating whether fines can be issued. ( ABC News: Kyle Harley )
summary: The environmental watchdog has its eye on businesses including CSF Proteins. ( ABC News: Kyle Harley )
author: Margaret Paul

title: International Olympic Committee bans transgender athletes from women's events
content:
The International Olympic Committee says it will screen athletes for the SRY gene to determine whether they were born female or male. ( Getty Images: Mattia Ozbot )
Transgender athletes will no longer be allowed to participate in women's events at the Olympic Games under changes announced by the International Olympic Committee.
Pride Cup, an advocacy group promoting LGBTQIA+ sports participation, said the proposal "will make all women targets for harassment and abuse".
The changes will come into effect from the 2028 Los Angeles Games.
Only biological female athletes, whose gender has been determined by a one-time gene-screening test, will now be eligible to take part in women's category events at the Olympic Games, the International Olympic Committee (IOC) has said.
The new rules essentially stop transgender athletes from competing in female category events at the Olympics; they had previously been eligible to compete at the Games once cleared by their respective federations.
Following an 18-month consultation phase, the IOC unveiled its policy for the Olympic Games, Youth Olympics and Games qualifiers.
It is widely expected to be adopted by international sports federations and become a universal rule for competitors in female elite sports, after years of fragmented regulation that led to major controversies.
All athletes wanting to qualify or take part in female category events from the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles onwards will ‌have to undergo an SRY gene test to determine their eligibility.
"Based on scientific evidence, the IOC considers that the presence of the SRY gene is fixed throughout life and represents highly accurate evidence that an athlete has experienced male sex development," the IOC said in a statement.
New IOC Kirsty Coventry president says it would "not be fair" for men to compete in women's categories. ( Getty Images: Milos Bicanski )
The screening would be conducted via saliva, cheek swab, or blood sample and would be unintrusive compared to other methods, the IOC said, and would be done only once in the athlete's career. SRY stands for sex determining region Y gene.
The Olympic body had long declined to apply any universal rule on transgender participation in the Olympics, and in 2021 instructed international federations to come up ⁠with their own guidelines.
New IOC president Kirsty Coventry did a U-turn immediately after taking over in June last year, saying the organisation ‌would take the lead for a uniform approach.
"At the Olympic Games, even the smallest margins can be the difference between victory and defeat," Ms Coventry said.
summary: The International Olympic Committee says it will screen athletes for the SRY gene to determine whether they were born female or male. ( Getty Images: Mattia Ozbot )

title: At 104, George Franklin is months away from becoming the world's oldest conductor
content:
George Franklin, aged 104, could soon become the record holder for the title of the world's oldest conductor. ( ABC News: Lauren Smith )
Musician and World War Two veteran George Franklin is the longest serving member of the City of Perth band.
Mr Franklin, 104, is a few months away from taking the Guinness World Record title for being the world's oldest conductor from American musician Frank Edmond.
Long-time friend and band member Don Steel described Mr Franklin as "amazing".
For most people, turning 104 is an accomplishment in itself.
For Western Australian musician George Franklin, the milestone puts him closer to another achievement: holding the record for being the world's oldest band conductor.
Music has always played a big part in the life of Mr Franklin, who celebrated his 104th birthday on March 11 among friends and family.
He started playing the cornet at the age of seven, before joining the City of Perth Band in 1934.
Mr Franklin was the conductor of the City of Perth brass band from 1974. ( ABC News: Lauren Smith )
Today Mr Franklin is their longest serving and most notable member, having conducted the brass band for decades before moving on to conducting the swing band every Monday evening.
George Franklin conducts the City of Perth swing band every Monday evening. ( ABC News: Lauren Smith )
He credits his love of music for his longevity.
"When it's grooving it can send shivers up and down your spine, it's exciting," Mr Franklin said.
Mr Franklin served in the Royal Australian Air Force during World War Two, and after that, he joined a military band until he was retired.
Aged 18, Mr Franklin joined the Royal Australian Air Force during World War Two. ( ABC News: Bridget McArthur )
summary: George Franklin, aged 104, could soon become the record holder for the title of the world's oldest conductor. ( ABC News: Lauren Smith )
author: Lauren Smith

title: Fuel dominates politics, Hanson confused over Rinehart flight questions
content:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says "don't panic" over Australia's fuel supply as prices rise. ( ABC News: Matt Roberts )
Welcome back to your weekly federal politics update, where Courtney Gould gets you up to speed on the happenings from Parliament House.
Up and down the hallways of Parliament House there's a growing sense of unease about what lies ahead as the war in the Middle East puts the Australian economy on an uncertain path.
Since the conflict began, petrol prices have risen to more than $2.56 a litre. Hundreds of service stations across the country have run out of at least one type of fuel. Prices in food, construction, and travel are likely to be pushed up as a result.
Then there are industries like waste collectors who are warning that diesel prices , and a struggle to secure supply, could lead to bin services being reduced.
Iran war live updates: For the latest news on the Middle East crisis, read our blog
Energy Minister Chris Bowen has continued to repeat his "don't panic" message. Australia's supply of fuel is fine, he's argued. He provided, when asked by the Coalition in Question Time, a breakdown of shortages state-by-state.
Under pressure, the prime minister has called a national cabinet meeting for Monday to coordinate responses between the state and federal government. Learning the lessons from the pandemic, the states want a national approach to rationing and other contingency measures, if we get to that point.
But there's also frustration that at a time of crisis the opposition is not looking to be constructive with the government, as Labor insists it was during the pandemic. The Coalition says it's willing to be constructive. Bowen accused them of not acting like "serious people".
Hundreds of service stations across the country are without at least one type of fuel. ( ABC News: Stuart Carngie )
They're in the business of politics. Not only are they fighting for their political survival against One Nation, the job of an opposition is to hold the government to account and to demand transparency.
The government argues it acted early and any call for fuel rationing (as we saw from Barnaby Joyce today) was too soon. Diesel flashpoint standards were relaxed this week , and all of the six shipments of fuel that were cancelled or delayed on the weekend had been replaced.
The war has also dashed any hope inflation could stabilise any time in the near future. You probably wouldn't blame Treasurer Jim Chalmers if another, more colourful, "F word" came to mind.
Chalmers warned a closed meeting of business leaders the impacts of the war could be as damaging as the global financial crisis and the pandemic. Modelling suggesting inflation could rise as high as 5 per cent was said to be looking "pretty conservative now".
"Purely from an economic and market point of view, the end of this war can't come soon enough for the economy," Chalmers said.
summary: Energy Minister Chris Bowen says "don't panic" over Australia's fuel supply as prices rise. ( ABC News: Matt Roberts )
author: Courtney Gould

title: Centre for Disease Control issues health warning after outbreak of respiratory diphtheria in NT
content:
Health professionals are urging Territorians to vaccinate against diphtheria. ( AP: Frank Augstein/Pool )
An outbreak of diphtheria has been declared in the Northern Territory after four cases were reported.
The NT's chief health officer said this was the first outbreak of the disease he had encountered in 26 years in the NT.
NT Health is urging people to vaccinate themselves and their children.
The Northern Territory Department of Health has declared an outbreak of diphtheria, with four cases of a deadly strain now confirmed.
On Wednesday, NT Health's Centre for Disease Control (CDC) issued a public health alert for the disease after three cases in Darwin and one in Alice Springs were reported in the last week.
Several cases have been detected in Darwin. ( Supplied: Jack Bullen )
There have also been 33 cases of cutaneous diphtheria notified since 2025.
"This is unusual, we haven't had an outbreak in my 26 years in the Northern Territory," NT Health Chief Health Officer Paul Burgess said.
He said contact tracing is underway for people who may have come into contact with cases of diphtheria.
Diphtheria has been a feared childhood disease throughout history because of devastating outbreaks .
The infection was a common cause of death in children up until the 1940s, when a vaccine was widely rolled out.
There are two strains of diphtheria identified in the NT; respiratory and cutaneous.
They are both highly contagious, vaccine-preventable diseases caused by toxic strains of Corynebacterium diphtheriae.
Diphtheria is a vaccine-preventable illness. ( ABC News: Michael Lloyd )
summary: Health professionals are urging Territorians to vaccinate against diphtheria. ( AP: Frank Augstein/Pool )
author: Tilda Colling

title: AI crocodile detection trials begin in north Queensland
content:
Crocodile detection cameras using artificial intelligence are being road-tested in Queensland.
If trials are successful, the technology could revolutionise crocodile monitoring at boat ramps or marinas.
Researchers are working on expanding the technology to estimate animal size.
Researchers are hoping a trial of artificial intelligence (AI) that automatically detects crocodiles could lead to an early alert system in north Queensland waters.
The prototype — developed by the Department of Environment, Tourism, Science and Innovation (DETSI) and James Cook University (JCU) — uses AI to scan waterway footage live streamed from a camera mounted on a movable trailer.
Over the next year, there will be three week-long, proof-of-concept field trials to test its accuracy.
Daniel Guymer at the Queensland Department of Science, Environment, and Innovation operations Centre in Cairns. ( ABC News: Conor Byrne )
The trials will take place in northern Queensland, but DETSI senior conservation officer Daniel Guymer said the department was still determining suitable test locations.
"[Somewhere] we're going to get a solid number of movements through the array to be able to glean the effectiveness of the technology," he said.
DETSI has provided $359,000 towards the project since January 2025, as well as thousands of videos to train the AI.
JCU researcher and project lead Tao "Kevin" Huang said the trials would shape further improvements.
Dr Huang said the system had already achieved "reasonable" results, and he was hoping for at least 70 per cent accuracy in the trials.
"There are some challenges in this research because of the light reflecting from the water surface, the changing conditions, and also the floating logs and debris that look similar to crocodiles," he said.
Tao "Kevin" Huang says success would mean big benefits for safety. ( Supplied: James Cook University )
Dr Huang said AI could outperform humans, but there was no guarantee of its effectiveness.
summary: Crocodile detection cameras using artificial intelligence are being road-tested in Queensland.
author: Conor Byrne

title: AFL reveals new illicit drug policy that Andrew Dillon says is 'most stringent in Australian sport'
content:
Andrew Dillon says the AFL's new illicit drug policy is the "most stringent in Australian sport". ( Getty Images: James Wiltshire )
All AFL and AFLW players will be hair-tested for drugs twice a year under a revamped policy trumpeted as the toughest in Australian sport.
The AFL and its players' association have an in-principle agreement for the fresh Illicit Drugs Policy (IDP), which ditches the oft-criticised current three strikes model.
"The revised IDP will be the most stringent illicit drugs policy in Australian sport," AFL chief executive Andrew Dillon said, in the presence of AFL players' president Darcy Moore, players' chief executive James Gallagher and AFL operations boss Laura Kane.
"The reality is, illicit drug use is not an issue unique to sport — it is a broader societal challenge and our players are not immune from the issues and pressures that exist in the community.
"It combines mandatory education, universal hair testing, treatment, monitoring and ongoing support across both AFL and AFLW."
The new policy, which features a suspension range of two to four matches for players for public use of illicit drugs, sat above World Anti-Doping Agency performance-enhancing drugs testing protocols.
Under the policy, players who test positive to illicit drugs three or more times will be sent to a panel at a newly formed Joint IDP (Illicit Drugs Policy) committee.
The player's club president, chief executive and football manager will be among those told of the positive tests.
A major change is introducing a fitness of play assessment — a player could still be deemed able to play after three positive tests.
The AFL announced the in-principle changes on Thursday, detailing a stepped process after a positive illicit drugs test.
Initially, players will undergo a behaviour change program possibly including health care interventions.
ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.
After a second positive test, players will have an individual management plan including clinical assessments and treatments.
If a player continued to use drugs they would be sent for a fitness of play assessment by an independent panel.
summary: All AFL and AFLW players will be hair-tested for drugs twice a year under a revamped policy trumpeted as the toughest in Australian sport.

title: AFL concussion class action expands to more than 100 former players and 11 clubs
content:
Max Rooke is the lead plaintiff in the class action which now includes more than 100 former players. ( Getty Images: Quinn Rooney )
Eight more former players have joined the class action including Ian Fairley, Nick Stevens and Michael Richardson.
Ten further clubs have been listed as defendants, alongside Geelong and the AFL.
Victorian Supreme Court Justice Andrew Keogh this week suggested May 31 next year as a possible trial date.
Eight more footballers have joined a multimillion-dollar concussion class action against the AFL, tipping the total to over 100 former players who all claim they have been left brain-damaged by the game.
Margalit Injury Lawyers have lodged a new writ in the Supreme Court of Victoria naming the AFL and now 10 additional clubs as defendants in the landmark court case.
In the new writ Carlton, Fremantle, Port Adelaide, St Kilda, Melbourne, Richmond, Collingwood, Essendon, North Melbourne and Footscray have been listed as defendants, alongside the AFL.
It has been three years since Margalit launched the class action.
Last year the AFL and Geelong Football Club attempted to have the case "declassed" or broken apart, meaning individual players would have needed to bring their own separate cases.
That bid was rejected, and trial dates are now expected to be confirmed next month.
Earlier this week Justice Andrew Keogh suggested May 31, 2027, as a trial date.
The lead plaintiff in the case is the dual premiership Cats defender Max Rooke who played 135 games for Geelong.
Rooke claims he sustained life-altering injuries due to concussion and head strikes.
The writ states Rooke was concussed between 20 and 30 times during his playing career and training, and was knocked unconscious on at least two occasions.
A new writ lodged on Wednesday included new plaintiffs including North Melbourne premiership player Ian Fairley, former Carlton vice-captain Nick Stevens, and Michael Richardson who played 302 games across Collingwood and Essendon.
summary: Max Rooke is the lead plaintiff in the class action which now includes more than 100 former players. ( Getty Images: Quinn Rooney )
author: Jessica Halloran

title: TGA proposes reforms to sunscreen regulation including new testing technologies
content:
More than a dozen sunscreens in Australia have been pulled from shelves or paused from supply over concerns about SPF protection. ( ABC News: Mark Leonardi )
The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) has proposed sweeping reforms to Australian sunscreen regulations.
The proposals include changes to SPF testing requirements, oversight of the labs that do SPF testing, and the way SPF is labelled — reforms that would overhaul the sunscreen industry.
One of the options flagged is removing SPF numbers and replacing them with low, medium, high and very high ratings.
The TGA has opened the draft proposals for public consultation.
It said the proposed reforms came after monitoring international sunscreen developments, as well as developments in Australia, including SPF testing by consumer group CHOICE and media reports by the ABC.
CHOICE last year revealed 16 out of 20 popular sunscreens didn't meet their SPF label claims.
The Australian sunscreen manufacturer at the centre of a spate of recent product recalls has been blocked from exporting sunscreens to the United States for the past three years.
An ABC investigation also revealed preliminary SPF tests had called into doubt the accuracy of SPF ratings of more sunscreens that hadn't been tested by Choice, as well as the way they were being sold.
The TGA referenced this reporting in its consultation paper on sunscreen regulation reform today.
"An ABC investigation revealed that a number of different products marketed in Australia were being supplied under the one Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods identification number, as well as raising concerns relating to a number of sunscreen products not meeting their claimed SPF rating," the TGA said.
"These developments, alongside our own internal reviews and investigations, have prompted the TGA to review aspects of the current regulatory framework for sunscreens."
The TGA said it was seeking to improve the regulation of sunscreens in Australia to ensure consumers had confidence in the protection offered.
"Given Australia has the highest rates of skin cancer and melanoma in the world, with around 2,000 people dying each year, it is critical that the regulatory settings are appropriate to ensure consumer confidence in sunscreens," the regulator said.
The proposals focus on improving the reliability of SPF testing, strengthening oversight of testing laboratories and simplifying SPF labelling.
summary: More than a dozen sunscreens in Australia have been pulled from shelves or paused from supply over concerns about SPF protection. ( ABC News: Mark Leonardi )
author: Rachel Carbonell

title: Law reform review calls for overhaul of WA's 'strictest' guardianship secrecy laws
content:
The law prohibits West Australians living under state guardianship from being publicly identified — even after their death.
An independent legal committee has recommended changing guardianship laws in Western Australia to allow vulnerable people under state care to speak up about their experiences.
These people are often subject to mistreatment, the ABC has revealed in several investigations.
WA Attorney General Tony Buti said the government would "closely consider" the recommendations of the report.
West Australians living under state guardianship arrangements should be allowed to publicly speak up about their experiences, a review of the laws has recommended, to ensure these often vulnerable people are not taken advantage of.
People deemed unable to make their own decisions, such as those with a cognitive disability, can be put under the care of state guardians or trustees who manage their lives and finances.
But across most of the country, it is illegal to identify these people, making it difficult for them to speak about their lives and experiences.
When Dan started suffering from memory loss and anxiety, it kicked off a chain of events that left him living in a nursing home against his will with little say over his future.
The ABC has revealed these include horror stories such as pensioners being left without enough money for food , and exorbitant fees being charged to manage properties at a loss.
In the first comprehensive review of Western Australia's laws since 1990, the independent Law Reform Commission (LRC) of WA has found "there is no good reason" to prevent people under state guardianship or administration from being identified, if they consent.
"The law must strike a balance between safeguarding the privacy of people involved … and respecting the autonomy of people to speak publicly, if they wish, about themselves, and their experiences," the commission said in a report, which was recently published.
How institutions designed to protect people are being used against them, while gag laws shroud the system in secrecy.
With the exception of South Australia and the Australian Capital Territory, it is illegal to publish the identity of people who are, or ever were, under state care.
That prohibition remains even if they are later deemed capable of making their own decisions, are no longer under state care, and even after they die.
Penalties range from hefty fines to jail time.
summary: The law prohibits West Australians living under state guardianship from being publicly identified — even after their death.
author: Cason Ho

title: Family launches palliative care foundation after 33-year-old son's final hospital experience
content:
Mount Gambier couple Chris and Deb Brooks say Ryan's palliative care fell short of expectations. ( ABC South East SA: Josh Brine )
Ryan Bowman has faced more health battles than most in his 33 years.
Born with five congenital heart defects, he underwent four surgeries, the last of which left him with an acquired brain injury.
Called a "miracle baby" in an article in the Adelaide Advertiser, he persevered through his teenage years to obtain his driver's licence and become a father in his 20s.
In 2023, he was diagnosed with Hodgkin lymphoma but, after intense chemotherapy, was able to celebrate entering remission.
Ryan Bowman was born with five congenital heart defects. ( Supplied: Deb Brooks )
"After he had the acquired brain injury, it was rehab, he had to learn to walk, talk, see.
"That sort of stayed with him throughout his life."
A year after entering remission, Ryan was diagnosed with late-stage heart failure.
On February 15, 2025, he was admitted to Mount Gambier and Districts Health Service.
"Ryan was admitted quite unwell on a Saturday morning, he had a chest X-ray and was diagnosed with double pneumonia," Deb said.
Ryan was a lifelong Adelaide Crows supporter. ( Supplied: Deb Brooks )
As his condition worsened, Ryan's admission became a case of palliative care, which his mother and an independent report both say fell short of expectations.
"One of our big things was not to talk in front of Ryan and they did," she said.
"They spoke about 'this could be end of life', things like that."
summary: Mount Gambier couple Chris and Deb Brooks say Ryan's palliative care fell short of expectations. ( ABC South East SA: Josh Brine )
author: Sam Bradbrook

title: This Mediterranean-style diet could keep your brain sharp as you age
content:
The MIND diet incorporates green vegetables, beans, whole grains, nuts, berries, poultry, fish and olive oil ( Unsplash: Anna Pelzer )
The Mediterranean diet — rich in olive oil, fish, vegetables and legumes — has long been linked to better heart health. Growing evidence suggests it may also help support brain health as we age, with a brain-focused variation of the diet drawing increasing scientific attention.
It is called the MIND diet. The name stands for Mediterranean -DASH Intervention for Neurodegenerative Delay — though what matters more than the acronym is what it actually involves: plenty of green vegetables, beans, whole grains, nuts, berries, poultry and fish, with olive oil as the main cooking fat, and limited amounts of red meat, butter, cheese, fried food and sweets.
It combines the most brain-friendly elements of two well-studied eating patterns: the traditional Mediterranean diet and the DASH diet, which was originally developed to lower blood pressure.
A recent analysis from the long-running Framingham heart study examined the diets of adults aged 60 and over and assessed how these dietary patterns were associated with brain scan data collected later in the study.
Those who followed the MIND diet most closely tended to have more grey matter — the tissue associated with memory and decision-making — and showed less overall loss of brain volume over time.
Both findings point in the same direction: that this way of eating may help keep the brain in better shape as we get older.
This is not the first study to suggest a link between diet and dementia risk. An earlier analysis combining 12 observational studies found an overall reduction in dementia risk of between 15 and 22 per cent among people who followed Mediterranean-style diets, with the MIND diet showing the strongest effect of the three patterns studied. That is a meaningful difference, even if it cannot be taken as proof that diet alone is responsible.
Within the Framingham study, berries and poultry stood out as particularly beneficial for grey matter.
This fits with what other research has suggested. Blueberries, for instance, have been the subject of several small trials, with one recent study finding improvements in memory even in people already showing early signs of memory problems.
Since red and processed meat have been linked to higher dementia risk in other studies , replacing them with chicken may partly explain why poultry appears beneficial.
Blueberries have been studied in several small trials, with one finding improvements in memory even in people already showing early signs of memory problems. ( ABC Rural: Brandon Long )
Some of the findings were less straightforward. Fried food, as expected, was associated with worse outcomes. But whole grains, generally considered one of the healthier staples, produced a surprisingly weak result.
The reasons are unclear, though large amounts of bread and pasta — even wholegrain varieties — may raise blood sugar enough to offset some of the benefits. The evidence on whole grains and brain health remains mixed , and this is one area where more research is needed.
It is also worth noting who, in the Framingham study, was most likely to follow the MIND diet. They tended to be women, non-smokers, well-educated, and less likely to be overweight or to have diabetes, high blood pressure or heart disease.
summary: The MIND diet incorporates green vegetables, beans, whole grains, nuts, berries, poultry, fish and olive oil ( Unsplash: Anna Pelzer )
author: Eef Hogervorst

title: Married at First Sight expert Mel Schilling remembered as 'amazing' and 'radiant' after bowel cancer death
content:
Mel Schilling is recognised for her remarkably powerful presence on television. ( Instagram: Mel Schilling )
Mel Schilling emerged as one of Australia's most recognisable relationship experts on Australian television.
On Tuesday, she died of bowel cancer at the age of 54.
As a judge on the hit reality show Married At First Sight Australia (MAFS), she was known for her sharp insight and a lack of tolerance for poor behaviour.
Schilling was born in Melbourne and was more recently based in the United Kingdom.
Away from the spotlight, she was a devoted wife and mother.
Schilling, who held a Bachelor of Arts in Psychology and a Graduate Diploma in Psychology, had been a practising psychologist for more than 20 years.
She began her career in 1998 as a consulting psychologist at Morgan and Banks, then moved into a senior role with Personnel Decisions International in 2001, later working as a consulting psychologist with Extraversion Consulting.
Family and friends have paid tributes to Mel Schilling. ( Supplied: Instagram )
She first joined Married At First Sight Australia in 2016 for its second season, alongside John Aiken and Trisha Stratford, who was later replaced by Alessandra Rampolla.
Following her success in Australia, she was appointed an expert on the UK edition in 2021, joining Paul C Brunson and Charlene Douglas.
Schilling married Gareth Brisbane in 2018.
The couple met on the dating site eHarmony, and eight years later, they tied the knot in Bali.
Schilling became a mother later in life at 42, and her husband celebrated her on Instagram, writing: "This is a woman who became a new mum and a TV star at 42 — and nailed both."
The 54-year-old was first diagnosed with colon cancer in December 2023.
summary: Mel Schilling is recognised for her remarkably powerful presence on television. ( Instagram: Mel Schilling )
author: Shari Hams

title: E-bike changes coming to NSW include reduced power and proposed age limit
content:
Restrictions on high-powered e-bikes have been introduced, as the state government explores age limits. ( ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring )
E-bikes have been a source of ire in New South Wales.
While offering an accessible form of transport and exercise, their rapid growth has also led to injuries, speed concerns, misuse, and lithium-ion fires.
In response, the state government recently announced, "measured and staged reforms aimed at improving safety for riders, pedestrians and the wider community".
A power output enforcement has been introduced from this month, while a minimum age requirement for riders is also currently being explored.
Here's what the new rules will look like for those who use the estimated 760,000 e-bikes across the state.
European safety and performance standards have been introduced from March for new e-bikes, following in the footsteps of WA.
An e-bike marked as "EN 15194" now meets the manufacturing requirements to keep it acting like a bicycle rather than a motorbike.
It means e-bikes must have a maximum power output of 250 watts, and pedal assistance has to cut out at 25km/h.
Additionally, no assistance will be provided if the rider is not pedalling at 6km/h or more.
The government says the standard also includes battery, electrical, and fire-safety requirements, as well as anti-tampering protections to prevent alteration of power or speed limits.
There are more than 700,000 e-bikes in NSW. ( ABC News: Emma Wynne )
In 2023, NSW enforced a 500-watt standard for e-bike motors — or the maximum continuous energy output.
Chief executive officer at advocacy group Bicycle NSW, Peter McLean, said there were "not a huge number" of 500-watt devices on the roads because "not many companies actually make" them.
Meanwhile, "some of the high-powered bikes out there, which we're commonly seeing referred to as just e-bikes, they're illegal devices," he said.
summary: Restrictions on high-powered e-bikes have been introduced, as the state government explores age limits. ( ABC Radio Sydney: Declan Bowring )
author: Millie  Roberts

title: NDIS participants work more, feel better after accessing scheme, report finds
content:
Accessing the NDIS is associated with better workforce outcomes and better self-reported vitality, according to a new study. ( Supplied )
People who access the NDIS fare better in the workforce and feel healthier within four years of entering the scheme, a new report finds.
The finding is based on data that tracks the same individuals over more than a decade.
It comes as the government seeks to contain the cost to taxpayers of the fast-growing scheme.
People who gain access to the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) fare better in the workforce and feel healthier than they did before they received government-funded support, according to new analysis.
A report from the e61 Institute, an independent economic research body, has found NDIS participants work nearly two hours more per week within four years of entering the scheme, and earn an extra $76 per fortnight.
As a result, participants are less likely to receive the JobSeeker unemployment payment and more likely to receive the Disability Support Pension, which supplements the income of those who are in the workforce.
NDIS recipients also show a steep improvement in their own assessment of their energy levels, whereas in the years prior to gaining access, they usually reported steep declines.
"People are typically entering the NDIS at a difficult point in their lives, after a sustained period of declining health, growing detachment from the labour force, and increasing reliance on income support," author Dr Pelin Akyol said.
"While we cannot prove the NDIS is the cause of these improvements, it is likely to support participants' transition through the services it provides."
The primary purpose of the federal scheme, which began in 2013, was to remove barriers to the economic and social participation of Australians with severe and permanent disabilities.
Its cost to taxpayers has vastly outstripped initial expectations, prompting successive governments to question its sustainability and contain its scope by targeting it to those in greatest need and ridding the scheme of unscrupulous service providers.
Previous work by the e61 Institute itself, which is helmed by former Productivity Commission chair Michael Brennan, has identified the NDIS as a key pressure on government spending, in particular because it is not means-tested.
The government has set a target to reduce annual growth in the $48 billion scheme to between 5 and 6 per cent per year. Current budget projections have its rate of growth at more than 7 per cent, down from more than 10 per cent in its early years.
At the same time, participants have long reported difficulty navigating the scheme and accessing support, and uncertainty about how the cost-cutting drive will affect their support plans.
summary: Accessing the NDIS is associated with better workforce outcomes and better self-reported vitality, according to a new study. ( Supplied )
author: Tom Crowley

title: Married at First Sight expert Mel Schilling dies, aged 54
content:
Mel Schilling became a new mum and a TV star at 42. ( Instagram: @mel_schilling1 )
Married at First Sight expert Mel Schilling has died of cancer, aged 54.
The public figure announced her terminal diagnosis in March, saying her cancer had spread to the left side of her brain.
She is survived by her husband and daughter.
Married at First Sight expert Mel Schilling has died of cancer, aged 54.
Schilling's husband, Gareth Brisbane, took to social media on Tuesday night to reveal the news.
"In her final moments, when I thought cancer had taken away her ability to speak, she ushered me closer and whispered a message for Maddie and me that will sustain me for the rest of my life," he wrote in a post on Instagram.
"It took all of her remaining strength, and that gesture summed up our wee Melsie perfectly. Even then, her only thought was for Maddie and me."
The public figure was first diagnosed with colon cancer in December 2023.
She announced her terminal diagnosis in March, saying her cancer had spread to the left side of her brain.
In a post on Instagram in March , Schilling said her oncology team told her "there's nothing further they can do".
Schilling became a new mum and a TV star at 42, her husband said.
"This is a woman who, through two years of chemotherapy, when she could barely lift her head from the pillow, never complained and never stopped showing courage, grace, compassion and empathy, and never missed a day of filming," he wrote.
In February, Schilling announced that she was stepping away from MAFS to prioritise her family and health, after 10 years on the show.
Her health took a turn for the worse over Christmas, when she started to experience "blinding headaches and numbness" down one side of her body.
summary: Mel Schilling became a new mum and a TV star at 42. ( Instagram: @mel_schilling1 )

title: Reducing 'just in case' purchases can help avoid empty shelves and fuel bowsers
content:
If you've topped up your tank at a petrol station recently, did it feel like you were "panic buying"? ( Pexels: Michael Burrows )
If you've topped up your tank at a petrol station recently, did it feel like you were "panic buying"? Or did it feel more like "I'd better buy some, just in case"?
During the COVID pandemic our research team wrote about the psychological drivers behind Australians buying up toilet paper: scarcity mindset, anticipated regret and regaining "control".
We also warned that politicians or media coverage rebuking people for buying more at the supermarkets actually risked making it worse .
Over recent weeks, some senior politicians have repeated this mistake, berating people as "un-Australian" for "panic buying" fuel.
Iran war live updates: For the latest news on the Middle East crisis, read our blog
But one of the lessons we should have learnt from COVID — when supermarket shelves were cleared and some buying limits had to be introduced — is that most people didn't perceive themselves as "panic buyers".
The real lesson of empty petrol stations or supermarket shelves isn't that people are irrational. ( ABC News )
A 2020 survey asked 450 people in the United States and Australia "to what extent did you engage in panic buying in the first few months of the COVID-19 outbreak?" On average, both the older US participants and mostly university-aged Australians participants scored themselves as only having "low engagement" in panic buying.
A smaller UK study published in 2022 found similar results, concluding "panic buying is not a useful concept".
Instead, Australian and other shoppers during COVID saw buying a bit extra as playing it safe, rather than panicking.
Many Australians have lingering memories of times when supply has struggled to meet demand: from banana prices jumping from $2 a kilogram to $15 a kilo after Cyclone Larry in 2006 to struggling to find eggs last year due to bird flu.
With little sign of the Strait of Hormuz being safe for oil tankers anytime soon, it's entirely rational for people to think, "I'd better get petrol now, before the price jumps further" — sooner than we might have refilled normally.
But when enough people buy more "just in case" all those individual choices can collectively overwhelm our fuel and food systems.
Australia has spent decades pursuing lean supply chains — what's known as " just-in-time " supplies, with minimal buffer stock sitting around in warehouses.
summary: If you've topped up your tank at a petrol station recently, did it feel like you were "panic buying"? ( Pexels: Michael Burrows )
author: Jennifer Macklin and Ananya Bhattacharya

title: Influencer Stacey Warnecke's 'birthkeeper' may be compelled to give evidence at inquest, court hears
content:
Stacey Warnecke died after giving birth at home last year. ( Instagram: Natural Spoonfuls )
A coroner may order a freebirth support worker to give evidence at an inquest into the death of Seaford woman Stacey Warnecke.
The nutrition influencer suffered a postpartum haemorrhage and cardiac arrest after giving birth at home in 2025.
The support worker, Emily Lal, has been given until May 15 to respond to the court's request that she provide a statement.
A "birthkeeper" who was present while a mother experienced deadly complications during childbirth could be hauled into court to explain her actions during the tragedy.
The 2025 death of nutrition influencer Stacey Warnecke is being examined by a Victorian coroner, with an inquest to probe the circumstances around Ms Warnecke's decline and the role of a support worker, Emily Lal.
Emily Lal was with Stacey Warnecke during the birth and when she developed postpartem complications. ( Supplied )
Ms Lal, known online as The Authentic Birthkeeper, was at Ms Warnecke's Seaford home when her baby was born and when Triple Zero (000) was called afterwards.
Ms Warnecke, 30, died from a postpartum haemorrhage and suffered multiple cardiac arrests after being taken to hospital.
Today, a lawyer assisting Coroner Therese McCarthy said a proposed inquest could take place in mid-June, but that Ms Lal had refused to assist the investigation.
"She is claiming privilege against self-incrimination and is thus refusing to provide a statement," counsel assisting Rachel Ellyard told the Coroners Court of Victoria.
"She was present at the birth, she can be heard on the triple-0 call … all of which make her a highly relevant witness to the birth and the events of Stacey's deterioration before she was taken to hospital."
The inquest heard Stacey Warnecke suffered a haemorrhage after giving birth. ( Supplied )
Under the Coroners Act, a court can compel a person to give evidence "in the interests of justice", even if the witness objects.
A coroner can also grant a certificate preventing that evidence from being used against the witness in a future case.
summary: Stacey Warnecke died after giving birth at home last year. ( Instagram: Natural Spoonfuls )
author: Kristian Silva

title: Regional WA ambulance volunteers receive 'unfathomable' donation from patient
content:
Steven Petchell and Chloe Bell have welcomed a generous donation of more than $280,000 towards a new ambulance. ( Supplied )
A grateful ambulance patient has donated more than $280,000 towards a new ambulance for their regional WA town.
The town of Koorda has five volunteer ambulance officers who travel up to 100km to attend call-outs.
The donor, who wishes to remain anonymous, said they wanted to show their gratitude for the local St John WA service.
In a town of 200 people, it is not often the Koorda ambulance sub-branch receives a $280,000 donation.
The anonymous donation came from a patient who made use of the ambulance's services in Western Australia's Wheatbelt region.
The large sum of money will mean the volunteers will be able to purchase a new ambulance.
The sub-branch has a team of just five volunteers and partner with the neighbouring Wyalkatchem sub-centre to turn out to more than 150 call-outs a year.
Kylie Burrell (left), Chloe Bell and James Holdsworth believe the new ambulance will bring pride to the town of 200 people. ( ABC Midwest and Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup )
Wyalkatchem Koorda sub-centre chairperson Steven Petchell said the generosity of the donation was difficult to fathom.
"I felt like I had to physically see the cheque to comprehend it," he said.
"I was blown away, the whole sub-centre was too.
Currently in WA, sub-centres are required to raise funds for new ambulances, with suitable ex-metro vehicles issued to the regions as part of an asset management program.
Steven Petchell says the new ambulance will come with improved safety and technology. ( ABC Midwest and Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup. )
Mr Petchell said once built and on the road, the new ambulance would provide a safer, more reliable workspace for ambulance volunteers.
summary: Steven Petchell and Chloe Bell have welcomed a generous donation of more than $280,000 towards a new ambulance. ( Supplied )
author: Eliza Bidstrup

title: Women are less likely than men to receive CPR. This surf club is looking to change that
content:
Kieran Lynch is behind Yamba's push to ensure women get the same help as men. ( ABC North Coast: Hannah Ross )
Yamba Surf Life Saving Club is leading a campaign for gender equity in lifesaving responses.
The campaign responds to data showing women are less likely than men to receive CPR or defibrillation from a bystander.
Surf Life Saving Australia says it is updating its training manual to address the problem.
When surf club volunteer Keiran Lynch came across data showing women were more likely than men to die if they have a cardiac arrest in public, he knew he had to act.
"I have three sisters, two daughters and a wife, so to do nothing was not an option," Mr Lynch said.
He decided to work within his "sphere of influence" as chief training officer with Yamba Surf Life Saving Club (SLSC) on the NSW North Coast to implement a campaign to address the issue.
Yamba Surf Life Saving Club ensures its volunteers train on manikins with female and male anatomy. ( ABC North Coast: Hannah Ross )
A 2024 analysis of data from the NSW Ambulance Service showed women were 10 per cent less likely than men to receive cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) from a bystander in a public setting.
The figure jumped to 50 per cent when it came to women receiving defibrillation in public, a technique that requires exposing the chest to allow skin contact with the electrode pads.
The study hypothesised this might be because bystanders were concerned about "modesty, fear of causing harm or legal liability and perceptions of fragility".
It mirrors the findings of previous research by the European Society of Emergency Medicine .
Mr Lynch said Yamba SLSC came up with a program dubbed CP-Her to make people aware of the problem and introduce ways to tackle it.
Yamba Surf Life Saving Club on the NSW North Coast has launched the "CP-Her" initiative. ( ABC North Coast: Hannah Ross )
The club now does all its CPR and defibrillation training using both female and male manikins.
summary: Kieran Lynch is behind Yamba's push to ensure women get the same help as men. ( ABC North Coast: Hannah Ross )
author: Hannah Ross

title: Rising fuel prices potentially forcing rural patients to forgo medical appointments, experts warn
content:
The Rural Doctors Association says some regional patients may be forced to make "life and death" decisions due to rising fuel costs. ( ABC North West Queensland: Kelly Butterworth )
Many regional and rural patients say they are struggling to afford fuel to attend critical medical appointments.
The Rural Doctors Association says some people may be forced to make "life and death" decisions.
The association says travel subsidies for regional and rural patients need to be increased.
Belinda Menzies and her family all live with significant health issues.
But as fuel prices rise, she says they are cutting back on driving to appointments, which she worries will cause her family's health to deteriorate.
Iran war live updates: For the latest news on the Middle East crisis, read our blog
"I'm scared more than anything of the decline in the progress that we've made," Ms Menzies said.
She said her family lived with Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, a genetic connective tissue disorder.
It takes more than an hour to travel from her home in Beaudesert to doctors and therapists in Brisbane and the Gold Coast and the trips are quickly becoming unaffordable.
Belinda Menzies (left) with Maddie Menzies, 24, and Elliot are spending hundreds of dollars on fuel every month. ( ABC News: Heloise Vyas )
"We might only do a half grocery shop and have to just live on the cheap baked beans or whatever's in the fridge and cupboards," she said.
Fuel bills for the Menzies family have "skyrocketed", with Ms Menzies estimating her monthly fuel costs between $300 and $350 will reach about $500.
But she said that expense could increase further "depending on how high the prices go".
Fuel costs are fast becoming a critical expense for patients in rural and regional Australia. ( ABC News: Chris Taylor )
summary: The Rural Doctors Association says some regional patients may be forced to make "life and death" decisions due to rising fuel costs. ( ABC North West Queensland: Kelly Butterworth )
author: Caitlyn Gribbin

title: St John WA reveals rise in assaults on paramedics as officer details incidents that left her 'traumatised'
content:
St John Ambulance officer Rebecca Thompson was working when a man allegedly attacked an ambulance in Kalgoorlie last year. ( ABC News: Nic Perpitch )
St John WA has reported an 11.9 per cent rise in violence and aggression towards its staff in 2025.
Today it launched a safety campaign aimed at preventing such incidents, called 'We Shouldn't Have To Say It'.
As part of the campaign, patient transfer officer Rebecca Thompson has detailed incidents on the job that had left her "traumatised".
A regional ambulance officer has detailed her traumatic experiences on the front line as St John WA reported a rise in violence and aggression towards its staff.
The ambulance service provider has revealed 442 incidents were reported in 2025, up 11.9 per cent from the previous year.
Of those incidents, 35 resulted in injury to front line workers, according to its new safety campaign 'We Shouldn't Have To Say It'.
More than half of the reported incidents involved drugs or alcohol, and more than a third triggered urgent police attendance.
Kalgoorlie patient transport officer Rebecca Thompson was on duty last year when a 25-year-old man allegedly smashed an ambulance windscreen with his head while naked.
A St John Ambulance vehicle damaged in an incident in Kalgoorlie last year. ( ABC Goldfields: Macey Turner )
Ms Thompson said the incident was terrifying and she was forced to take time from work.
"It was the most traumatic moment in my life," she said.
A Kalgoorlie magistrate has heard the man charged after allegedly smashing the windscreen of an ambulance with his head is 'in a coma'.
Ms Thompson said the ordeal left her "looking over her shoulder" when out in the field.
"I still have moments where I will obviously get a bit jumpy, but I guess I'm just left with that constant anxious feeling of something like that happening again," she said.
summary: St John Ambulance officer Rebecca Thompson was working when a man allegedly attacked an ambulance in Kalgoorlie last year. ( ABC News: Nic Perpitch )
author: Mya Kordic

title: Perth parents frustrated by difficulties accessing specialist epilepsy care for their son
content:
Teddy Tangney playing on a climbing frame. He was diagnosed with epilepsy six months ago. ( Supplied: Sarah Tangney )
Three-year-old Perth boy Teddy Tangney has complex epilepsy and his family are frustrated at his medical care.
His mother Sarah Tangney wants her son to have ongoing specialist care through Perth Children's Hospital but he currently can only be treated through the emergency department.
There is currently a Senate committee inquiry underway into epilepsy and barriers to appropriate treatment.
A Perth mother whose three-year-old son has a complex form of epilepsy, believes the health system is failing her son because he's being pushed between two public hospitals.
Six months ago Teddy Tangney was diagnosed with drug resistant generalised myoclonic epilepsy syndrome, which triggers sleep activated seizures.
His mother Sarah Tangney said Teddy has had a seizure every night since September and they can last up to 20 minutes.
Sarah Tangney with her son Teddy and daughter Evelyn. ( Supplied: Sarah Tangney )
"He has myoclonic seizures, vocal seizures and tonic clonic seizures. The tonic clonic seizures consistently happen between 1am and 4am every single night," she said.
"His body goes rigid, the jerking is uncontrollable, he starts vomiting and his breathing changes. After five minutes we have to give a rescue medication and call an ambulance.
After initially taking Teddy to the emergency department at nearby Rockingham Hospital, in Perth's southern suburbs, she was then referred to the emergency department at Perth Children's Hospital (PCH).
Ms Tangney said her son was initially being pushed between the two hospitals.
She said Rockingham Hospital has told her Teddy's case is too complex and he needs to be treated at PCH but she can't get him under the care of its epilepsy specialist.
She claims PCH will only treat Teddy through the emergency department, rather than as an outpatient with continuous specialist care.
Teddy outside Perth Children's Hospital — the family are frustrated that they have to keep going to the emergency department instead of having ongoing specialist care. ( Supplied: Sarah Tangney )
summary: Teddy Tangney playing on a climbing frame. He was diagnosed with epilepsy six months ago. ( Supplied: Sarah Tangney )
author: Nadia Mitsopoulos

title: Nurses' union claims Tasmanian government policy has contributed to patient deaths
content:
The government says it "doesn't have any evidence" to substantiate the claims. ( ABC News: Georgia Hogge )
The Tasmanian branch of the Australian Nursing and Midwifery Federation says patients have died in the Launceston General Hospital's emergency department because of challenges created by a state government policy that gives paramedics an hour to transfer patients to the care of an emergency department.
The state government says it does not have any evidence to support the claim.
The government says the Health Department will "continue to look into these matters".
Tasmania's nurses' union has claimed patients in the Launceston General Hospital's emergency department have died because of the challenges created by the government's transfer of care policy.
The health department said it was not aware of any adverse patient outcomes or patient safety incidents "consistent with these claims".
The government's transfer of care policy, which was announced during the 2024 election campaign, sets a target for ambulances to transfer care of patients to hospitals within 60 minutes of arriving.
The government said the policy was working, but the Australian Nursing and Midwifery Federation (ANMF) argued the policy was putting patients at risk.
"When you're taken off that ambulance trolley that [might mean] you're put in a chair in a waiting room, it might mean you're put on a trolley in the waiting room … [or] in a corridor," Tasmanian branch secretary Emily Shepherd said.
"It might mean that you're put in an airlock or a thoroughfare with no oxygen, no suction and no continuous monitoring.
Nurses believe their fears have now been realised.
Emily Shepherd says members have "repeatedly raised" concerns about the policy. ( ABC News: Kelsey Reid )
Ms Shepherd said she had been told by members that people have died avoidable or "perhaps avoidable" deaths in the emergency department at the Launceston General Hospital (LGH).
"Our members have provided us with specific details in relation to these deaths, but they're also extremely conscious in relation to patient confidentiality and appropriate reporting processes," she said.
"As a result of that and the continuous delays of patients being unable to get into a bed or being cared for in an unsafe space, members believe this is contributing to patients' deaths or potentially deaths that could've been avoided."
summary: The government says it "doesn't have any evidence" to substantiate the claims. ( ABC News: Georgia Hogge )

title: Doctors say government ignoring scientific evidence in favour of 'ideology' in move to scrap drug diversion laws
content:
Drug diversion laws allow those caught with small quantities of certain illicit substances several chances before facing criminal charges. ( Pixabay )
The Australian Medical Association of Queensland has criticised the state government's proposal to scrap drug diversion laws, saying the decision was based on ideology rather than scientific evidence.
Earlier this month, Police Minister Dan Purdie said the government was cracking down on "a gateway to addiction and criminal offending" by removing the three strike policy.
A parliamentary committee will further examine submissions this week.
Queensland's peak medical body has condemned the state government's proposal to scrap drug diversion laws, labelling it "dangerous and contrary to evidence".
The three-strike program, launched by the former Labor government, allows those caught with small quantities of certain illicit substances several chances before facing criminal charges.
First-time offenders receive a warning, while second and third-time offenders are offered placements in a health practitioner-run diversionary program.
Queensland's new premier has confirmed the LNP will repeal drug laws that are diverting thousands of people away from the criminal justice system.
Under the LNP government's legislation, only first-time offenders would be referred to a drug diversion program, while further offending would incur fines or criminal charges.
In a parliamentary submission, the Australian Medical Association Queensland (AMAQ) said doctors "overwhelmingly support a health approach to drug use rather than criminalisation of patients".
"As such, AMA Queensland condemns the government's decision to repeal the laws supporting the police drug diversion program as dangerous and contrary to evidence.
"In fact, the government has jettisoned the program when preliminary data indicated it was successfully reducing police costs and supporting patients and before it had sufficient time to be properly evaluated.
The Queensland Nurses and Midwives Union (QNMU) was similarly critical in its submission, strongly opposing the legislation.
"The QNMU believes that the Queensland Government's decision to remove the current PDDP is imprudent and does not fully consider the available evidence and the implications that this will have on the individuals involved, their families, the criminal justice system, the health system, and the community.
"The Queensland government has an obligation to make an evidence-based decision, not one based on unsound information and prejudice."
summary: Drug diversion laws allow those caught with small quantities of certain illicit substances several chances before facing criminal charges. ( Pixabay )
author: Alex Brewster

title: Albury Wodonga Health CEO resigns from hospital board amid staff concerns over safety
content:
Bill Appleby has resigned from the his position on the board of Albury Wodonga Health. ( ABC News: Erin Somerville )
Albury Wodonga Health CEO Bill Appleby has resigned and will step down from his position on Wednesday.
It comes after multiple votes of no confidence by doctors and nurses in the hospital's executive leadership, over safety concerns.
A newly appointed interim CEO will meet with staff on Wednesday.
The chief executive officer of an embattled health service on the Victorian-New South Wales border has stepped down.
Albury Wodonga Health (AWH) board chair Jonathan Green announced this morning that Bill Appleby had resigned and Andrew Way would stand in as interim CEO from Wednesday.
In the announcement, Mr Appleby said it had been a privilege to lead AWH.
"I would also like to thank the AWH board and executive team for their steadfast leadership in implementing much-needed transformation across this critically important health service."
Mr Green thanked Mr Appleby for his service.
"Bill has led AWH through a period of sustained pressure across the health system, with a clear focus on strengthening the organisation and supporting its people," he said.
The announcement comes after more than 100 doctors passed a vote of no confidence in Albury Wodonga Health's executive leadership after a number of senior staff were sacked.
Staff say their colleagues have been sacked over criticisms of a controversial $558 million redevelopment of the hospital.
Albury Hospital staff say there have been safety concerns at the hospital. ( ABC News: Jason Katsaras )
The Albury Hospital Branch of the NSW Nurses and Midwives' Association echoed those concerns.
"Nurses and midwives working across AWH have raised serious and longstanding concerns regarding safety, governance and organisational transparency," union delegate and nurse Kim Cole said after a union meeting on March 20.
summary: Bill Appleby has resigned from the his position on the board of Albury Wodonga Health. ( ABC News: Erin Somerville )
author: Philippe Perez

title: Perth and Mandurah youth homelessness hub Passages sees more demand amid housing crisis
content:
Ed Easter says his work is often difficult and heartbreaking. ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
As doors shut after a busy day at this inner-city safe haven for homeless young people, staff brace themselves for what they must tell those who had sought their help.
"Good luck out there," is what they are having to say more often than not.
"We can help you with your essential needs right now, but at the end of the day, here's a sleeping bag [or] here's a tent," says youth worker Ed Easter.
There are guidelines and rules for young people at the Passages youth hub. ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
As more young people end up homeless in Western Australia, a coalition of youth and homelessness services is calling on the government to fund more centres like this one — the Passages hub in a quiet backstreet in Perth's inner city.
A downward spiral led Sully into homelessness — now he's part of a coalition dedicated to getting young people off the streets and into accommodation.
Until they secure that funding, services worry about those they are unable to reach.
Last year, about 750 children and young people aged between 12 and 25 years came to the Passages' Perth and Mandurah hubs — the majority of them for the first time.
About 250 of those young people were sleeping rough at the time they asked for help, and one in five disclosed they were dealing with an "adverse event" ranging from mental health issues to abuse.
Aimee Robinson says many homeless youth are assaulted on the streets. ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
"They can come in under the influence of alcohol and other drugs … in crisis because they're experiencing homelessness and hardship," said hub coordinator Aimee Robinson.
She said the "low barrier" service helped those who needed it most, and who might be turned away from other providers.
Kaya was 13 when she found herself homeless as her father struggled with substance abuse.
Kaya says the hub gave her a foundation from which to "move forward". ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
summary: Ed Easter says his work is often difficult and heartbreaking. ( ABC News: Keane Bourke )
author: Keane Bourke

title: How living with a disability in regional Australia poses extra challenges
content:
About half of Grace's NDIS funding is chewed up by extra costs due to her rural location. ( ABC News: Sharon Gordon )
As she fell, she hit her hand on a cupboard and tore skin off the back of her hand.
Someone with sight could probably manage that kind of injury alone.
Grace, whose name has been changed to protect her identity, is in her 60s and lives alone in regional Victoria, about a 30-minute drive from the closest major centre.
One of her carers was already on the way to Grace's home when she fell, so help was only about 10 minutes away.
Had Grace fallen on a different day of the week, she wouldn't have been so lucky, and would have potentially had to wait three hours for an ambulance, she said — something that had happened with a previous fall.
Grace only has carers coming to her home three days a week, despite her occupational therapist recommending she have care on hand daily.
Experts say NDIS participants living in regional areas face "far greater" barriers. ( ABC News: Margaret Burin )
This also means she can only shower safely three times a week.
The carers' schedules are determined by her National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) funding, which isn't enough to meet her needs, and about half of it is chewed up by extra costs due to her rural location.
She knows she's not the only one, and says she wants the government to fix the NDIS for all vulnerable people living remotely.
Thirty-four years ago, Grace was diagnosed with a type of retinitis pigmentosa, a group of inherited eye disorders where the cells in the retina stop working, leading to vision loss.
The breakdown of these cells can happen slowly or quickly.
Her older two children were teenagers at the time and took on the responsibility of helping their mother navigate this new life.
But when they were at school, Grace was left alone, blind, to care for the other small children.
summary: About half of Grace's NDIS funding is chewed up by extra costs due to her rural location. ( ABC News: Sharon Gordon )
author: Olivia Sanders

title: Private health insurers expand into GP clinics, telehealth, prompting calls for more oversight
content:
Health insurers are moving into a range of GP clinic and telehealth services. ( ABC News: Eric Hao Zheng )
Have you ever been to a MyHealth GP clinic, bought weight loss treatments at health.hub or had a telehealth appointment with Blua?
If so, you were being treated by an operation owned in part by some of the country's major private health insurers.
Since the pandemic, health insurers have increasingly pursued a "vertical integration" strategy, which involves investing in or partnering other businesses along the health service supply chain.
Once,insurers mainly had their own dental or optical service providers. Now insurers are increasingly expanding their reach into GP medical centres and telehealth providers.
The most recent foray by insurers into GP care has raised specific concerns that insurers may be creeping too close towards US-style managed care.
It has raised alarms among medical groups, which are calling for more guardrails.
In the most recent moves, Medibank Private's subsidiary, Amplar Health, now has a 90 per cent stake in MyHealth's 105 GP clinics.
In December, the competition watchdog, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, approved its purchase of another 61 clinics owned by Better Medical.
Bupa owns 33 medical centres in Australia and plans to grow to 65 by the end of next year, along with 60 mental health clinics, which would make it one of the biggest in the country.
"There's far too little oversight of the sector," Australian Medical Association vice-president Julian Rait said.
While there are laws in Australia restricting managed care, the concern is that insurers are increasingly testing what is legally allowed or exploiting loopholes.
Catholic Health Australia represents 80 Catholic hospitals nationally.
Its health policy director, Katharine Bassett, said the insurers' moves toward vertical integration had been happening slowly over time, but had reached a critical juncture.
Dr Bassett said insurers were finding ways to exploit loopholes in existing laws preventing managed care.
summary: Health insurers are moving into a range of GP clinic and telehealth services. ( ABC News: Eric Hao Zheng )
author: Alison Branley

title: Why Ghanim named his robotic arms after the Peaky Blinders brothers
content:
Ghanim Al Shnen lost both of his arms in a workplace accident in 2018. ( Australian Story: Tom Hancock )
"Relax, Tommy," says Ghanim Al Shnen, looking down at the black robotic arm attached just below his left elbow.
He concentrates and his hand twists 360 degrees at the wrist, before his thumb and forefinger move together to pick up a plastic bottle from the table.
His right arm is also robotic but attached closer to his shoulder and far harder to operate. He calls that arm Arthur.
Ghanim named his arms after brothers Tommy and Arthur Shelby, from the period crime drama Peaky Blinders.
"Tommy is a very smart and very quiet man," Ghanim explains, "so I pick the name Tommy for the left arm because it's quiet and helpful; easy to connect, easy to control. Arthur is crazy and make a problem. He's always making a mess in the kitchen and hits me in the face."
Ghanim has a remarkable ability to find humour and positivity in the bleakest situations, something that has served him well over the past 14 years.
A former Iraqi policeman, Ghanim fled that country in fear of his life in 2012 after refusing to change an interrogation report, falling foul of local militia. Leaving his wife and three boys behind, he made his way to Indonesia and came here in a fishing boat in August that year, seeking asylum, just as the government was tightening conditions concerning boat arrivals.
He was sent to Nauru and after a year there he was allowed to return to mainland Australia after signing a document that said he could never settle in the country. He was, however, given a bridging visa, allowing him to work while his asylum claim was being heard. Twice his claim to be recognised as a refugee was refused.
Ten days before his third and final appeal, a catastrophic accident changed everything.
Ghanim Al Shnen worked as a policeman in southern Iraq. ( Supplied )
On the morning of November 6, 2018, Ghanim was working alone on a building site and picked up a long steel bar used for reinforcing concrete. His next memory was waking up in hospital, his arms so badly burnt they would have to be amputated.
It is believed the steel bar touched overhead wires and a massive bolt of electricity ran up both his arms before exploding out of his back.
Ghanim Al Shnen worked as a labourer in western Sydney after he was released from Nauru. ( Supplied )
Many months after the accident, he underwent surgery to implant titanium rods into the bones in each of his arms, a process known as osseointegration, so that robotic arms could be attached.
summary: Ghanim Al Shnen lost both of his arms in a workplace accident in 2018. ( Australian Story: Tom Hancock )
author: Greg Hassall

title: The do and don'ts of fuel storage, as petrol and diesel prices surge amid Iran war
content:
Motorists are urged to make sure portable fuel containers are safe and legal. ( ABC News: John Gunn )
Despite repeated calls from authorities not to stockpile fuel, warning it will only push prices even higher as demand outpaces supply, some Australians are continuing to fill jerry cans and store petrol at home.
