In fact, stockpiling has surged to the point where major retailers like Bunnings are struggling to keep jerry cans in stock, with the item now the most searched on the hardware giant's website.
Some people are even selling their used fuel containers on online marketplaces, including Facebook.
However, there are some important things to consider if you are thinking about buying a second-hand fuel container.
Age is one of the first things to check when buying a second-hand fuel container.
WorkSafe Tasmania executive director Liz Radcliff said using plastic jerry cans older than five years was "not recommended as they might degrade or lose integrity over time".
A variety of fuel containers for sale on Facebook Marketplace. ( Facebook Marketplace )
Unlike retail businesses, which are required to ensure the items they sell comply with safety standards, second-hand sales are less regulated, increasing the risk of non-compliant fuel containers being advertised online.
Ms Radcliff said older or degraded containers "might rupture or leak, which increases the risk of fuel spills, which creates hazards during transport".
People have been spotted using intermediate bulk containers to store fuel, which is not recommended. ( ABC News: James Dunlevie )
You can check when a plastic fuel container was manufactured by looking for a date stamp moulded into the plastic, which usually shows the month and year of manufacture.
Even if the container is in date, Ms Radcliff warns against buying ones that have "damage, swelling, distortion, or other defects".
Another key consideration, whether buying fuel containers second-hand or new, is whether the container is actually approved for fuel storage.
The Australian Dangerous Goods Code requires portable plastic and metal fuel containers with a capacity of up to 25 litres to comply with Australian Standard 2906 and display a United Nations (UN) approval marking, showing they have passed safety testing.
summary: Motorists are urged to make sure portable fuel containers are safe and legal. ( ABC News: John Gunn )
author: Crystal Mckay

title: Football legend Kelvin Templeton draws on 'wild and dangerous' 70s VFL in debut novel
content:
Kelvin Templeton had a successful VFL playing career with Footscray and Melbourne in the 1970s and 1980s. ( Supplied: AFL Photos )
Playing VFL football for Footscray and Melbourne during the 1970s and 1980s, Kelvin Templeton had the type of career most players can only dream of.
He won the Brownlow Medal as the league's best and fairest in 1980 after taking Coleman medals for most goals in the 1978 and 1979 home-and-away seasons.
He kicked almost 500 goals for Footscray between 1974 and 1982 then played two seasons for Melbourne, where he finished his career.
After retiring, he served as the Sydney Swans chief executive for seven years and in 2024 was inducted into the Australian Football Hall of Fame.
Now 69, Templeton has turned to writing, releasing his first novel, Collision, which tells the story of the violent world of 1970s VFL football.
After his playing days, Kelvin Templeton was chief executive at the Sydney Swans then built a successful business career. ( Supplied )
Growing up in Tyers, in Victoria's south-east, in the 1960s, Templeton revelled in the immersive, transformative power of stories.
An avid reader all his life, he played senior football in the Latrobe Valley after moving to Traralgon as a teenager.
He moved to Melbourne aged 17 to play for Footscray, now the Western Bulldogs, while attending university.
"If we go back to 1974, there were fierce tribal loyalties between teams. At Footscray, quite a lot of players in the team were from the local area or the Latrobe Valley," he said.
"You played on often-muddy suburban grounds. It was very localised.
He said fights were common and, while there was spontaneous violence, some players were assigned to "fix up" an opponent.
Kelvin Templeton with his debut novel Collision. ( ABC News: Rachael Lucas )
"The era that I played in was quite a dangerous one and there were a number of players that got injured badly," Templeton said.
summary: Kelvin Templeton had a successful VFL playing career with Footscray and Melbourne in the 1970s and 1980s. ( Supplied: AFL Photos )
author: Rachael Lucas

title: High-powered e-bike use among children prompts call for more action to stop 'preventable injuries'
content:
Footage of groups of children riding e-bikes in Brisbane has been shared widely on social media. ( Supplied )
Falling off a bike and suffering a scrape is part of growing up, but the surge in popularity of high-powered e-bikes across Australia is leading to children sustaining potentially deadly "hidden" injuries instead, a doctor has warned.
As bans on children riding e-mobility devices are being considered by several states, a frontline surgeon says more must be done to stop "preventable injuries".
Paediatric surgeon Bhavesh Patel said the emergency department of the Queensland Children's Hospital was busiest on afternoons, when children leaving school get into accidents on their e-bikes and e-scooters.
Dr Bhavesh Patel says the most common time for children to present to the emergency department with injuries from e-bikes is after school. ( ABC News: Luke Bowden )
"The average age of all of the children presenting to hospitals with injuries is 10," he said.
Dr Patel said the intensity of e-bike crashes could lead to hidden internal injuries that weren't immediately apparent and took time and effort to diagnose.
"These aren't the minor injuries that we can just do a quick x-ray and give some pain relief and go home … they take time to diagnose.
A study using data from admissions at the QCH found e-scooter injury-related admissions at hospitals more than doubled between 2021 and 2024, with young boys accounting for three-quarters of admissions.
Zeke Hondow was killed after an alleged head-on crash e-bike collision last year. ( ABC News: Luke Bowden )
Eight-year-old Zeke Hondow was killed after an alleged head-on e-bike collision on Queensland's Sunshine Coast last year.
"He used to fall off his bike all the time, lots of cuts and bruises," said his mother, Kloe Weedon.
"So it still feels like it's not real, it's still a nightmare in my eyes."
Zeke Hondow's mother Kloe Weedon says he sustained internal injuries. ( ABC News: Luke Bowden )
When Ms Weedon got a call from her teenage daughter in October saying Zeke had come off his e-bike, she was worried — but she never assumed the worst.
summary: Footage of groups of children riding e-bikes in Brisbane has been shared widely on social media. ( Supplied )
author: Lucy Loram

title: The price of Australia's 'ethical' surrogacy laws
content:
Australians desperate to start a family are being driven overseas into markets that can be dangerous, costly and ethically dubious.
Is it time for a national conversation about paying surrogates here?
It's been a big journey for Lewis and Edward, in both a literal and metaphorical sense.
The couple should be bleary-eyed, but they're buzzing.
After a sleepless, long-haul flight — 10-week-old Flora in Lewis's arms and 16-week-old Quinto in Edward's — they're embraced by family who've been eagerly watching the Melbourne airport arrivals gates.
Their landing marks two years since the couple first travelled to Colombia with the hope of having children via surrogacy.
"There have been lots of hurdles," Lewis says. "One thousand and one processes and hoops to jump through, but we're just really happy to be back home."
When the pair previously explored local surrogacy options, they hit roadblocks almost immediately.
Australia allows an altruistic model, meaning surrogates cannot be financially compensated, but they can be reimbursed for out-of-pocket expenses, including medical costs, IVF, travel and loss of income.
"If you don't have someone in your immediate pool of friends or family that could be a surrogate, finding one is going to be next to impossible," Edward says.
And so, they headed to Colombia, which has become a popular destination for same-sex couples seeking surrogacy.
Lewis and Edward embarked on what's known as a "parallel journey", where two embryos are made from the same egg donor and implanted into two surrogates around the same time.
Lewis is the biological father of Flora and Edward is the biological father of Quinto. Born six weeks apart, they're as close as you might get to surrogacy "twins".
But welcoming their newborns into the world was only half the journey.
International surrogacy arrangements can be fraught with medical and legal complications — bringing a baby home isn't always guaranteed.
summary: Australians desperate to start a family are being driven overseas into markets that can be dangerous, costly and ethically dubious.
author: Siobhan Marin and Margaret Burin

title: Woman injured in tractor accident at CMC Rocks music festival in Willowbank
content:
Aerial footage of the scene at the CMC Rocks festival. ( ABC News )
A woman aged in her 40s has been taken to Ipswich Hospital in a stable condition after an incident involving a tractor at the CMC Rocks music festival at Willowbank.
The Queensland Ambulance Service said the accident happened at 1:53pm on Champions Way, Willowbank.
CMC Rocks, which calls itself the premier country music festival in Australia, is a four-day event that this year includes headline performances by Jordan Davis, Old Dominion and Riley Green.
Comment has been sought from the organisers.
The CMC Rocks event after the accident. ( ABC News )

title: Resurgence of ancient trade as young people seek to slow down in a fast-paced world
content:
Ash Credlin has ADHD and finds wood carving is one of the few things that can totally focus his attention. ( ABC Riverina: Andrew Mangelsdorf )
Considered to be one of the oldest human eating implements, wooden spoons have been a staple cooking tool for thousands of years.
Through their colourful history, they have also served many other uses — from warding off evil spirits, to mixing perfumes in ancient Egypt, administering punishment to children, and presented as a token of love to a sweetheart.
Today, however, their creation is considered a dying trade.
But the rare skill appears to be making a comeback, as young people increasingly turn to traditional trades and crafts in a growing digital world.
Wooden spoon carving is a craft that has been around for thousands of years. ( ABC Riverina: Andrew Mangelsdorf )
Ash Credlin, who lives in the small southern New South Wales town of Junee, took up the trade after a friend sparked his interest — and quickly got "hooked".
"Three weeks later, I bought a full carving kit for my birthday, and I really haven't looked back. That was about seven years ago," he said.
Mr Credlin has attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), which makes it difficult to focus on one thing at a time, but making spoons is an exception to this.
"You really do have to pay attention, and that's a nice thing to be able to do — just to put the phone down and do an activity, so I find that's beneficial," he said.
Ash Credlin has been carving for seven years and now runs his own business where he looks to pass on the skills to others. ( ABC Riverina: Andrew Mangelsdorf )
His passion for carving spoons is deeply rooted in their history.
"Spoons have been made for thousands of years," he said.
"They serve one purpose, or two. One's cooking and one's putting food in your mouth.
"It's really not a technical instrument to make, but for me it's about perfecting a shape or a style, and making a spoon pretty or appealing or something that's different."
summary: Ash Credlin has ADHD and finds wood carving is one of the few things that can totally focus his attention. ( ABC Riverina: Andrew Mangelsdorf )
author: Andrew Mangelsdorf

title: Why I watch Married at First Sight with my teenage son
content:
Virginia Trioli tunes into the most watched television show in Australia with her son. ( Supplied: Nine )
Which makes my household's recently adopted strategy of watching Married at First Sight as a kind of aversion-therapy for the education of our teenage boy either crazy-brave, or just crazy. Because he could walk away from weeks of this interpersonal melodrama with a programmed horror for toxic behaviour — or he might become a total master of it.
Never was there a stronger example of "do as I say, not as they do".
Watching the largely fake, but also completely true, juggernaut reality TV series Married at First Sight with our 14-year-old was never a deliberate choice: unlike my strict requirement that our son be properly versed in the ways of Andor, this one wasn't mandatory. Instead, it was opportunistic — and it's potentially very powerful.
MAFS contestant Paul has admitted to punching a door off-screen after learning about his on-screen wife's romantic history. ( Source: Nine/Married at First Sight )
I only had to once see my child's eyes widen in horror as he accidentally caught sight of me watching a bunch of lying, manipulating reality TV stars screaming at each other on TV to see the potential. Sitting together analysing the behaviour we see on MAFS has now become a sort of real-time social studies exercise for us that I reckon should be taught in schools.
And for those who might suggest that these outlandish people and their even more outlandish behaviour offer nothing of value to developing minds, don't kid yourself. There's a teen-size version of MAFS playing out at your kid's school right now, and they're either stuck in it or unwillingly observing it.
And like all good sex education, being properly informed is being properly protected.
It is compelling to watch human psychology play out under pressure in most of these so-called TV experiments. No matter the motivation of the contestants — fame, more followers on Instagram or being able to monetise their persona — almost nobody can avoid reverting to form and falling into the ways of their personality and psychology when they are put together in intense social connection. Thus, the bully reveals themselves as a bully, the traumatised child comes out in the high-functioning adult, and the carefully constructed persona falls apart when far away from its carefully constructed social circle.
A kind of TV truth will always come out.
So, it was fascinating to see the federal minister for social services, Tanya Plibersek, use her social media to scold the makers of this season of MAFS for casting Tyson — a bloke who had once been married to a Mormon and who wanted a "submissive" wife with no "masculine" energy, who would adopt a "traditional" role of keeping house and kids and leave him to be the provider.
You can imagine how this guy's energy went down in a group of ambitious, opinionated, independent women.
Plibersek thought it was irresponsible to platform these views on MAFS at a time when the "manosphere" was quietly brainwashing young men with misogynistic and dangerous views.
I completely disagree. Tyson's views are everywhere — they trip from the lips of young men, and women, with many not even realising from where they originate. The algorithm is persuading young kids of this toxic stuff on a minute-by-minute basis. And I don't imagine many of them are going to sit down and watch Louis Theroux's "Manosphere".
Louis Theroux faces down "red pilled" figureheads in "Inside the Manosphere".
summary: Virginia Trioli tunes into the most watched television show in Australia with her son. ( Supplied: Nine )
author: Virginia Trioli

title: Coroner finds Worimi woman's death at Lightning Ridge hospital was preventable
content:
The woman died at the remote hospital in Lightning Ridge in 2021. ( ABC News: Genevieve Blandin de Chalain )
The NSW deputy coroner has found a 42-year-old mother of three could have been saved if a transfer to Dubbo hospital had been ordered sooner.
The Worimi woman died at Lightning Ridge Multi-Purpose Health Centre while awaiting transfer to the larger health facility.
The Western NSW Local Health District says it will consider the coroner's recommendations.
WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised this article contains the name and images of a person who has died, included with permission of the family.
A coroner has found that a mother of three could have been saved if a transfer from a remote hospital had been ordered sooner.
Eve Brown, 42, died at the Lightning Ridge healthcare facility on July 2, 2021 while awaiting transfer to Dubbo.
An autopsy found the Worimi woman died of hypovolaemic shock due to a hidden spleen injury, resulting in sepsis.
Eve Brown died waiting to be airlifted from Lightning Ridge. ( Supplied: Angie Brown )
In delivering her findings, Deputy State Coroner Harriet Grahame said she was "sorry to have never met" Ms Brown, who was described as a kind and devoted mother.
"Had her transfer been expedited the previous day, it is more probable than not that she would have survived," Coroner Grahame said.
Speaking outside the court, Trina Brown said the loss of her daughter had been devastating for their family.
"She's gone and she shouldn't be, and I'm here rearing [her] three kids. She should be rearing her children, not me," she said.
Trina Brown says she will keep fighting for health services in remote communities. ( ABC News: Genevieve Blandin de Chalain )
The inquest heard Ms Brown was a well-known local artist, having contributed murals around Lightning Ridge.
summary: The woman died at the remote hospital in Lightning Ridge in 2021. ( ABC News: Genevieve Blandin de Chalain )
author: Genevieve Blandin De Chalain

title: Coroner finds paramedic failures in death of Phillip Mark Walton in custody
content:
Phillip Mark Walton died while in custody at Casino Police Station in March 2023. ( Supplied: Ian Walton )
Coroner Kasey Pearce has found paramedic care was "below a reasonable standard" in the death of Phillip Mark Walton at Casino Police Station in northern NSW in 2023.
She found police actions in restraining the 48-year-old man were appropriate in the circumstances, although they contributed to his death.
The coroner will supply transcripts of the inquest's evidence to the Paramedicine Council of NSW.
A coroner has referred the actions of two paramedics, who treated a man who died in custody, for review by health authorities.
Deputy State Coroner Kasey Pearce said the paramedic examination of Phillip Mark Walton in Casino Police Station lasted just 45 seconds and the patient was not touched to check vital signs.
"In my view, this did not comply with the policy of New South Wales Ambulance Service and fell below the reasonable standard of care," Coroner Pearce said.
Lismore ambulance station officer Tracey Wheeler entered the custody dock to assess Mr Walton, observed by fellow paramedic, Sharon Chapman outside the room.
Given the "reasonable grounds" for a complaint of "unprofessional conduct," the coroner will send a transcript of evidence to the Paramedicine Council of New South Wales.
The coroner found the actions of two NSW Ambulance paramedics "fell below a reasonable standard of care". ( ABC News: Timothy Ailwood )
The findings follow the inquest into the death of 48-year-old Phillip Mark Walton at Casino Police Station in March 2023, in hearings in Lismore Court in October 2025.
Police were called to the Casino Showgrounds on March 19, 2023, to reports Mr Walton was assaulting two circus staff members.
During an intense struggle to arrest him, police officers described using pepper spray and two police taser cycles, before being able to handcuff him.
The court heard two of the police officers attending the circus were not wearing body-worn cameras and a third did not activate a camera.
The inquest focused on events at Casino Police Station, where paramedics arrived to assess Mr Walton after the use of pepper spray and taser and his admission to taking cannabis, and having schizophrenia.
summary: Phillip Mark Walton died while in custody at Casino Police Station in March 2023. ( Supplied: Ian Walton )
author: Claire Simmonds

title: Epworth releases details into hospital review following endometriosis allegations
content:
Simon Gordon billed himself as the guru of laparoscopic surgery — keyhole surgery for endometriosis. ( Four Corners: Nick Wiggins )
A South Australian health commissioner will lead the review of clinical governance at Epworth Hospital after allegations a top gynaecologist performed unnecessary operations to remove endometriosis.
Complaints had been made about this gynaecologist for years. Only when hospital management discovered that Four Corners was looking into his practice, did it start investigating further.
Epworth HealthCare today revealed it has appointed a panel of experts for the review, which was launched after Four Corners revealed allegations Dr Simon Gordon allegedly performed operations when pathology reports showed little or no endometriosis.
Dr Gordon maintains he always acted ethically and responsibly and told the ABC he, "never performed surgery to treat endometriosis or any other condition unless I was absolutely convinced it was in the patient's best interest and to improve their overall quality of life".
In a statement, the health service said Professor Keith McNeil, the commissioner and chief executive of South Australia's Commission on Excellence and Innovation, would chair the review panel.
It described him "as a nationally respected health system leader with a deep expertise in clinical governance and system reform".
Commissioner for Excellence and Innovation in Health, Professor Keith McNeil conducted the review. ( Supplied: digitalhealth.gov.au )
Safer Care Victoria has nominated Professor Beverley Vollenhoven, "a leading gynaecologist and internationally recognised expert in women's health and endometriosis care" to sit on the review panel, the statement said.
Professor Alan Lam from New South Wales has also been appointed, described by Epworth as "an experienced senior clinician with extensive expertise in surgical practice, clinical governance and medical leadership".
The review will focus on gynaecological services at the hospital, including how doctors are credentialled, how concerns raised by patients and staff are addressed, and how clinical practice is overseen.
The Epworth media statement said the panel would also include two "consumer advocates with lived experience" in women's health including endometriosis, to bring "patient perspective directly into the review".
Epworth Hospital has commissioned an independent review following a Four Corners report airing allegations about Melbourne doctor Simon Gordon. ( ABC News )
The statement also said the review would seek advice from Women's Health Victoria to "support the inclusion of lived experience in the review".
Former patients have previously expressed dismay and anger that they would not be formally invited to participate in the review in either interviews or submissions.
summary: Simon Gordon billed himself as the guru of laparoscopic surgery — keyhole surgery for endometriosis. ( Four Corners: Nick Wiggins )
author: Antonia Oflaherty

title: When girls don't feel comfortable or confident in their uniforms it can cause them to drop out of sport
content:
Sweat marks, shapeless designs and period concerns are among girls' frustrations with sports uniforms. ( Adobe Stock: Louis-Paul )
Zoe, 18, quit PE as soon as she could. She says it was because of her sports uniform.
"I felt like I was way too exposed," she says.
"Some girls who did have wider hips would have also felt that way about the shorts, so a lot of us would restrict ourselves when we were actually moving."
As an adult, Zoe says it's still difficult to find activewear that makes her feel comfortable and confident.
"Going to the gym now, I would feel uncomfortable in gym outfits, so I would wear a lot of baggy stuff," she says.
Zoe, right, with Nejla and Sophie, has struggled to find sportswear she likes. ( ABC Adelaide: Lincoln Rothall )
Studies in Australia and overseas show that a big percentage of girls drop out of sport when they become teenagers.
In Australia, by the time girls hit the age of 15, only 23 per cent play organised sport , and teenage boys are three times more likely than teenage girls to get the recommended 60 minutes of exercise each day.
One of the contributing factors to this is the clothes they have to wear.
National research shows schoolgirls want to wear shorts and T-shirts when playing sport and not skirts, with uncomfortable clothing a factor in turning some away from sport.
Nejla, 18, who plays lacrosse, has noticed the decline in girls' participation as she has gotten older, and believes that how they feel in their sportswear plays a massive role.
"I feel like it's very difficult to find activewear that makes you feel good … things ride up a lot," she says.
"I've experienced a lot of girls stopping playing sport when they reach a certain age, where they feel like it's not worth it anymore.
"They can't put in the effort they want to put in."
summary: Sweat marks, shapeless designs and period concerns are among girls' frustrations with sports uniforms. ( Adobe Stock: Louis-Paul )
author: Michelle Wakim

title: Maggie Beer brings aged care nutrition project to outback renal health hub
content:
Maggie Beer gives some advice in the kitchen at Purple House in Alice Springs. ( ABC Alice Springs: Emma Haskin )
The Maggie Beer Foundation is working with Purple House in Alice Springs to help aged care staff provide healthy and nutritious food.
Maggie Beer says malnutrition is the biggest danger for elderly residents.
Purple House CEO Sarah Brown says she lured Ms Beer to Alice Springs with the promise of a feral cat sandwich.
Australian culinary icon Maggie Beer is in Alice Springs training workers at an Aboriginal community-controlled health organisation that provides dialysis and aged care for people with kidney failure.
Her work at Purple House is part of her foundation's effort to transform food and menus in aged care homes across Australia.
Ms Beer was lured to central Australia by Purple House CEO Sarah Brown's promise of a feral-cat sandwich.
"She got very excited about this and I said, 'Well if you come to Alice Springs and do some cooking classes with us, then Bobby West will teach you how to cook a pussycat and you can have a pussycat sandwich for lunch,'" Ms Brown said.
Maggie Beer and Sarah Brown meet at Purple House in Alice Springs. ( ABC Alice Springs: Emma Haskin )
Ms Brown said they were unfortunately unable to secure a feral cat due to recent rain in the region.
She said having a team from the Maggie Beer Foundation was crucial to providing nutritional food and keeping elderly people living on country in their homelands.
Smithy Zimran died before he could realise his dream of receiving dialysis on country, but his legacy lives on.
"We cook thousands of meals every year, both for dialysis patients in town and we run aged care in Mount Liebig and Yuendumu," she said.
Ms Brown said hosting the Maggie Beer Foundation on Close the Gap Day highlighted how Purple House was "thinking outside the square".
"It's all about agency and having a voice and having some hope and that's what Purple House is all about," she said.
summary: Maggie Beer gives some advice in the kitchen at Purple House in Alice Springs. ( ABC Alice Springs: Emma Haskin )
author: Emma Haskin

title: China cracks down on illegal fentanyl trade after Trump delays Beijing meeting
content:
US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping have long debated the fentanyl trade between China and the US. ( ABC )
China has arrested seven people and shut down more than 200 websites linked to its illegal fentanyl trade.
For years, the United States has urged China to implement tougher measures on those selling and producing the chemicals illegally.
The crackdown on fentanyl suppliers comes after US President Donald Trump announced a delay to his Beijing trip.
China has launched a crackdown on the trade of fentanyl chemicals, arresting seven people and shutting down more than 200 websites.
The country is one of the world’s highest producers of the chemicals used to produce the synthetic opioid in America.
And for years, the United States has urged China to implement tougher measures on those selling and producing the chemicals.
Information of the police operation has been released just days after US President Donald Trump announced he was delaying his visit to Beijing.
On top of the arrests, four companies have been penalised and 12 people subjected to “coercive measures”, which could mean they were summoned or detained.
That is according to Chinese state media, which also reported that the operation in the Hubei province investigated 22 cases in February.
Similar operations around China have been targeting the whole supply chain from production to storage and export of the chemicals.
China does not often publicly release information about such cases.
A trial of fentanyl drug traffickers was publicised 2019, in Xingtai, north China's Hebei Province. ( AP: Jin Liangkuai/Xinhua )
The United States has long called on China to do more to tackle the precursor trade.
Fentanyl is about 100 times more potent than morphine and has been responsible for the deaths of tens of thousands of Americans every year.
summary: US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping have long debated the fentanyl trade between China and the US. ( ABC )
author: Claire Campbell

title: Health insurance premiums to hike as much as 25 per cent, CHOICE analysis shows
content:
CHOICE found private health customers holding gold-level cover with the big five would feel the sting the most, with an average rise of 13.3 per cent. ( Adobe Stock: Gorodenkoff )
CHOICE analysed the big five health funds and found people with gold-tier policies would face substantial increases in premiums next month.
A group that represents private health funds says the increases are necessary as gold policies cover the most expensive and complex treatments.
While those with basic cover may not see any change to their premiums, CHOICE urges consumers to shop around for the best policy and pay up front if possible.
Millions of Australians could see their private health insurance premiums rise by as much as 25 per cent next month, according to analysis from the country's leading consumer advocacy group.
Health Minister Mark Butler recently approved a 4.41 per cent average increase in premium health insurance from next month, the highest rise in almost a decade.
However, new analysis by consumer group CHOICE of the big five health funds — Bupa, HCF, HBF, Medibank and NIB — found some policyholders would pay up to five times more than that.
"There's a deep structural problem with comprehensive healthcare overall and this has just been creeping and creeping along for decades, and now we are seeing astronomical figures."
Insurance policies are priced based on a system of tiers, including basic, bronze, silver and gold, with the highest tiers — gold and silver — offering the most comprehensive coverage but costing the most money.
Roughly 15 million Australians have some level of private health insurance, with health department figures showing more than 3.5 million hold gold-level hospital cover.
CHOICE found those with gold-level cover with the big five would feel the sting the most, with an average rise of 13.3 per cent.
But HCF's Hospital Optimal Gold cover trumped that with a 25 per cent premium increase on their policy, which does not include extras.
"We would hope that consumers would actually look at it and think, 'actually, this is quite a substantial increase, maybe I should shop around'," Mr Blades said.
CHOICE looked at policies currently for sale and did not include "closed" policies, which are held by existing customers but no longer for sale.
It found those with basic, bronze or silver policies would see much lower increases next month between 2.6 per cent and 3.3 per cent on average.
summary: CHOICE found private health customers holding gold-level cover with the big five would feel the sting the most, with an average rise of 13.3 per cent. ( Adobe Stock: Gorodenkoff )
author: Stephanie Dalzell

title: How boosting vaccinations could prevent meningitis outbreak in Australia
content:
Two people have died so far in the ongoing outbreak in the UK. ( Reuters: Chris J. Ratcliffe )
A growing outbreak of meningococcal B and meningitis has killed two young people in the United Kingdom.
Five new cases of meningitis have been found in Kent in south-east England in an outbreak the UK Health Security Agency has called "unprecedented".
The spread has been traced back to a Canterbury nightclub and infections of meningococcal B, which led to the development of meningitis.
The National Health Service in England has rolled out a meningococcal B vaccination program. ( Reuters: Chris J. Ratcliffe )
In Australia, Queensland woman Anna Gallo fell into a coma after contracting the disease overseas in February , just months after cricket star Damien Martyn was treated for meningitis.
Here's what you need to know about meningococcal B, meningitis and why an infectious disease expert warns Australia may be at risk of an outbreak.
Meningitis is the inflammation of tissues surrounding the brain and spinal cord, as in this latest outbreak, it can be caused by the meningococcal B bacterial Infection.
Meningococcal B is spread via prolonged, close contact with infected persons, usually through coughing, sneezing and kissing.
Professor Paul Griffin has warned a similar outbreak could "absolutely" happen in Australia. ( Supplied )
Director of Infectious Diseases at Mater Health Services in Brisbane, Paul Griffin, said it wasn't as infectious as the flu or common cold.
"It is harder to spread than those viral infections, but having said that, it can spread fairly readily given the right circumstances," Professor Griffin said.
He said the disease was more common in and presented a "slightly" higher risk to children.
Early symptoms of meningococcal B can be "non-specific" and confused with signs of viral infection.
"It can include respiratory symptoms that might mimic another respiratory virus like the flu," Professor Griffin said.
summary: Two people have died so far in the ongoing outbreak in the UK. ( Reuters: Chris J. Ratcliffe )

title: Family who conceived through IVF at Royal North Shore Hospital seeks DNA testing
content:
A family says news of an IVF mix-up at Sydney's Royal North Shore Hospital has prompted it to seek DNA testing. ( ABC: Kala Lampard )
A family is pursuing DNA testing after hearing about an IVF mix-up at the same Sydney fertility clinic used to conceive its baby.
One of the parents has spoken to the ABC, saying they felt unnerved by this week's story about Sasha Szafranski, who discovered she and her twin sister were not biologically related to their parents.
The NSW Health department will offer support to the Szafranski family and others affected by the discovery.
A parent who conceived a child through Royal North Shore Hospital's fertility clinic in the 1990s, just months before an embryo mix-up at the same clinic, says their family is now planning to undertake DNA tests.
This week the ABC revealed a historic IVF error occurred at the Sydney clinic in April 1995, resulting in twin girls being born to a mother they are not related to.
When Sasha Szafranski matched with a stranger on a genealogy website, she uncovered an IVF mix-up that had been hidden for 30 years.
The mistake was only discovered last year, when one of the twins, Sasha Szafranski, used a genealogy tracing website.
Now, another parent who conceived not long before that error has told the ABC that news of that serious mistake has been "unnerving" for them.
"We are all going to get a DNA test to make sure it was all good," they said of their family.
"We might go on ancestry.com and see what happens."
The former patient said they expected other families who conceived at the clinic during a similar time period to the embryo mix-up would likely do the same "just to be reassured".
While the parent is confident their child is biologically their own, they said news of the serious mistake had "rocked" them.
"It really is an inexcusable mistake," the parent said of the embryo mix-up.
The parent said they felt for Sasha Szafranski's mother, Penny Szafranski, who learnt last year that her twin daughters were not her biological children.
summary: A family says news of an IVF mix-up at Sydney's Royal North Shore Hospital has prompted it to seek DNA testing. ( ABC: Kala Lampard )
author: Elise Kinsella

title: Ship crew involved in French Polynesia cocaine bust sent to immigration detention
content:
Australian Border Force boarded the MV Raider before transferring crew to immigration detention. ( ABC News: Seni Iasona )
Australian Border Force officers have boarded a vessel that was intercepted by French Polynesian authorities and found carrying nearly 5 tonnes of cocaine.
Crew members of the MV Raider, which is now moored in Sydney Harbour, have been transferred to an immigration detention centre.
After intercepting the MV Raider earlier this year, French Polynesian authorities allowed it to continue sailing to Australia after dumping the drugs at sea.
The crew of a vessel that was intercepted in the Pacific Ocean carrying nearly 5 tonnes of cocaine has been escorted off the ship by Australian authorities.
An Australian Border Force (ABF) spokesperson said that its officers boarded the MV Raider this morning and detained all 11 crew members, who have now been transferred to an immigration detention centre.
The ABC understands that MV Raider has been monitored by the ABF since January 2026.
A reason for the crew's detention was not provided.
Australian Border Force officers approached the vessel moored in Sydney Harbour on Thursday morning. ( ABC News: Seni Iasona )
The ship was intercepted in international waters by French Polynesia authorities earlier this year and was escorted into Sydney Harbour after issuing a distress call on Friday.
Moored at Snails Bay in Sydney Harbour, the MV Raider's 11 Ecuadorian and Honduran crew members have remained on board since arriving on Friday.
The International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said the welfare of the crew was its main priority.
"We expect that the detention would be for the purpose of repatriation and the crew will be afforded fair treatment and due process while ABF arrange for them to be returned home to their families," said Ian Bray, ITF's Australian coordinator.
Mr Bray said the ITF was focused on identifying where the crew members would be detained and obtaining visitation rights for ITF inspectors.
"The ITF will be monitoring the situation and will assess whether the crew will be further assisted by legal representation when it is known if there are any other matters that are being considered or pursued by ABF whilst the crew is being detained," he said.
summary: Australian Border Force boarded the MV Raider before transferring crew to immigration detention. ( ABC News: Seni Iasona )
author: Lucy Cooper

title: Cable Beach venue in Western Australia's far north charged with liquor breaches
content:
WA Police issued infringement notices against two workers at Cable Beach General Store and Cafe. ( ABC Kimberley: Andrew Seabourne )
West Australian Police say they have charged a Broome venue by summons for multiple alleged liquor breaches and fined two of its workers.
The Cable Beach General Store and Cafe allegedly sold takeaway alcohol to a banned drinker, while also not having a takeaway liquor licence.
WA Police says the company that operated the venue is due to appear in court in May.
A general store and cafe in a West Australian town with strict alcohol restrictions has been charged with multiple alleged liquor breaches.
In January, the Cable Beach General Store and Cafe, a licensed restaurant in the Kimberley town of Broome, is alleged to have sold takeaway wine despite not having a takeaway liquor licence.
WA Police further allege alcohol was sold to a community member who was on the Banned Drinkers Register (BDR), a list of people prohibited from purchasing or possessing packaged liquor in a bid to reduce alcohol-related harm and ill-health.
The Cable Beach General Store and Cafe has been charged with liquor breaches. ( ABC Kimberley: Andrew Seabourne )
New laws were introduced in Broome and Derby in 2024, with the sale of alcohol banned on some days and restricted on others to curb high rates of alcohol-fuelled violence.
Bottle shops in Broome can only trade between midday and 8pm with daily purchase limits in place for customers, including a choice of either two bottles of wine, a carton of beer under 6 per cent alcohol, or a single bottle of spirits.
Situated near Broome's iconic Cable Beach, the Cable Beach General Store and Cafe offers mini golf, groceries and meals.
The WA Police West Kimberley district liquor engagement and support manager, Senior Sergeant David Rudd, said liquor enforcement officers were alerted to an incident while patrolling a nearby venue.
"[They observed] one of the banned drinkers known to them to be hiding alcohol under bushes outside the Cable Beach General Store and Cafe," he said.
WA Police allege the venue sold takeaway liquor without the appropriate licence. ( ABC Kimberley: Andrew Seabourne )
"When they went in and confronted the staff member about it, they basically admitted they sold it to him.
summary: WA Police issued infringement notices against two workers at Cable Beach General Store and Cafe. ( ABC Kimberley: Andrew Seabourne )
author: Dunja Karagic
